content.text: Strategic Overview: The Pillar Map
P1: Product & Distribution (Design)
Focus on New Product features, Target Market, and Benefit Design. [Linked Themes: Product Design, Group Products, CI, IP, LTC, PMI, State Healthcare].
P2: Pricing & Underwriting (Sell)
Focus on Assumptions (Morbidity/Mortality), Rating Factors, and Selective Underwriting. [Linked Themes: Pricing, Underwriting].
P3: Claims Management (Pay)
Focus on Fraud control, Triage, TPA management, and Cost containment. [Linked Themes: Claims Management].
P4: Risk Mgmt & Reinsurance (Protect)
Focus on Risk Taxonomy, Reinsurance structures, and SCR/Capital. [Linked Themes: Risks, Reinsurance, Risk Management & Capital].
P5: Valuation & Data (Measure)
Focus on BEL/Risk Margin, IBNR methods, Data quality, and Investments. [Linked Themes: Reserving & Valuation, Data, Models, Investments].
P6: Regulation & Ethics (Comply)
Focus on IRDAI 2024 updates, PPH, and the Appointed Actuary role. [Linked Themes: Regulation, IRDAI Regulations, Professionalism].
Pricing Framework (SP1 Chapters 17 & 19)
Overview & Approach
Pricing is one of the most important uses of actuarial models in SP1. [src:IAI_0416]
Two main pricing methods: (1) Formula/Equation of Value approach, (2) Cashflow projection approach. Cashflow approach generally used in practice for long-term contracts. [src:IAI_1123]
Focus of pricing: modelling benefit cashflows to derive a risk premium, then building up to the office premium. [src:IAI_0916]
Risk Premium — Individual Products
**IP Insurance — Inception/Annuity Approach**

Two functions: (1) Claim inception rate (probability that claim becomes payable in a given year, after deferred period), (2) Disabled life annuity (PV at claim inception of expected payments until policy expiry, allowing for escalation, interest, death and recovery).
Note: Sickness inception rates ≠ Claim inception rates. Claim inception = sickness inception × probability of remaining sick through deferred period.
Expected PV of claims in a year = (survival probability) × (claim inception rate) × (annuity value) × (discount factor) × (annual benefit amount).
**IP Insurance — Multi-State Modelling**
More complex methodology; separately tracks policyholders through: healthy, sick within DP, claiming, recovering, dead.
Each subclass has its own transition probabilities: sickness inception, lapse, mortality, recovery, expiry.
Rates may vary by duration within state and number of previous claims.
Data limitations often require combining sub-cohorts or reducing transition intensities.
**CI Insurance**
Much simpler than IP — benefit is a lump sum.
Overall CI incidence rate = sum of incidence rates by cause (heart disease, stroke, cancer, other). Care needed to avoid double-counting overlapping conditions.
Claim incidence rate = ix + (1 − kx) × qx, where kx = proportion of deaths due to CI. Double-counting kx overstates it → understates claim cost → inadequate premiums/reserves.
Claim incidence rate = ix × probability of surviving the survival period.
**PMI — Incidence × Average Cost**
Risk premium (age, gender) = Σk [ik × ACk], where ik = incidence rate for benefit/procedure class k, ACk = average claim cost for class k.
Data split by age bands, gender, location, hospital band, smoker status, occupation (territory and legislation dependent).
Adjustments for: excesses (reduce incidence and average cost), NCD rating structures, co-insurance.
If gender-neutral pricing required: weight gender-specific rates by expected male:female mix. Risk: if one gender is cheaper, anti-selection worsens the pool.
Multi-state modelling used if premiums guaranteed for longer than one year.
**LTCI**
Very similar approach to IP — inception/annuity or multi-state modelling.
Multi-state model tracks transitions between dependency levels (healthy → care level 1 → level 2 → level 3 → dead).
Transition probabilities needed between all states; rates may vary by duration in current state and cause of incapacity.
**Health Cash Plans**
Expected claims per benefit = inception rate × average benefit paid (adjusted for excesses/coinsurance).
Flat rate often charged across broad age ranges despite age-varying incidence.
Adjust for 'inertia' — people's propensity not to claim when entitled (small benefit amounts + customer apathy). No adjustment needed if inertia already embedded in experience data.
**Accident & Sickness (ASU)**
Follows inception/annuity approach similar to IP.
Average rate often agreed per lender/scheme (same premium per £ of loan regardless of age/gender). Large schemes use group techniques.
Modelling Complexities by Product

Estimate transition intensities (separate second/subsequent inceptions); recovery rates by duration and cause; sales volumes (affected by economic morale, government welfare, tax). Data limitation: published IP tables have limited credibility; industry data is heterogeneous.

Up to 40 separate condition distributions to estimate. Medical advances affect new vs existing business differently — conditions can be removed from new policies but not guaranteed in-force ones. Separate disease-based and treatment-based trend modelling advisable. Data limitation: CI not around long enough for credible experience except for cancers and heart attacks.
Limited experience data on transition rates. Claim amounts dependent on economy, inflation, care cost structures.

⬡ - OP pricing uses a burning cost approach: Burning cost = claim frequency × average claim severity. Risk factors include age, gender, network tier, benefit limits, co-pay level, and presence of dental/optical/maternity cover (which must be priced separately). [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - OP pricing must load for high claims administration expenses (high frequency, low value), initial product development and system costs (amortised over projected volume), underwriting loadings for sub-standard lives, and a profitability margin calibrated to capital requirements. [src:IAI_0722]
From Risk Premium to Office Premium (Chapter 19)
Risk premium + expense loadings (initial/renewal/fixed overhead contribution) + profit loading + cost of capital = office premium.
Sensitivity testing must be performed throughout — test different future scenarios to ensure no serious financial difficulty.
Profit Criteria
Sequence of profits from inception to termination — definitive guide to profitability but unwieldy.

Discount profit signature at risk discount rate. Best criterion in theory (maximises company value). Expressed as % of initial sales costs or % of discounted premium income. Limitation: subject to law of diminishing returns; says nothing about competition.
Rate at which discounted cashflow = zero. May not be unique (multiple sign changes); cannot be related to sales costs or premium income. Easier to understand but less reliable than NPV.
Duration at which profits emerged have PV = zero. Useful for capital-constrained companies. Ignores all cashflows after payback — not reliable on its own.
NPV as primary criterion + DPP as secondary reference. DPP more useful for product design than pricing.
Marketability
Premiums must be tested for marketability. May lead to reconsideration of: product design, distribution channel, profit requirement, or whether to proceed at all.
Expenses (especially acquisition costs and marginal admin costs) must be re-examined.
Competitiveness
Monitor competitors' pricing closely. Significant discrepancies should be investigated. [src:IFoA_0425,IFoA_0922]
If competitor uses more detailed risk differentiation: better lives go to competitor → insurer's average pricing assumptions are called into question (anti-selection).
Two factors: (1) overall premium level vs competitors, (2) number and accuracy of rating factors used.
Volume matters: fixed expenses spread over volume. Low volume → high per-policy expense loading → uncompetitive premiums.

Products priced below full premium to gain market share, compensated by higher margins on other lines. Risk: adverse business mix (more loss-leader, less high-margin). Over time, bring each product back in line to its fair profit contribution.
Each profit centre must meet its target return on capital. Excess return → pricing can be relaxed to promote volume. Insufficient return → grounds for withdrawal.
Reserving & Solvency Capital in Pricing
Writing a policy creates a capital requirement (supervisory reserves + solvency capital). Each policy must be charged for the cost of holding this capital.
Cost of capital = difference between internal rate of return and yield on locked-in statutory assets (e.g. 10% IRR − 4% asset yield = 6% per annum charge on SCR).
Reserving assumptions in pricing model should match those expected to be used in practice (prudent or best estimate depending on regulatory regime).
Reinsurance can reduce solvency capital requirements — consider pricing net of reinsurance.
Reinsurance Impact on Pricing
Reinsurance roles: technical assistance/data for new products; risk sharing; smoothing profitability; financing new business strain; tax and solvency capital arbitrage; enabling larger risks.
Cost: reinsurance premiums generally exceed expected reinsurance benefits (reinsurer covers own expenses and profit) → net cost to insurer, must be included in pricing model.
Benefit: where arbitrage possible (tax, capital), pricing net of reinsurance can improve competitiveness.
Quota share: reinsurer may require right to vary commission if insurer changes premium rates.
Regulatory Impact on Pricing
Basis may be prescribed (e.g. community rating) or certain rating factors restricted (e.g. gender, genetic test results).

Regulatory restrictions on operations (e.g. investment constraints, premium tax collection) impose costs that must be reflected in pricing assumptions. Affects all insurers equally — does not alter competitive position.
Premium Reviews
Keep all assumptions under review. Test impact of any change against existing tariff before deciding whether to reprice. Changing prices is expensive and affects marketability.

Smaller margins included (because adverse experience can be corrected at next review). Monitor closely. Risk of selective lapsing on rate increase — healthier lives leave, worsening remaining portfolio. Rate increase must account for this risk. [src:IFoA_0922]
Where premiums inadequate, reserves must be established. Reserve size depends on speed at which premium can be reviewed and renewal likelihood as rates rise.

⬡ - Under consumer responsibility / TCF frameworks, pricing review should ensure: profitability margins are not excessive, unnecessary risk loadings are removed once experience matures, commission payments are proportionate to distributor effort, and premium/maximum benefit ratios remain consistent with fair value. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - In a high-inflation environment, short-term and group products (PMI, health cash plans, group IP) have lower inflation risk than long-term guaranteed-premium products (CI, individual IP) because premiums can be reset annually — sales strategy should reflect this differential exposure. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - The full office premium build-up includes: risk premium + expense loading + profit margin + cost of capital + cost of reserves + reinsurance cost/recoveries + margins + investment return credit + persistency adjustment + tax. In a high-inflation environment, multiple components shift simultaneously — a full review before repricing avoids uncoordinated partial adjustments. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Expense loading by sum insured (rather than flat per-policy or by premium) better allocates costs to higher socio-economic segments with larger sum insureds, and enables the insurer to target a higher final office premium where price sensitivity is lower. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - To test price sensitivity of new business volumes, insurers can use: consumer surveys, focus groups, distributor interviews, phased test pricing by region or channel, comparison website data, internal sales/retention team data, reinsurer or consultant research, and own historic data on volume responses to past rate changes. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - The full office premium build-up includes: risk premium + expense loading + profit margin + cost of capital + cost of reserves + reinsurance cost/recoveries + margins + investment return credit + persistency adjustment + tax. In a high-inflation environment, multiple components shift simultaneously — a full review before repricing avoids uncoordinated partial adjustments. [src:IAI_0924]

⬡ - Expense loading by sum insured (rather than flat per-policy or by premium) better allocates costs to higher socio-economic segments with larger sum insureds, and enables the insurer to target a higher final office premium where price sensitivity is lower. [src:IAI_0924]

If profitability is lower than assumed, identify adverse trends and take action: update future assumptions, re-price with higher margins, re-design benefits (e.g. stricter protocols), change investment/sales/reinsurance strategy, or re-organize workforce/IT for efficiency. [src:IAI_1124]
Claims Management
Claims Management Process
Clear definitions, documentation required, verification of pre-existing conditions. [src:IAI_0722,IAI_0918]
Non-disclosure checks, tele-interviews, use of specialized medical officers. [src:IAI_0917]
Home visits (IP), second opinions, independent medical exams. [src:IAI_0722]
Early intervention, proportionate benefits for partial return, funding treatment to reduce claim duration.
Annuity vs lump sum, tax implications, currency, avoiding over-insurance. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Claim payment speed is a key customer outcome metric; rising settlement times (from days to weeks or months) damage policyholder trust, increase complaints and ombudsman referrals, and may indicate understaffing, process complexity, or governance deterioration. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Insurers should minimise disputed CI claims through: clear and precise condition definitions, proactive claimant communication, transparent documentation requirements, and regular review of definitions against medical advances and claim dispute patterns. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Declined claims can cause reputational and regulatory risk: policyholders and brokers may complain to the ombudsman, policies may be mis-sold, and regulators may scrutinise the insurer. Monitoring declined-claim rates and reasons is essential. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - Claims experience analysis should be split by: age, policy duration, channel, product variant, and reason for decline. High declined-claim rates early in policy duration may indicate anti-selection or poor underwriting; late-duration spikes may indicate non-disclosure at inception. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - When claim declines increase, the sales process and application forms should be reviewed to ensure clear disclosure requirements. Penalties for misrepresentation should be communicated at point of sale and reiterated in renewal communications. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - Capitation (fixed annual payment per covered member to a provider network) reduces OP fraud, waste and abuse by aligning provider incentives — providers profit from efficiency rather than volume. Challenges include: difficulty agreeing rates for new business, quality-of-care risk, member inconvenience across networks, and potential regulatory barriers. [src:IAI_0722]
Declined Claims — Analysis & Management

⬡ - Reasons for an increase in declined claims include: absolute growth in claim numbers, maturing book (underwriting effects wearing off), improved claims control processes, policyholder misunderstanding of coverage, fraud, and non-disclosure/misrepresentation at inception. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - A key distinction is whether the proportion of declined claims (not just the absolute number) has risen — the proportion is a more meaningful indicator of claims control effectiveness or underwriting deterioration. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - Declined claims generate regulatory, legal, and reputational risk: complainants may escalate to the financial ombudsman, and there is a risk of forced settlement on claims correctly declined. Brokers may withdraw if they face persistent customer complaints. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - Response actions: review sales process and channel performance; update application forms and policy wording; strengthen underwriting evidence requirements; educate customers on covered conditions; monitor and publish declined-claim rates to demonstrate transparency. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - To improve a deteriorating IP loss ratio, insurers can: increase or granularise premium rates; amend product terms (reduce replacement ratio, increase deferred period, introduce partial/rehabilitation benefits); tighten underwriting (limit high-risk occupations, reduce free cover limits, increase health evidence); tighten claims assessment; introduce loyalty benefits to reduce selective lapses; improve fraud detection; review and tighten policy wording; and seek reinsurer advice. [src:IFoA_0924]
Clear definitions [src:IAI_1124], documentation required, verification of pre-existing conditions. [src:IAI_0722,IAI_0918,IAI_1124]

⬡ - Digital tools like EPR/EHR streamline the claims process by enabling real-time pre-authorization and providing immediate data on co-payments/benefit limits, reducing administrative burden for both provider and insurer. [src:IAI_1124]
Regulatory / Compliance Framework
IRDAI 2024 Updates & Prudential Norms
60-month moratorium (not 48); PED waiting period max 36 months; 1-hr/3-hr cashless TATs; 30-day claim settlement mandate; Bima Sugam digital marketplace launch.
Available Solvency Margin (ASM) >= 150% of Required Solvency Margin (RSM); statutory reserves certified by AA; investment restrictions by asset class.
State Interaction & Conduct of Business
Integration with PMJAY (Ayushman Bharat); public provision defining the floor for private demand; co-payment and top-up structures.
Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) principles; mandatory 15-day grievance resolution via GRM.
Capital, Taxation & Grievance
Solvency II Pillar 1/2/3 analogues; transition to Risk-Based Capital (RBC) and IFRS 17/Ind AS 117 on the horizon.
Section 80D benefits (up to Rs 1L for seniors); 18% GST impact on premiums and affordability.
Ombudsman hierarchy (internal -> Ombudsman -> Consumer Forum -> Civil Court).
Professional Roles
Statutory role of Appointed Actuary (AA); whistleblowing; compliance with IAI APS 33/34.
Regulatory Intervention Powers

⬡ - Regulators can require an insurer to purchase additional reinsurance to limit exposure to adverse experience — protecting policyholders from insolvency risk; however, reinsurance availability on acceptable terms cannot be guaranteed, and the cost may strain the insurer's finances. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Regulators can require the insurer to hold additional capital above minimum solvency requirements to provide a buffer against adverse experience; raising capital may be difficult or expensive depending on market conditions and investor appetite. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Regulators can require a merger or acquisition — for example, requiring a poorly diversified insurer to combine with a better-diversified counterpart to improve portfolio risk spread and protect policyholders through a stronger combined entity. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Regulators can revoke the fitness and propriety approval of senior management where governance or risk management failings are identified — replacing leadership may alter strategic direction, but creates short-term disruption that could itself harm policyholder service. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Other regulatory intervention tools: (a) pricing restrictions to prevent excessive premiums; (b) restrictions on rating factors (e.g. banning genetic test results in underwriting); (c) restrictions on the sales process (mandatory disclosure requirements); (d) restrictions on investment strategy (limiting high-risk assets); (e) requirements to treat customers fairly, particularly in premium reviews on long-term products. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Pros/cons of government-provided chronic illness cover: (Insurer) Reduced financial risk but loss of market share; (Government) Improved access and cost containment but budgetary and admin burdens; (Customers) Financial relief and expanded options but limited choice and potential coverage gaps. [src:IAI_1124]

⬡ - A comprehensive report to the Board on new health products should cover: executive summary, overview of diseases/trends, analysis of existing policies/competitors, detailed data analysis (internal/external), regulatory compliance, financial implications (reserves/capital), customer experience, and risk mitigation strategies. [src:IAI_1124]
Official IRDAI Regulations (2024)

Replaces Health Insurance Regulations 2016; in force 1 April 2024. Core consumer protections: PED waiting period capped at 36 months; moratorium of 60 continuous months after which no policy or claim is contestable except on fraud grounds; renewal cannot be denied on grounds of prior claims; NCB offered as cumulative bonus or renewal premium discount at policyholder's choice; grace period 15 days (monthly) / 30 days (other modes); enhanced SI carries a fresh 60-month moratorium clock.

Governs product design, approval, and launch across all insurers. Life insurers: benefit basis only; General / health insurers: both indemnity and benefit permitted. Insurers must offer products for all ages and medical conditions including PEDs, chronic illness, disabilities, HIV/AIDS, and mental illness. AYUSH mandatory at par with allopathy. Two launch routes: File and Use (prior IRDAI filing + UIN before launch) or Use and File (UIN then launch, but PMC approval still required). Premium fixed during policy term; renewal loadings applied at portfolio level, not individual policy level.

Governs the full proposal-to-claim lifecycle. Proposal decision within 7 days; policy document issued within 15 days of acceptance. Free look: 30 days from receipt (policies ≥1 year); refund within 7 days else interest at bank rate + 2%. CIS mandatory with every policy in local language on request. Cashless: authorisation within 1 hour; final discharge within 3 hours — delay charged to shareholders' funds. Claims not settled within timelines attract bank rate + 2% paid suo-moto. No repudiation without PMC or 3-member CRC approval. Grievance hierarchy: Internal GRM → Insurance Ombudsman → Consumer Forum → Civil Court.

In force 1 April 2024; reviewed every 3 years. EOM limits: General insurer ≤30% of GWP; Standalone health insurer ≤35% of GWP; commission subsumed within overall EOM limit. Board-approved EOM Policy, Commission Policy, and Business Plan mandatory. Additional allowances: up to 5% of EOM for Insurtech / insurance awareness; up to 15% of incremental premium for rural / PMJAY / PMSBY / PMFBY schemes. Return signed by CEO, CFO, Chief Compliance Officer, and Appointed Actuary; certified by Statutory Auditor; filed with annual financials. Penalty for EOM overrun ≥10% of projection: no variable pay for MD/CEO/WTD/KMPs.

content.bold: Actuarial, Finance & Investment Functions of Insurers Regulations 2024 (AFI)
content.text: Mega-consolidation: 9 prior regulations → 1. F. No. IRDAI/Reg/10/204/2024; effective 1 April 2024; reviewed every 3 years. Scope: all insurers and reinsurers. Structure: 3 Chapters + 5 Schedules. Schedule I (Actuarial): AA eligibility and duties; GPV-based life reserving with MAD; GI/health reserving (UPR + PDR + IBNR + IBNER); solvency framework (ASM ≥ 150% RSM). Schedule II (Finance): financial statement formats for life and GI/health. Schedule III (Investment): minimum investment pattern norms. Supplemented by 270-page Master Circular dated 17 May 2024 superseding all prior actuarial, finance, and investment circulars.

Governs outward reinsurance arrangements. Retention hierarchy: Indian reinsurers (GIC Re and others) → Foreign Reinsurer Branches (FRBs) → Cross-Border Reinsurers (CBRs); CBR residency and regulatory compliance rules apply. Simplified Quota Share and Excess of Loss structures defined. PMC must review reinsurance risks before product launch. Appointed Actuary is responsible for certifying adequacy of reinsurance arrangements. Arrangements must be consistent with product profitability and capital assessments approved by the PMC.

Entry capital: ₹100 Cr minimum under s.6 of Insurance Act 1938. Governs new insurer registration, amalgamations, and transfer of shares; net worth requirements. Board-level governance of all product, expense, and capital decisions via the Product Management Committee (PMC). Insurer must maintain ASM ≥ 150% RSM at all times. IRDAI approval required before an AA appointment takes effect; IRDAI must decide within 30 days of receipt of the application.
Products Regulation 2024 & Master Circular
Identity & Legal Basis

F.No. IRDAI/Reg/8/202/2024; notified 20 March 2024; in force 1 April 2024; applicable to all life, general, and health insurers; reviewed every 3 years. Repeals: IRDAI (Health Insurance) 2016, (ULIP) 2019, (Non-Linked) 2019, (Micro Insurance) 2015, (Minimum Limits for Annuities) 2015, (Acquisition of Surrender & Paid-up Values) 2015.

Ref: IRDAI/HLT/CIR/PRO/84/5/2024; dated 29 May 2024; issued under s.14(2)(e) IRDA Act 1999 and s.34 Insurance Act 1938 read with Regulation 7 of Schedule III of the Products Regulation. Reviewed annually. Applicable to all General, Health and Life Insurers. Supersedes all prior health insurance guidelines and circulars.
3 Objectives of Products Regulation
Facilitate insurers to respond faster to emerging market needs; design innovative products; promote ease of doing business; improve insurance penetration.
Protect policyholders' interests by enabling insurers to adopt good governance in product design and pricing.
Ensure sound and responsive management for effective oversight and adequate due diligence on insurance products including innovative ones.
Scope of Health Insurance Business (Schedule III)

May offer individual and group health products on indemnity and/or benefit basis. Overseas/domestic travel: general and health insurers only. Credit-linked products: up to loan period not exceeding 5 years.

Benefit basis only — no indemnity products permitted (individual or group). May also offer health insurance on unit linked platform. Life insurer health products additionally subject to Schedule I (life) provisions.
Product Design Principles & Inclusivity Mandate

Products must be available for: all ages; all existing medical conditions including PEDs, chronic illness, disabilities, HIV/AIDS, mental illness; all systems of medicine including AYUSH; all treatment settings (domiciliary, OPD, day care, homecare); all regions and occupational categories; persons with disabilities. Emergency coverage must never be denied.

Products must comply with: Mental Healthcare Act 2017; Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016; Surrogacy (Regulation) Act 2021; Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Act 2019; HIV and AIDS (Prevention and Control) Act 2017. All general and health insurers must offer a model product for PWD / HIV/AIDS / mental illness.

Insurers to endeavour to cover: robotic surgeries, stereotactic radiosurgery, oral chemotherapy, immunotherapy (monoclonal antibody), deep brain stimulation, intra-vitreal injections, stem cell therapy (haematopoietic for bone marrow transplant), bronchial thermoplasty, IONM, and other advanced modalities.
Board-approved AYUSH policy mandatory; AYUSH treatments at par with allopathy; policyholder has right to choose treatment system.
Premium fixed for policy term; instalment payment facilities permitted. Renewal loadings at portfolio level only — not individual policy experience; discounts permitted for good claims experience.

Appointed Actuary must review all products at least once per year, considering: reasonable expectations of all stakeholders; financial viability; emerging risks and experience; other relevant factors. AA presents results to PMC with recommendations for modification or withdrawal. Any premium or benefit revision must be based on credible underlying experience.
Product Management Committee (PMC)

Mandatory members: Appointed Actuary, Chief Marketing/Distribution Officer, Chief Investment Officer, Chief Technology Officer, Chief Compliance Officer. Additional senior management as members or invitees permitted. Quorum: 3 members + AA. CEO has overall responsibility and must countersign relevant certifications.

(i) Ensure adherence to product design and pricing principles. (ii) Ensure product appropriateness for target market. (iii) Regulatory compliance and File and Use filing. (iv) Approval of Use and File products. (v) Periodic product performance review and corrective action. (vi) Modification or withdrawal of products. (vii) Overall product management. (viii) Maintain documentation of all product decisions for IRDAI inspections.

Must record concurrence/sign-off on product-related risks (capital requirements, profitability, underwriting, reinsurance) before product approval. Systems, infrastructure, and SOPs must be confirmed in place from policy issuance to claim settlement before PMC recommends product launch.

Constitution: minimum 2 KMPs + 3 senior management officials (one level below KMP) + 1 permanent invitee from PMC. Quorum: 3 members including 1 KMP + permanent PMC invitee. Approves all insurer advertisements; must consider customer expectations and potential market conduct issues.
Product Filing Routes
Prior filing to IRDAI + UIN assignment required before marketing/launch. UIN disclosed in all product literature, policy documents, and supporting documents.

UIN obtained first; insurer may then launch without prior IRDAI filing. PMC approval mandatory in all cases. If product not launched within 30 days of UIN generation: full PMC approval process must be repeated.

Individual products/riders/add-ons: Form IRDAI-HIP. Group products (including Govt schemes): Form IRDAI-HLTGRP. Withdrawal: Form IRDAI-HPW within 30 days of withdrawal. Modification of existing product: also requires PMC approval and fresh Form IRDAI-HIP.
Health-Specific Provisions — Schedule III

Any condition, ailment, injury or disease: (a) diagnosed by a physician ≤36 months before policy commencement; OR (b) for which medical advice or treatment recommended/received ≤36 months before commencement. Not applicable to overseas travel policies. Life insurers may define separate norms at reinstatement.

PED waiting period: maximum 36 months of continuous coverage. Specific disease waiting period: also maximum 36 months (except due to accident). Insurers encouraged to offer shorter periods. Continuous renewal without break required to complete waiting periods.

After 60 continuous months of coverage (including portability and migration credits), no policy and no claim can be contested by the insurer on grounds of non-disclosure or misrepresentation — except established fraud. Enhanced SI: a fresh 60-month moratorium clock runs from the date of enhancement, applied only to the enhanced amount.

Health policy renewable except: established fraud, non-disclosure, misrepresentation, or product withdrawal. Insurer cannot deny renewal because policyholder made claims in prior years (except benefit-based policies that terminate on benefit payment, e.g. CI). No fresh underwriting/medical examination at renewal unless sum insured is being increased. If product withdrawn: policyholder gets one-time option to renew within 90 days, or migrate to suitable alternative.

15 days (monthly mode) / 30 days (all other modes). During grace period: condonation of delay — not treated as policy break. All accrued credits (SI, NCB, waiting periods, moratorium) protected if renewed during grace period. If premium paid in instalments during policy period, coverage also available during grace period.

Transfer of PED/waiting period/NCB/moratorium credits from one product to another within the same insurer. Insurer may underwrite the migrating proposal if insured has not been continuously covered for 36 months.

All indemnity-based health policies of general and health insurers (except PA and travel) must permit portability regardless of individual or group policy. Credits transferred: sum insured, NCB, specific waiting periods, PED waiting period, moratorium. Existing insurer must transfer data via IIB portal within 72 hours of request. Acquiring insurer must decide within 5 days of receiving information.
At every renewal policyholder chooses: (a) Cumulative Bonus — addition to sum insured without premium increase; OR (b) Discount on renewal premium. Choice is made by policyholder, not insurer.

Indemnity: policyholder nominates primary insurer; primary coordinates balance claim with other insurers — no hassle imposed on policyholder. Benefit: on occurrence of insured event, policyholder may simultaneously claim from all insurers under all policies.
All insurers must establish a separate servicing channel for health insurance claims and grievances of senior citizens. Channel details to be published on insurer website.
Master Circular — Customer Protections

Mandatory with every policy for individual and group policyholders. Contents: type of insurance, sum insured, coverage, exclusions, sub-limits, deductibles, waiting periods, free look, migration, portability, moratorium; plus claims procedure, servicing, grievance redressal details and Insurance Ombudsman contact details. Acknowledgement (physical or digital) required from policyholder. Available in local language on request.

30 days from date of receipt of policy document; available for policies with term ≥ 1 year. If cancelled with no claim: proportionate premium refund. Refund processed within 7 days; delay attracts bank rate + 2% from date of request.

7 days written notice required. Refund: proportionate premium for unexpired term if policy ≤1 year and no claim made. For multi-year policies: full premium refund for future policy years where risk coverage has not commenced.

Insurer must decide on cashless request within 1 hour of receipt. Pre-authorisation available via digital mode. Dedicated hospital help desks permissible. Insurer to strive for 100% cashless settlement — reimbursement only in exceptional circumstances.

Final discharge authorisation within 3 hours of hospital's discharge request. Policyholder must not be made to wait. Delay beyond 3 hours: additional hospital charges borne by insurer from shareholders' funds. Death during treatment: claim processed immediately and mortal remains released without delay.

Claims not settled within prescribed timelines: insurer pays interest at bank rate + 2%, paid suo-moto (without policyholder request). Documents collected by insurer/TPA from hospitals — policyholder not required to submit.
No claim may be repudiated without approval of PMC or a 3-member sub-group of PMC constituted as CRC. Repudiation must cite specific policy terms and conditions.

Insurer's grievance response letter must include Ombudsman contact details for escalation. Ombudsman award: insurer must comply within 30 days of receipt of award. Non-compliance: Rs 5,000 per day payable to complainant, plus penal interest under Insurance Ombudsman Rules 2017.

Board-approved criteria for: monitoring TPA performance, customer servicing, TATs. TPA remuneration must not depend on Incurred Claim Ratio of policies served. Customer feedback obtained; claw-back mechanism for poor TPA performance; clawed-back amounts passed to customers. Payments to TPAs made only after full and satisfactory service delivery.

Insurers may facilitate Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) number creation with policyholder's specific consent. Medical records shared with healthcare providers only with express consent in each instance. Insurers to implement end-to-end technology for seamless onboarding, renewal, servicing, grievance redressal, and claim settlement.
Exam Angle — Key Recall

Health Insurance Regulations 2016 is fully repealed. All health-specific provisions (PED, moratorium, NCB, renewal, portability) now sit within Schedule III of Insurance Products Regulations 2024. Do not cite the 2016 regulation in exam answers.

PED definition / waiting period: 36 months. Specific disease waiting period: 36 months. Moratorium: 60 months continuous. Grace period: 15 days (monthly) / 30 days (other). Cashless auth: 1 hour. Discharge auth: 3 hours. Late claims interest: bank rate + 2%. Ombudsman compliance: 30 days. Ombudsman penalty: Rs 5,000/day. Portability data transfer: 72 hours (existing insurer). Portability decision: 5 days (acquiring insurer). UIN lapse if not launched: 30 days. Existing product modification deadline: 30 September 2024.

Every product lifecycle step runs through PMC: design → approval → filing → annual AA review → modification → withdrawal. AA is a mandatory PMC member. CRC is a PMC sub-group. No product decision can bypass PMC.

Life insurers: benefit only, no indemnity. G&H insurers: both. Moratorium applies only to health policies of general and health insurers. Multiple policies: indemnity = primary insurer coordination; benefit = simultaneous claims from all insurers.
Protection of Policyholders' Interests 2024
Proposal form decision within **7 days** of receipt; policy document issued within **15 days** of acceptance
30 days from receipt of policy document (policies with term >= 1 year); proportionate premium refund if cancelled with no claim
Refund processed within 7 days of Free Look cancellation; delay attracts interest at bank rate + 2% from date of request
mandatory with every policy — covers sum insured, coverage, exclusions, sub-limits, deductibles, waiting periods, Free Look, portability, moratorium; available in local language on request
No claim repudiated without PMC or 3-member Claims Review Committee (CRC) approval; repudiation must cite specific policy terms
Documents collected by insurer/TPA — policyholder not required to submit
authorisation within **1 hour**; final discharge authorisation within **3 hours**
Delay beyond 3 hours: additional hospital charges borne by insurer from shareholders' funds
Claims not settled within timelines attract interest at bank rate + 2% paid suo-moto by insurer
Internal GRM → Insurance Ombudsman → Consumer Forum → Civil Court
Ombudsman award complied with within 30 days; non-compliance: Rs 5,000/day penalty to complainant + penal interest
Separate servicing channel required for senior citizen health insurance claims and grievances
Expenses of Management (EOM) 2024
Enable flexibility to manage expenses (including commissions) within IRDAI limits; optimally utilise resources to enhance policyholder benefits and improve insurance penetration
In force: **1 April 2024**; reviewed once every 3 years; applies to all insurers transacting health insurance business

content.text: General insurer: EOM ≤ **30%** of gross premium written in India per FY
Standalone health insurer: EOM ≤ **35%** of gross premium written in India per FY
Commission payable for health insurance products (by general or standalone health insurer) shall **not exceed** the overall EOM limit under these regulations
additional allowance of up to **5% of allowable EOM** (under Reg 6) to widen customer reach; depreciation on Insurtech capex also included
(if branch outside India / IFSC Insurance Office): additional allowance ≤ **10% of gross premium written outside India** through such branch/IIO (for health/general insurer)

content.text: additional allowance for health insurer reporting growth in gross direct premium from rural sector, PMJAY, PMFBY; allowance ≤ **15% of incremental premium** over previous FY from these schemes; in no case exceeding actual EOM incurred for these schemes in previous FY
(Schedule II for health/general insurer): prepared at FY end; signed by CEO, CFO, Chief Compliance Officer, and **Appointed Actuary**; certified by Statutory Auditor
Return reviewed by Audit Committee → placed before Board → filed with IRDAI along with annual financial statements
filed within **45 days** of FY end; reviewed by Audit Committee before Board approval
Computation based on accrual basis income/expenditure
IRDAI may grant forbearance (case-by-case) to health/general insurers exceeding EOM limits if **duration of business ≤ 5 years**
For FY 2023-24 excess: IRDAI may grant forbearance if Board confirms it will bring expenses within limits within **2 years** (by end of FY 2025-26); insurer must submit a business plan
Health/general insurer must ensure EOM within allowable limits on **overall basis**; excess charged to **Profit & Loss Account**
If actual EOM exceeds projected EOM (from Business Plan) by **≥10%**: no variable pay to MD/CEO/WTD/KMPs for that FY (not applicable if duration of business ≤ 5 years)

Penalties for breach: excess charged to P&L; restriction on new places of business; warning; valuation of financial health; penal action under s.102 of Insurance Act; restriction on MD/CEO/KMP performance incentives; removal of managerial personnel; IRDAI may restrict new underwriting in breached segments
Actuarial, Finance & Investment Functions of Insurers Regulations 2024
Overview
F. No. IRDAI/Reg/10/204/2024. Notified 20 March 2024; effective 1 April 2024. Applicable to all insurers including reinsurers, unless otherwise specified. Reviewed every 3 years.

ALSM (Life) 2016, ALSM (GI) 2016 (as amended), Financial Statements 2002 (as amended 2021), Appointed Actuary Regulations 2022, Investment Regulations 2016, Actuarial Report & Abstract (Life) 2016, Distributions of Surplus 2002, Inspection & Fee for Returns 2015, Loans & Advances to Employees 2016.

3 Chapters + 5 Schedules. Chapter I: Preliminary (definitions, 4 objectives). Chapter II: Guiding Principles (Board-approved policies, routes to Schedules). Chapter III: Miscellaneous (IRDAI power to issue circulars; repeal clause). Schedule I: Actuarial (5 Parts). Schedule II: Finance (3 Parts). Schedule III: Investment. Schedule IV: Loans & Advances. Schedule V: Inspection & Returns.

(1) Sound management of actuarial/finance/investment functions including valuation, bonus distribution, ALM, solvency, investment and risk management. (2) Regulatory returns give true and fair view of insurer's state of affairs. (3) Policyholders' interests protected. (4) Ease of doing business facilitated.

Issued 17 May 2024 under s.34 Insurance Act 1938 and s.14 IRDA Act 1999 read with Regulation 7. 270 pages of operational guidance. Reviewed annually. Supersedes all prior individual actuarial/finance/investment circulars. Chapter 1: Actuarial. Chapter 2: Finance. Chapter 3: Investment.
Key Definitions (Chapter I & Schedule I, Part I)

Amount representing that part of premium written attributable and allocated to succeeding accounting periods. Accounting concept. Estimated using approved method. Certified by CFO and Statutory Auditor (not the AA).

Reserve held in excess of UPR, allowing for expectation that UPR will be insufficient to cover claims, expenses, and maintenance costs during the unexpired risk period. Actuarial concept. Certified by Appointed Actuary.
Aggregate of UPR and PDR. URR = UPR + PDR. This is the total unexpired risk liability on the solvency balance sheet.
Provisions for liabilities under GI/health (re)insurance contracts including specific provision for adverse deviations. Technical Liabilities = URR + Claims Reserves (OS Claims + IBNR + IBNER).

Provisions for life (re)insurance liabilities — prospective gross premium valuation — includes MAD for mortality, morbidity, lapse, interest, expense. Excludes fallen-due liabilities and deposit-back arrangement liabilities.
Life: excess of admissible assets over Mathematical Reserves + other liabilities. GI/Health: excess of admissible assets over Technical Liabilities + other liabilities.
Computed per Schedule I (Part III for Life; Part IV for GI/Health), subject to a minimum of 50% of minimum capital under s.6 Insurance Act (i.e., 50% of ₹100 Cr = ₹50 Cr).
ASM ÷ RSM. Control level: ≥ 150% at all times. AA must ensure this is maintained.
Includes IBNER + estimate for reopened claims + provision for claims in transit on valuation date + ALAE. If IBNR for any year of occurrence is negative → set to zero. Certified by AA.
Expected changes in estimates of already reported claims (including ALAE). Separate from IBNR. Both certified by AA.

Provision for all outstanding reported claims on valuation date including ALAE. If amount known → provided in full; if estimable → case-by-case method; AA may use statistical method where appropriate (with certification).
Allocated Loss Adjustment Expenses — claim-related expenses directly attributable to a specific claim (e.g. legal/surveyor fees). Included in IBNR, IBNER, and OS Reserve.
Appointed Actuary — Eligibility (Schedule I, Part II)

(i) Ordinarily Resident in India; (ii) Fellow of IAI; (iii) Holds Certificate of Practice (CoP) from IAI; (iv) Full-time employee of insurer; (v) Not AA of any other insurer in India; (vi) No professional/other misconduct; (vii) Not over age 70. No relaxation permitted on Fellowship, CoP, or misconduct.

≥9 years in General or Health Insurance; of which ≥4 years post-fellowship. Of those post-fellowship years, ≥2 must be in preparation or review of statutory valuation or pricing of an Indian GI/Health insurer / Indian reinsurer / FRB. ≥3 years in middle or senior management.

If applicant has passed Specialist Application or Specialist Advanced subject in General or Health Insurance (IAI or MRA body), both the total (9 yrs) and post-fellowship (4 yrs) requirements are each reduced by 2 years → 7 years total / 2 years post-fellowship.
≥12 years in Life Insurance; ≥7 years post-fellowship (reduced by 2 if SA2/equivalent passed). ≥3 of those PF years in statutory valuation or pricing of Indian Life insurer. ≥3 years in management.
≥9 years in GI; ≥4 years post-fellowship (reduced by 2 if SA3/equivalent passed). ≥2 PF years in statutory valuation or pricing of Indian GI insurer. ≥3 years management.

Insurer applies to Competent Authority (IRDAI) with Board resolution + appointment letter copy. IRDAI approves or rejects within 30 days (opportunity of hearing before rejection). AA appointment takes effect only from date of IRDAI approval. If rejected → re-apply within 4 weeks.
If insurer cannot find eligible AA, may apply for relaxation of one or more conditions (except Fellowship, CoP, misconduct).
Appointed Actuary — Powers, Duties & Independence

AA has right of access to all information held by insurer necessary for duties. May seek information from any officer/employee. Entitled to attend, speak, and discuss at Board, management, shareholder, or policyholder meetings that relate to actuarial advice, solvency, policyholders' reasonable expectations, or matters requiring actuarial advice.

No provision of the letter of appointment may restrict or prevent AA's statutory duties. Insurer must not obstruct AA in performing duties. AA must not function in any capacity creating conflict of interest with the AA role.

(1) Ensure all records available for actuarial valuation. (2) Actuarial advice on product design, pricing, contract wording, investments, reinsurance. (3) Identify/monitor solvency risks; report material concerns to Board with recommendations; inform Competent Authority if insurer fails to act. (4) Certify assets and liabilities under s.64V. (5) Ensure solvency at control level under s.64VA. (6) Draw management's attention to matters requiring action to protect policyholders. (7) Comply with IRDAI directions. (8) Ensure pricing policy aligns with underwriting and claims management policy. (9) Ensure adequacy of reinsurance arrangements. (10) Contribute to risk management system. (11) Comply with s.21 of Act.

(i) Certify premium rates are fair. (ii) Certify IBNR, IBNER, and PDR determined using actuarial principles per Schedule I, Part IV. (iii) Render actuarial advice on expenses of management. (iv) Coordinate calculation of IBNR, IBNER, PDR. (v) Assess sufficiency and quality of data for reserving. (vi) Inform Board about reliability and adequacy of IBNR, IBNER, PDR.

Must inform Competent Authority in writing within a reasonable time if: (i) insurer's contravention of Act materially affects policyholders; (ii) directors failed to take action enabling AA to perform duties; (iii) officer/employee engaged in conduct preventing AA from performing duties.

AA must inform Competent Authority of any disciplinary proceedings against them within 7 days. If disqualified → ceases to be AA forthwith. Voluntary cessation: 3 months' notice required; cessation date must NOT fall between 1 January and 30 June (to avoid delaying annual filings).
Insurer Obligations & Mentor Actuary (Master Circular Ch.1)
Insurer must provide adequate resources to the AA. AA must report directly to the CEO.

Life insurers: ≥2 actuaries + the AA for pricing and valuation. GI/standalone health insurers: ≥1 additional actuary beyond the AA — by 31 December 2024. New insurers exempt for first 2 years from registration.

No insurer shall carry on insurance/reinsurance business without an AA. Non-compliance attracts action under the Act. Competent Authority may grant up to 1-year relaxation in exceptional circumstances.

With IRDAI approval, insurer may appoint a consulting actuary for up to 180 days while sourcing an AA. Must meet all AA eligibility criteria except the full-time employee condition. Responsible for all actuarial functions. Cannot hold this role at any other insurer simultaneously.

Where AA applicant is short of required experience by ≤1 year, insurer may apply for relaxation supported by a Mentor Actuary. Mentor must meet all AA eligibility criteria except the full-time employee condition, plus additional experience (Life: 5 extra years beyond AA standard; Health/GI: 3 extra years). Mentor and AA jointly responsible for all actuarial functions. Mentor cannot simultaneously be Mentor or AA for any other insurer.
Reserving — GI/Standalone Health Insurer (Schedule I, Part IV)

Marine Hull: 100% of net written premium of preceding 12 months. All other segments (including health): 50% of net written premium of preceding 12 months, OR proportion of unexpired period to total period of respective policies (daily pro-rata). Method must be applied consistently; change requires prior IRDAI approval.

PDR must be recognised if: expected claim costs + expenses + maintenance costs > related UPR. Maintained at insurer level (floor = zero). IRDAI monitors at segmental level for product sustainability. Negative PDR in one segment cannot offset positive PDR in another — except Motor OD and Motor TP sold as a package.

content.bold: Health lines of business (for claims reserves)
content.text: Health Insurance: Individual (line 9); Group Government Schemes (10); Group Employer/Employee (11); Group Other Schemes (12). Personal Accident: Individual (13), Group Government (14), Group Others (15). Travel (16). Also: Motor, Fire, Marine, Engineering, Aviation, Liability, Crop, Weather, Credit, Others.

(i) Determined using appropriate actuarial principles; certified by AA. (ii) Estimated on both gross and net of reinsurance basis. (iii) Estimated for each year of occurrence; aggregated for total provision. (iv) Negative IBNR for any year of occurrence → set to zero. (v) No discounting of estimated future claims development.

Set to zero: agents' balances not realised within 30 days; government scheme premiums not realised within 365 days; co-insurer balances outstanding >90 days; Indian reinsurer/FRB balances outstanding >365 days; other reinsurer balances outstanding >180 days; GST unutilised credit outstanding >120 days; 75% of deferred tax asset (other than accumulated losses); deferred tax asset on accumulated losses; unclaimed amount investments; furniture/stationery; deferred expenses; leasehold improvements.
Solvency Framework
ASM ≥ 150% RSM (Solvency Ratio ≥ 150%) at all times. AA must ensure this is maintained.
RSM is the higher of: (a) amount per Part IV (GI/Health) or Part III (Life) of Schedule I, and (b) 50% of minimum capital under s.6 Insurance Act (50% of ₹100 Cr = ₹50 Cr minimum).
If ASM falls below 150% RSM or is likely to do so → AA must immediately advise insurer and recommend corrective actions. If insurer fails to act → AA must inform Competent Authority in writing.
Bonus distribution philosophy (if applicable), ALM, investment policy, and risk management — all must be Board-approved. Fundamental governance requirement.
Risk-Based Capital (RBC) framework and IFRS 17 / Ind AS 117 adoption underway. Current factor-based solvency margin is the transitional framework.
Valuation — Life Insurance (Schedule I, Part III)

Standard method for all individual life business. Discounts future policy cash flows (premiums, benefits, bonuses, commissions, expenses, tax) at a valuation rate of interest. Exception: one-year renewable group term → reserve for UPR + PDR + IBNR. Riders on individual products: higher of GPV and UPR.

Each parameter = best estimate (from insurer experience → industry study → pricing basis if neither available) adjusted upward by MAD per IAI APS/Guidance Notes. MAD must not result in decrease of mathematical reserves. All parameters must be mutually consistent.

Mortality/Morbidity: published IAI/IRDAI-approved table; not less than 100% of published table (AA may justify lower %). Lapse: prudent assumption based on past experience; consider product nature, target market, distribution channel. Expenses: based on actual experience; inflation-adjusted consistently with valuation rate. Valuation interest rate: best estimate from current/expected yields adjusted downward by MAD.

1/9th of surplus allocated to policyholders for par fund goes to shareholders (max 10% of total actuarial surplus to shareholders). 100% to shareholders for non-par fund surplus. Prior IRDAI approval required if deviating from the 1/9th allocation.
Investment Norms (Schedule III)

Central Govt Securities ≥20%. Govt Securities (Central + State + Approved) ≥30%. Approved + Other Investments together ≤70%. Other Investments (s.27A(2)) ≤15%. Housing + Infrastructure (combined across all categories) ≥15%. Unit-linked segregated funds: ≥75% in approved investments.

Central Govt ≥25%. Govt Securities (Central + State + Approved) ≥50%. Approved + Other Investments together ≤50%. Other Investments ≤15%. Housing + Infrastructure ≥15%. Pension/Annuity/Group funds: Central Govt ≥20%; Govt ≥40%; no 'Other Investments' permitted.

First charge debentures; preference shares/equity of dividend-paying companies (≥10% dividend in 2 of last 3 FYs); fixed deposits with scheduled banks; government securities; ABS/PTCs with housing/infrastructure underlying; bonds rated AA or above (A1 for short-term); commercial papers of AIFI rated A1; money market instruments; TREPs (RBI-approved tri-party agent).

Pattern of investment norms do not apply to shareholders' funds held in excess of RSM. Such excess must: (a) comply with Govt/Housing+Infra norms first; (b) be held in a separate custody account with identified scrips; (c) be determined only after actuarial valuation certified by AA and filed with IRDAI.
Finance Functions — Financial Statements (Schedule II)

Revenue Account (Policyholders' Account) — Form A-RA; P&L Account (Shareholders' Account) — Form A-PL; Balance Sheet — Form A-BS; Receipts & Payments Account (AS3 direct method). Prepared separately by segment: Linked (Life/Pension/Health/Others); Non-Linked Par; Non-Linked Non-Par; business inside/outside India.
Revenue Account — Form B-RA per segment; P&L — Form B-PL; Balance Sheet — Form B-BS. Balance sheet Schedule 14 shows UPR Reserve and PDR Reserve separately. Figures rounded to nearest ₹ Lakhs.

Must confirm: (1) valid registration; (2) all statutory dues paid; (3) shareholding pattern compliant; (4) funds not invested outside India; (5) required solvency margins maintained; (6) assets not exceeding realisable/market value; (7) appropriate accounting standards applied; (8) internal audit system exists and is effective; (9) schedule of payments to directors' interests; (10) 5-year ageing of claims trend.

Must certify: (a) actuarial valuation of liabilities duly certified by AA with appropriate assumptions per IRDAI guidelines; (b) investments valued per Act and regulations; (c) balance sheet gives true and fair view; (d) financial statements prepared per Insurance Act 1938, IRDA Act 1999, and Companies Act 2013.
Audited financials filed within 3 months of FY-end, or 30 days from Board adoption — whichever is earlier.
SA1 Exam Angle — Key Themes

content.bold: The reserving chain — know who certifies each
content.text: UPR (CFO + Auditor) → PDR trigger (AA) → URR = UPR + PDR → Technical Liabilities = URR + OS Claims + IBNR + IBNER. UPR methods: 50% NWP or daily pro-rata for health; 100% for Marine Hull. Consistency rule: method change requires IRDAI approval.

Triggered when expected outgo (claims + expenses + maintenance) > UPR. Floor = zero at insurer level. No cross-segment netting — except Motor OD+TP package. Monitored at segmental level by IRDAI. AA must certify.
Estimated by year of occurrence then aggregated. Gross and net of reinsurance both required. Negative IBNR for any year of occurrence = zero. No discounting. AA certifies.

content.bold: AA eligibility — quick recall for health insurer
content.text: Fellow IAI + CoP + full-time + not AA elsewhere + under 70. 9 years total / 4 post-fellowship / 2 PF in Indian GI/HI valuation or pricing / 3 years management. Each reduced by 2 years if SA1/SA3 passed.

Certify IBNR + IBNER + PDR. Certify premium rates as fair. Certify adequacy of reinsurance. Ensure ASM ≥ 150% RSM at all times. Inform Board + Competent Authority if solvency at risk. Disclose disciplinary proceedings within 7 days.
Health insurer: Central Govt ≥20%; Govt ≥30%; Other Investments ≤15%; Housing+Infra ≥15%. Life insurer: Central Govt ≥25%; Govt ≥50%; Other Investments ≤15%; Housing+Infra ≥15%.
This regulation replaced ALSM 2016, Appointed Actuary Regulations 2022, and Investment Regulations 2016 among others. Do not reference any of the 9 repealed regulations in exam answers.
Re-insurance Regulations 2024
Simplified Quota Share and Excess of Loss structures
Defined retention hierarchies for Indian reinsurers, Foreign Reinsurer Branches (FRBs), and Cross-Border Reinsurers (CBRs)
CBR residency and regulatory compliance rules apply
PMC must review reinsurance risks before product launch
Appointed Actuary responsible for ensuring adequacy of reinsurance arrangements
Reinsurance arrangements must be consistent with product profitability and capital assessments approved by PMC
Registration & Capital 2024
Minimum capital entry: **Rs 100 Cr** under s.6 of Insurance Act
Structure for amalgamations and transfer of shares; net worth requirements
Board-level governance of all product, expense, and capital decisions via PMC
Insurer must at all times maintain ASM >= 150% RSM
IRDAI approval required before AA appointment takes effect; IRDAI must decide on AA appointment application within 30 days of receipt
🧠 Mnemonic: 17 Risk Categories
**Sequence**: DCCS · IEP · MV · GCMC · LRF · AN
**Phrase**: *"Dogs Chase Cats Sometimes; It Exasperates People. Most Vets Give Chase; Many Cats Lose. Running Fast And Nimble."*

**Key**: Data · Claim rates · Claim amounts · Screening | Investment · Expenses · Persistency | Mix · Volume | Guarantees · Competition · Management · Counterparties | Legal · Reputation · Fraud | Aggregation/Cat · Non-disclosure
SP1 Textbook: Nature of Risks (Chapters 23–24)
Nature of Risk Components
the underlying model structure is inappropriate or contains errors
wrong parameter values used, even if the model structure is correct
sample error and heterogeneity mean actual experience deviates unpredictably from the model
Policy & Other Data Risks
Inaccurate or incomplete policy records → actuarial results and recommendations are unreliable
model points used to represent the portfolio may not capture its actual characteristics accurately
e.g. using USA data for an Indian market without adequate adjustment
Claim Rates (Morbidity / Mortality)
Future parameters can never be predicted with certainty — new diseases and medical trends can emerge at any time
uncertainty grows the further into the future projections extend
Claim Amounts (PMI-specific)
Short-term variability in procedure costs, even within a single year
Medical advances can shift costs rapidly — e.g. hip replacement rehab time shortened from weeks to days, significantly reducing per-claim cost
Earlier Screening & Diagnosis
For CI: earlier diagnosis = earlier payment of the sum insured
Screening campaigns can prompt claims that would otherwise never have been payable (e.g. policy would have lapsed or expired before diagnosis)
Investment Performance & Assets
a company appearing solvent today may become insolvent the next day after an asset value change (snapshot risk)
Low reserves in CI and PMI mean investment performance is relatively less important compared to long-term products such as LTCI
Expenses and Inflation
Mismatch risk — expense inflation may outpace the loadings built into premiums over time
low business volume means fixed costs are spread over fewer policies, increasing per-policy expense above assumed
third-party administrators (e.g. for IP or PMI claims) add costs that are harder to predict and control
Persistency & Selective Withdrawal
healthier lives are more likely to lapse, leaving behind a worse-than-average insured group
if early lapses occur, initial acquisition costs are not recouped from future charges
Mix & Volume of New Business
smaller-than-expected average policies generate lower premium loadings but fixed per-policy costs remain, reducing contribution to overheads
strains capital resources and administration capacity
fixed development and overhead costs are not recovered
Guarantees and Options
Terms must be offered in advance of the triggering event, making costing inherently uncertain
Health and care events are harder to predict than life events, demanding more caution in pricing and reserving
Stochastic models are usually required to properly value the cost of guarantees
Competition & Management Actions
pressure to reduce premiums, increase coverage, or add guarantees beyond what is prudent
management may set aside the actuary's recommendations to pursue market share or shareholder returns
Counterparties (RI / Distributors / TPAs)
reinsurer fails to meet claim recovery obligations, leaving full cost with the insurer
broker holds premiums or claims payments and delays/fails to pass them on
third-party administrators can commit expenditure on behalf of the insurer, reducing claims cost control
Legal, Regulatory & Tax
Retrospective legislative changes can override policy terms for in-force contracts
Prohibition of certain rating factors — e.g. race, genetics — can affect pricing ability
Reputation & Operational Fraud
media or social media exposure, particularly for claims involving the sick or elderly, can damage brand and new business volumes
staff in positions of trust may embezzle funds or leak confidential information; requires robust audit and governance
Aggregation, Concentration & Catastrophe
localised outbreak of illness affecting a geographically concentrated insured group
industry-specific exposure (e.g. mining workforce with a specific occupational disease)
pandemic, war, or terrorism giving rise to a large number of claims from a single cause

⬡ - Overseas/expatriate PMI adds accumulation risk: expatriate communities are close-knit and may face higher epidemic/pandemic exposure than the general population. Exchange rate, investment, lapse (currency-driven), operational, and taxation risks also emerge in a new territory. [src:IAI_0523]
Non-disclosure & Anti-selection
asymmetric information at proposal stage makes accurate premium rating more difficult
accepting all lives initially and underwriting at claim stage complicates proof of non-disclosure and increases media risk on claim refusals
access to genetic test results is subject to ongoing legislative debate and varies by jurisdiction

⬡ - Poor selling practices are a systemic cause of non-disclosure — sellers failing to explain the materiality of information, or completing forms on behalf of customers. Review of application forms, marketing literature and policy wording can reduce incidence. [src:IAI_0722]
Product-specific: IP Insurance
Sickness transition probabilities (claim inception and termination rates) are the primary risk
Moral hazard — claimants have less incentive to return to work while receiving benefit
Leniency of the incapacity definition increases both expected cost and uncertainty of outcome
Product-specific: CI Insurance
Diagnosis rates are the primary risk, driven by medical advances and screening programmes
Limited historical data in newer markets → high parameter risk
Heterogeneous industry data (different CIs covered by different insurers) reduces reliability of pooled data
Product-specific: LTC Insurance
High claim inception probabilities and transfer between dependency levels are the main risks
Significant investment and reserving risk given large reserves that accumulate prior to claims
policyholders may expect 100% of care costs to be covered, but most benefit definitions do not guarantee this
Product-specific: PMI
Third parties (consultants, doctors, hospitals) control the triggering and cost of claims — insurer has limited direct control
Risk of single very large claims (e.g. expensive overseas treatment) or accumulation of claims from one incident
Capital strain is lower than for long-term products due to short-term nature of the contract
Inflation Risk (Macro Environment)

⬡ - In a high-inflation environment, lapses may be selective: healthier lives may choose to drop cover to save money, leaving behind a worse-than-average insured pool — particularly affecting longer-term guaranteed-premium products. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - High inflation increases the risk of non-disclosure at underwriting: cost-pressured consumers may be tempted to omit material information to reduce premiums, raising claims investigation costs and fraud risk. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Operational risk increases under high inflation: hiring freezes to manage costs increase workload on existing staff, raising the risk of errors, staff turnover, and quality deterioration. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Counterparty risk may increase in a high-inflation environment: reinsurers may be downgraded, and outsourced service providers may cease contracts or reduce quality if their own cost base is squeezed. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Options and guarantees embedded in policies (e.g. guaranteed premium rates, guaranteed insurability options) are more likely to come into the money in a high-inflation environment, increasing their cost and reducing product profitability. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Group business retention may be higher during inflationary periods as employers use benefits-in-kind rather than salary increases to retain staff — partially offsetting the lapse risk on individual business. [src:IFoA_0924]
Climate & Catastrophe Risk (PMI-specific)

⬡ - Climate-related events (e.g. flooding) can increase PMI claim frequency and severity, introduce greater year-on-year variability, and shift the regional mix of claims — with flood-prone areas experiencing large spikes while other regions remain unaffected. Extreme events create tail-risk that standard historical data cannot capture. [src:IFoA_0923]

⬡ - Flood events impact PMI claims through: increased incidence of injury, infection, and disease; worsening of existing conditions (e.g. respiratory illness); damage to healthcare infrastructure reducing treatment access; supply disruptions for medicines; and delayed claim settlement due to increased complexity. [src:IFoA_0923]

⬡ - Historic claims data becomes unreliable as a predictor of future experience under climate change — trends are harder to identify, credible data takes years to accumulate, and external sources (university studies, government flood models) must be evaluated for credibility before being incorporated into pricing or capital models. [src:IFoA_0923]

⬡ - Allowing for increased flood risk in regulatory capital (99.5% VaR) is highly challenging: limited data on tail scenarios, difficulty quantifying how flood risk changes correlations between risks (morbidity, expenses, lapses), need for external expertise, and risk that initial capital estimates are materially imprecise and require revision over time. [src:IFoA_0923]
Underwriting Strategy
Prevent anti-selection, classify risks equitably, maintain competitor parity.
Medical (files, exams) vs. Non-Medical (questionnaires, tele-interviewing).
Moratorium underwriting (often PMI), Free Cover Limits (Group).
Standard rates, rated premiums (+%), exclusions, deferral, decline.
Cost vs. Value of info, speed of onboarding (straight-through processing).

⬡ - Telephone interview underwriting for IP gives the insurer a wide range of relevant information, allows nuanced risk assessment, reduces anti-selection, and makes subsequent claims management simpler — but is time-consuming, expensive, and prone to human error. From the customer perspective: fair premium and claim certainty are advantages; lengthy process, potential decline, and intrusive disclosure requirements are disadvantages. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - For OP cover, underwriting emphasises high-frequency/low-severity risk. Controls include: medical declaration and diagnosis testing upfront, simplified underwriting for small sub-limits, co-payment/deductible design, and credibility approaches with high claim counts but low amounts. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - Where a significant proportion of claims are being declined, strengthening initial underwriting (e.g. collecting more medical evidence at inception) can reduce non-disclosure and misrepresentation, improving the declined-claim ratio over time. [src:IAI_0722]
Automated / AI Underwriting

⬡ - IP is more complex to underwrite than other health products because incapacity can arise from a wide range of causes (back pain, stress, mental health) that are not relevant to CI or PMI — an automated system must be explicitly designed to assess these IP-specific factors. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - Automated underwriting can improve speed, consistency, and cost-efficiency; faster decisions may attract more financial advisors and increase new business volumes. However, human underwriters offer nuanced judgment for complex cases that AI may not replicate, and their expertise is permanently lost if they are made redundant. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - Key risks of automated underwriting: (a) bias or discrimination if prohibited characteristics (or proxies for them) are embedded in the algorithm, (b) data quality issues leading to incorrect decisions, (c) lack of transparency making it difficult to identify or explain errors, (d) complete operational dependence on a third-party system with no fallback if the vendor ceases trading or the system fails. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - Before implementing an automated underwriting system, an insurer should obtain from the vendor: details of other countries/companies where the system has been used (especially for IP), the data sources and risk factors used, how prohibited characteristics are excluded, data security and jurisdiction controls, pricing structure, ongoing support and update processes, and regulatory track record. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - Governance controls for automated underwriting: human oversight with override capability; pre-launch testing and bias checking; regular sample-based monitoring against human decisions; clear feedback channels for advisors and policyholders; phased implementation; written undertaking on data law compliance; contingency processes if the system fails. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - Implementing automated underwriting increases reserving and capital uncertainty in the short term as existing claims data may no longer be representative of the new business mix — prudence margins in reserves and capital should be increased until sufficient experience under the new system has accumulated. [src:IFoA_0424]
Underwriting Policy Review

⬡ - Failing to regularly review underwriting policy exposes an insurer to: over-insurance and fraud risk, concentration in poor-risk segments, uncompetitive terms (inflation eroding financial limits; outdated medical limits vs market), regulatory non-compliance (e.g. mental health access rules), and loss of broker/IFA support leading to volume and expense risk. [src:IFoA_0923]

⬡ - When reviewing underwriting policy, investigate: how current policy is being implemented in practice; cost/benefit of current approach; early claims experience (anti-selection indicator); claims by underwriting category (policy effectiveness); exposure data by age/occupation/geography; competitor and market practice; reinsurer/consultant data; lapse experience; and distributor opinions. [src:IFoA_0923]

⬡ - When a competitor removes initial medical underwriting and moves to moratorium/PEC exclusions: standard lives may get better terms from the insurer retaining full UW; groups with loaded/declined occupations may migrate to the competitor; the insurer's book becomes concentrated in cleaner risks but may lose volume — strategic responses include matching the competitor's approach or tightening UW to offer lower prices to better risks. [src:IFoA_0923]

⬡ - Non-disclosure risk is higher in online and direct sales channels where no intermediary explains the legal duty of disclosure or the consequences of misrepresentation — policyholders may omit material information without realising it. Mitigant: clear disclosure prompts and prominent consequences notices in the online journey. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - For online-distributed, lower-sum products (e.g. MME), simplified underwriting (including moratorium or exclusion-only approaches) is appropriate: the premium income is too small to justify full medical underwriting, and fixed benefit amounts reduce the anti-selection incentive compared to indemnity PMI. [src:IFoA_0922]
Biometric & AI-Based Underwriting Tools

⬡ - A photo-based AI underwriting tool (estimating age, gender, smoker status, weight, and healthy/overall life expectancy from a smartphone image) can enable more individualised IP pricing and reduce underwriting costs and margins for uncertainty — but requires higher margins if tool accuracy is uncertain, and past claims data becomes less representative once the underwriting basis changes. [src:IFoA_0422]

⬡ - Key risks of photo-based underwriting: photo fraud (altered, old, or substitute images); algorithmic bias against protected characteristics with serious reputational and regulatory consequences; data consent and security obligations; business mix distortion (healthy lives self-select to the photo channel); reinsurer reluctance or requirement for higher margins; and vendor dependency risk if the underwriting company ceases trading. [src:IFoA_0422]

⬡ - Beyond pricing and acceptance, a biometric underwriting tool can be used for: case reserving (individual health data improves reserve accuracy); claims validation (verify claimant identity and health status at claim stage); rewards/wellness programmes (track lifestyle changes over policy life); marketing differentiation; and policyholder identification for administration. [src:IFoA_0422]
Risk Framework
Risk Types & Quantification
Market/Investment, Default/Credit, Underwriting (Mortality/Lapse/Expense), Operational.
Stress and Scenario testing, Internal Models vs. Standard Formula.
Mitigation & Capital
Reinsurance, Asset-Liability Matching (ALM), product redesign (reviewable premiums), stricter claims controls. [src:IFoA_0922]
Economic capital vs. Regulatory capital, risk appetite statements, diversification benefits.

⬡ - Expanding the product range (e.g. adding LTC to an insurer writing mainly short-term products for younger lives) can naturally hedge mortality/morbidity concentration risk: LTC generates longevity exposure that partly offsets mortality exposure on ACI. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Options and guarantees held by policyholders create adverse experience and capital strain risk: mitigants include restricting the terms under which options can be exercised, requiring additional premium at option exercise, or not offering options where the product is distributed without advice. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Surrender/withdrawal penalties on pre-funded LTC products can limit mass-lapse risk if government introduces public LTC provision — ensuring the insurer has time to run off liabilities in an orderly manner. [src:IFoA_0922]
Reserving & Valuation
Reserving Principles
Mathematical reserves (BEL), Risk Margin, Unearned Premium Reserve (UPR), Outstanding Claims (OSLR), IBNR. [src:IFoA_0922]
Bornhuetter-Ferguson, Chain Ladder / Run-off triangles (short-tailed), Cashflow projection (long-tailed).
Pricing (realistic + profit margin) vs. Supervisory (prudent/margins) vs. Embedded Value (market consistent).
Allowance for adverse deviation, discounting at risk-free rates.

⬡ - For a level-premium protection product, gross reserves typically build during the early years as the increasing cost of cover exceeds the level premium, then run off as the remaining term shortens. [src:IAI_0523]

⬡ - Best estimate (BE) assumptions are derived first, typically close to pricing assumptions. Margin for Adverse Deviation (MAD) is added, calibrated to regulatory requirements and the appointed actuary's judgment. [src:IAI_0523]

⬡ - The reinsurance credit equals the discounted value of future expected excess of reinsurance recovery over reinsurance premium. It reduces to zero at policy expiry, typically declining each year as the term shortens. [src:IAI_0523]
Reinsurance Credit in Valuation

⬡ - The reinsurance credit is the discounted present value of expected future net recoveries from the reinsurer (recoveries less premiums payable). It reduces gross reserves to give net reserves. [src:IAI_0523]
⬡ - For a level-premium CI product, the reinsurance credit typically declines year-on-year as the remaining term shortens, falling to zero at policy maturity. [src:IAI_0523]

⬡ - Reinsurance reduces reserves but also reduces the prudence margin: if the reinsurance premium rate equals the BE mortality assumption, the gross prudence for 50% of the benefit is lost, which reduces reserves and improves EV under traditional methods. [src:IAI_0523]
⬡ - Reinsurance premiums are paid in advance while recoveries are received in arrear — this creates a cash-flow timing disadvantage that slightly reduces investment income. [src:IAI_0523]
1. Reasons for Reinsurance

Factors affecting appetite include size of insurer, marketplace experience, available free assets, portfolio size (credibility), and predictability of business outcome. Larger free assets and larger portfolio reduce the need for reinsurance. [src:IAI_0523]

Insurer compares potential maximum claim to free assets. Small/medium insurers cede top slice of large payouts to protect solvency. Particularly relevant for PMI where individual claims can reach hundreds of thousands. [src:IAI_0722]

Reinsurance reduces variance of expected experience relative to mean. Helps achieve smooth year-on-year accounts, especially for immature portfolios. Stop loss directly reduces extreme losses. Nearly all reinsurance types smooth profits to some degree. [src:IAI_1120,IAI_0722,IAI_0523]
