When entering new risk areas, reinsurer assists with product design, pricing, underwriting and claims management. Often via quota share with low initial retention. As insurer gains experience and confidence, reinsurer involvement reduces. [src:IAI_0523,IFoA_0922]

Insufficient capital may prevent accepting certain risks by sector or volume. Reinsurance cover reduces solvency capital requirements in line with proportion ceded (subject to upper limit due to reinsurer default risk). [src:IAI_1123,IAI_0523]

(i) New business strain: reinsurance commission factors future surplus streams as an effective loan. (ii) Improving free assets: reinsurer pays initial commission, receives share of future surpluses; arrangement is off-balance-sheet if contingent. (iii) Merger/acquisition: reinsurer advances funds against identified profitable block. Quota share is the norm for all three. [src:IAI_1222,IAI_0523]

Solvency capital arbitrage: reinsurer holds less capital per unit of risk — saving shared with cedant via cheaper premiums. Tax arbitrage: reinsurer subject to lower taxes, enabling favourable premium rates.
⬡ - The terms on which the reinsurer will accept business can provide a check on premium rates, and the arrangement may be a condition imposed by the regulator. [src:IAI_0523]
⬡ - The reinsurer may provide new software to assist with pricing, underwriting, administration or improving health outcomes for policyholders. [src:IAI_0523]
2. Types of Reinsurance Business

Individual risk-by-risk negotiation; both parties free to accept/reject. Used for risks outside treaty scope (unusually large or unusual features). Advantages: flexible, best terms sought per risk. Disadvantages: time-consuming, costly, cover uncertain, delays risk acceptance, reduces market standing.

Formal arrangement: cedant obliged to cede, reinsurer obliged to accept risks falling within treaty conditions. Efficient (automatic), certain (cover guaranteed within limits), cheaper. Disadvantage: inflexible once set up; treaty terms may restrict freedom. Treaty must precisely define: what is covered, financial arrangements, obligations of both parties.
First word = cedant's position; second = reinsurer's position. Ob/Ob is the standard treaty basis. Fac/Ob means the insurer can choose whether to cede; the reinsurer must accept if offered.
3. Proportional Reinsurance

Reinsurer covers an agreed proportion of each risk. Long-term health: quota share or surplus, on original terms or risk premium basis. Short-term health: quota share on original terms only. Reinsurance commission paid (or rebate) to cover cedant's initial expenses — only under proportional original terms business.

Premium and claims split in same fixed proportion. Reinsurer shares all policy risks: morbidity, investment, and lapse. Cedant provides premium rates to reinsurer for adequacy check. Deposits back: reinsurer deposits its reserve share with cedant — cedant retains full investment profit; mitigates reinsurer default risk.

Reinsurer sets its own premium rate independently of cedant's premium — gives cedant freedom to reprice without triggering reinsurance rate changes. Premiums applied to reinsured benefit (full benefit or sum-at-risk). Rates can be: (i) level (net level premium — spread over term), or (ii) yearly increasing (reflect rising age/risk). Either may be guaranteed or reviewable.

Proportions applied to excess of benefit over reserve (not full sum insured). Rarely used in H&C: IP/LTCI pay regular benefits so sum-at-risk is undefined; CI reserves are small relative to benefit so difference from full-benefit approach is negligible. Possible exception: unit-linked CI (sum at risk = benefit minus bid value of units).

Constant R% ceded on every risk in treaty. Purposes: spread risk (reduces parameter risk); write larger portfolios; encourage reciprocal business; improve solvency ratio (reduces net written premiums); satisfy statutory capital requirement; finance new business strain. Limitations: same % regardless of risk size (inefficient for large risks); cedes profit to reinsurer.

Long-term only; fixed sum insured required (not PMI). Insurer sets monetary retention per policy; reinsures only the excess above that level. More efficient than quota share at targeting large risks — reduces claims volatility. Advantages: write larger risks; reduce concentration risk. Disadvantages: less control over parameter risk; less suitable for financing.

Quota share: same % for all risks — simple, good for financing and solvency improvement but inefficient (cedes profit on small risks). Surplus: % varies by risk size — efficient at targeting large risks, reduces claims volatility, but more complex and less suitable for financing.
4. Non-Proportional Reinsurance

Caps large claim costs. Mainly used for short-term contracts and possibly group long-term contracts. Not appropriate for individual long-term policies where benefit is fixed at outset (no variability to cap). Limits may be indexed (stability clause) to preserve real value against inflation. No reinsurance commission (profit commission possible for lower layers only).

Operates on individual claims. Reinsurer pays claims above excess point up to upper limit. Layers stack with no gaps. Expressed as '£X in excess of £Y'. Reinsurer may cover only a proportion within the layer (e.g. 90%) to deter moral hazard in claim settlement. Used mainly for PMI portfolios.

Covers total claims across account above excess point for 12-month period. Excess point and upper limit expressed as % of earned premium income (claims ratio) — prevents insurer writing more volume to trigger fixed monetary limits. In H&C context, aggregate XL and stop loss are interchangeable (only one peril: morbidity). Rarely used for long-term insurance. Reinsurers reluctant: limited control over underwriting/claims; historically loss-making.

Covers claims from a single event or common cause (e.g. chemical explosion, epidemic). Pandemic cover is generally not available at affordable price due to extreme uncertainty. Event cover (for specific non-pandemic events) is generally available.

Accept risks that could produce very large claims; reduce risk of insolvency; stabilise technical results by reducing claims fluctuations; enable more efficient use of capital (lower reserves needed with lower volatility).
5. Financial Reinsurance (Fin Re)

Minimal insurance risk transfer. Primary aim: improve apparent accounting/supervisory solvency of cedant. Only effective where regulatory regime does not already credit future profits and does not require realistic liability for loan repayments. Scope significantly reduced under Solvency II.

content.bold: Risk Premium Fin Re (New Business Strain Relief)
content.text: Reinsurer pays large initial commission (= disguised loan). Repayments embedded in higher future reinsurance premiums over the remaining policy term. Lapse experience of the portfolio is factored into repayment schedule.

Reinsurer provides loan repayable only from future VIF profits — if VIF does not emerge, reinsurer has made a capital gift. Cedant does not reserve for repayments (off balance sheet). Contingent repayments are higher than non-contingent (reinsurer charges for the contingency). Little change to realistic embedded value (loan received ≈ PV of future repayments). Risk reduced: some uncertain VIF profit converted into certain cash.
6. Determination of Retention Level

Model distribution of claims costs at various retention levels. Set retention so ruin probability stays below a specified level (e.g. solvent in 995/1,000 simulations). For fixed-benefit long-term contracts: model claim numbers stochastically. For indemnity short-term: model both claim numbers and amounts stochastically.

Balance (a) cost of financing a risk experience fluctuation reserve and (b) cost of reinsurance (expense + profit loading). As retention rises, (a) rises and (b) falls. Adopt retention that minimises total (a) + (b).

Greater uncertainty about future claims; lower acceptable insolvency probability; greater variance of benefit levels; smaller free assets; higher cost of capital; less strict underwriting; more volatile business mix; high new business volumes / new business strain. Large insurers with sufficient free assets may dispense with XL entirely for normal policies.
7. Key Examiner Watchpoints

Cedant remains liable to policyholders even if fully reinsured. A minimum capital level must always be held regardless of reinsurance. Deposit back arrangements mitigate (but do not eliminate) this risk.

content.bold: When Reinsurer Knowingly Writes Loss-Making Business
content.text: Expects higher future profits; profits from connected business (same cedant/broker); retaining market share at bottom of (re)insurance cycle hoping premiums recover.

content.bold: Why Insurer Handles XL Claims Prudently Even If 100% Reinsured Above Excess
content.text: Preserve long-term relationship with reinsurer; potential profit commission at stake; future reinsurance premiums are experience-rated.

Cedant liable for reinsurer's share of all future and in-payment claims; may face large claims from non-proportional treaties; solvency position worsens; must seek replacement cover (likely more expensive); premium rate increases likely.

Names of parties; period/territorial limits; classes covered; exclusions; definition of loss; retention; cover granted; premium rates and payment terms; commission/profit commission; claim notification and payment terms; currency clause; access to risk details; termination terms; arbitration clause.
Portfolio Transfer Methods

⬡ - Indemnity reinsurance for portfolio transfer: the original insurer reinsures its liabilities with the acquiring insurer (cedant remains legally liable to policyholders). Policyholder notice is generally not required and policyholders do not have the right to object — making this a faster method. Regulatory approval is still required but familiarity with the method often speeds the process. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Assumption reinsurance for portfolio transfer: actual policies are novated from the original insurer to the acquiring insurer — the acquiring insurer becomes the new insurer of record. Requires policyholder notification and typically policyholder consent; this is slower, exposes the transfer to policyholder objections, and may also require the transfer of assets (creating further operational complexity if assets are in non-cash form). [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Choice between indemnity and assumption reinsurance in a time-constrained transfer: indemnity reinsurance is generally preferable where speed is critical — no policyholder communication requirement and no right of objection. The insurer should also consider the regulator's familiarity with each method, as a well-understood approach receives approval more quickly. [src:IFoA_0922]
Product Design Framework (SP1 Chapters 1-5)
Core Definition & Scope

Long-term (IP, CI, LTCI: typically 5+ years, usually no renewal) vs Short-term (PMI, health cash plans: annual renewable). Long-term products often sold by life insurers; short-term by general insurers or specialists.

For PMI—Inpatient (IP), Outpatient (OP), Day care, Domiciliary, AYUSH. Each must be explicitly defined. OP cover has much higher frequency/lower severity than IP; major operational readiness required before launch.

Indemnity (insurer bears medical inflation risk; reimburses actual costs) vs Fixed Benefit/Benefit Basis (insurer knows max exposure; policyholder bears cost variation risk). Life insurers in India restricted to benefit basis only; general/health insurers offer both.
Target Market & Customer Needs

By age, income, occupation, health status (including pre-existing conditions). Regulatory mandate (IRDAI 2024): must offer products catering to all ages, medical conditions, PED, chronic illness, disabilities, HIV/AIDS, mental illness, and all treatment systems (allopathy, AYUSH).
Products designed to fill gaps left by PMJAY (Ayushman Bharat—covers bottom 40% income). Private PMI adds depth of cover, wider hospital choice, faster access.
Match benefit design to policyholder protection gaps; distinguish between affordability (what they can pay) vs adequacy (what they actually need).
Benefit & Coverage Design

Sum insured (global); sub-limits by service (room, ICU, surgery, consultants, organ transplants, mental illness parity). Room/board limits create incentive toward cost-effective hospitals. Mental illness parity mandated—difficult to diagnose/control, fraud risk.
PED waiting period: maximum 36 months continuous coverage (was 48 months)
Disease-specific waiting period: maximum 36 months
Moratorium (non-contestability): 60 continuous months; after which no denial except fraud. Moratorium clock includes portability/migration credits, resets on enhanced sum insured.

Offered as cumulative bonus (increase in SI without premium increase) or discount on renewal premium—policyholder chooses form. Healthy lives self-select for NCD; pricing must account for selection benefit.
Applies to new illnesses only (not pre-existing/PED). Annual or per-claim reinstatement; critical design decision affecting benefit adequacy and claims frequency.
Cost-sharing mechanisms (reduce moral hazard; increase policyholder engagement). Regulatory compliance: must be clearly disclosed in Customer Information Sheet (CIS).
Product Features & Options
Second opinion provision, home nursing, day-care procedures, domiciliary care, ambulance cover, pre-authorization (mandatory under IRDAI 2024; 1-hour TAT for cashless), cashless facility.
Guaranteed Issue Options (GIO): No medical underwriting at entry; attracts anti-selection; premium loadings required.
Conversion Options: Rider-to-standalone conversion; benefit enhancement without fresh medical.
Guaranteed Renewability: Premium reviewable at portfolio level (not individual policy) per IRDAI 2024; no denial of renewal on grounds of prior claims.
Premium Guarantee Period: 1 or 3-year guarantee typical; insurer cannot reprice individual policies within guarantee period.
Usually nil for indemnity PMI/CI (no savings element). Impact model pricing as policyholder can exit without penalty (lapse risk). LTCI may have return-of-premium rider.
Exclusions & Conditions (Must Be Clearly Defined)

War, terrorism, non-disclosure, criminal acts, cosmetic procedures (except post-accident), fertility treatments, experimental/unproven treatments, sports injuries (depending on policy), alcohol/drug-related claims, self-inflicted injury.
High-risk occupations (mining, aviation, hazmat); extreme sports; geographic restrictions (war zones, epidemiology reasons).
World-wide, Asia-Pacific, or India-only. Affects premium; claims management capability in overseas locations.
Any condition diagnosed/treated within 36 months prior to policy commencement. Clear definition essential to avoid disputes.
General waiting period (usually 30 days for acute conditions), disease-specific (36 months for chronic disease), maternity (usually 9 months), PED (36 months max). Clearly stated in policy document.
Operational & Marketability Constraints

Product design must match insurer's IT/underwriting/claims processing systems. Complex benefits require robust pre-authorization and adjudication engines. OP cover with high volume demands high-capacity, low-cost processing.
Distribution channel (direct, tied agents, brokers, group) influences commission structure, underwriting standards, claims management approach, and product positioning.
Product design must fit within reinsured limits; excess of policy limits over reinsurance creates unhedged exposure. Quota share, XL layers, and aggregate XL affect product feasibility.

Brand positioning (economy, standard, premium); perceived value vs price; comparative advantages vs competitors. 'Saleability' vs 'adequacy' tension: complex, comprehensive cover is hard to sell; simple cover may be inadequate.
CI/IP riders attached to life cover increase attachment rate but complicate experience analysis and claims management.
Risks & Design Mitigations
Risk that policyholder deliberately incurs claims or inflates claims. Mitigation: benefit limits, co-payments, replacement ratio caps, claims review, hospital networks with fixed tariffs.
At Entry: Healthy lives opt for policies; comprehensive cover attracts sicker lives. Mitigation: appropriate medical underwriting, waiting periods, exclusions.
At Renewal: Selective lapsing (healthy lives leave when premiums rise; sicker remain). Mitigation: competitive pricing, strong retention strategy, NCD design to reward persistence.
NCD Selection: Healthier lives self-select for NCD; pricing must account for improved risk pool selecting into NCD.
Complex exclusions, sub-limits, waiting periods lead to policyholder dissatisfaction and complaints. Mitigation: clear wording, mandatory CIS, simplified benefit designs, staff training.

In indemnity PMI, insurer bears medical inflation (~12-15% p.a. in India historically; varies by treatment type). Failure to trend assumptions forward leads to rapid deterioration. Mitigation: annual repricing, inflation-linked sub-limits, shift toward benefit basis.
Profitability & Capital Impact (Solvency II Pillar 1)
New product launches consume capital due to negative asset shares early in policy life (acquisition costs exceed first-year premiums). Affects profit targets and ROI calculations.
Target Return on Capital (RoC) by product; Value of New Business (VNB); Embedded Value (EV). Hurdle rate typically 15-25% depending on product and company risk appetite.
New products with high claims uncertainty, guarantees, or policyholder options increase SCR. Must be assessed before launch via capital projection models.
Available Solvency Margin >= 150% Required Solvency Margin; affects distribution of free assets and capital-raising capacity.
India-Specific Regulatory & Market Context (IRDAI 2024)

IRDAI 2024 requires Board approval of all product, pricing, expense, and capital decisions via Product Management Committee (PMC). File-and-Use or Use-and-File regime for new products; UIN assignment required before launch.

All credits (SI, NCB, waiting periods, moratorium) transfer to new insurer; existing insurer provides data within 72 hours via Insurance Information Bureau (IIB). Acquiring insurer decides within 5 days. Alters experience significantly as waiting periods do not re-apply.

Government's Ayushman Bharat program covers bottom 40% with basic indemnity cover. Private insurers fill gap for middle/upper income; higher sum insured, wider hospital choice, faster claim settlement.

content.bold: ABHA (Ayushman Bharat Health Account) & Digital Records
content.text: National health account system improves UW and fraud detection capability. Voluntary participation; uptake still low. Data privacy regulations (DPDP 2023) restrict usage.
Historical trend ~12-15% p.a. in India (varies: ICU ~20%, routine OP ~8%). Repricing essential annually but risks selective lapsing if competitor pricing lags.

Detailed CIS, 30-day Free Look, mandatory 15-day grievance resolution, Board-governed commission, Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) principles, Ombudsman interface. Increase operational expenses and slow claims settlement.
Product-Specific Design Considerations

Key levers—Deferred Period (DP: 4, 8, 13, 26 weeks), Replacement Ratio (capped max 50-75% to maintain work incentive), Definition (Own Occ vs Any Occ vs ADL). Moral hazard higher in economic downturns ('unemployment disguise'). Group IP easier to underwrite (active-at-work condition).

Fixed lump sum on diagnosis (not on claim payment). Survival period (typically 14-28 days) reduces frequency but increases severity. Standalone vs accelerated (rider). Diagnosis-based (pays on diagnosis) vs cash plan (pays after diagnosis period). High anti-selection risk; medical advances change diagnosis timing.

Long claims duration (average 3-5 years); high inception; significant reserve build. Benefit-triggered on ADL loss (activities of daily living). Transfer between dependency levels (independent → partial → full). Savings element justifies surrender value design.

Annual renewable; multiple claims per policy. Indemnity or benefit basis. NCD critical for persistency. Pre-authorization reduces moral hazard; cashless settlement (100% target under IRDAI 2024) reduces administration. Provider networks essential for cost control.

Indemnity (insurer bears medical inflation risk; reimburses actual costs) [src:IAI_1124] vs Fixed Benefit/Benefit Basis (insurer knows max exposure; policyholder bears cost variation risk). Life insurers in India restricted to benefit basis only; general/health insurers offer both.

⬡ - Chronic illness is a long-term, typically incurable but manageable health condition (e.g. diabetes, heart disease, asthma, mental health) requiring ongoing medical care to maintain quality of life. [src:IAI_1124]

⬡ - Strategic benefits of analyzing chronic disease impact include: accurate risk assessment/pricing, tailored product development, proactive claims management, improved customer engagement (wellness programs), regulatory compliance, and informed reinsurance/investment/market segmentation strategies. [src:IAI_1124]
Data (SP1 Chapter 12 & Chapter 23)
1. Data Quality Criteria (RCAR)
relates to a similar class of lives and policy conditions to those expected for the product
sufficiently large volume of data to enable precise estimates (low sampling error)
accessible in a usable format, within reasonable cost
fit for the intended purpose
Also consider: **cost** of obtaining/analysing data and **suitability of format**
Actuaries use judgement to compile statistics from one or more sources meeting these criteria, then project to estimate future claims experience
2. Projecting Rates to Mid-Point of Insurance Usage
Data is essentially **historic**; must be projected forward to cover the full period rates will be in use
Rates must remain valid for: **[expected shelf-life of premium rates] + [expected duration of policy to termination or next review]**
vary by age AND calendar year; project forward to mid-point of expected usage period
vary by duration from claim commencement; experience improvement/deterioration over time must be allowed for
function of inflation, protocols, hospital charges, State provision; typically only need 1-year projection as PMI rates reviewed annually
Longer projection → greater uncertainty → larger margins required → higher premiums; shorter terms or frequent review dates reduce margins needed
incidence rates should be projected **separately by each cause of claim**, then combined
total claim inception and termination rates projected
3. Own Data
**Relevance — HIGH (best source)**
Reflects insurer's specific: underwriting approach; policy conditions; claims management; distribution method/channels; target market
Cost, reliability and format present no problems
**Credibility — OFTEN LOW**
Volume of business for most insurers is insufficient, especially when broken down into many risk cells
New insurer or new product line = extreme case; may take many years to build credibility
Where insufficient, must supplement with external data using appropriate adjustments
Techniques: state assumption as adjustment to a standard table; use statistical fitting procedures
4. Population Data
**Relevance — LOW to MODERATE**
Does not relate specifically to insured lives — different class of lives AND different claim definition
Two conflicting effects on insured vs population: (i) underwriting filters out less healthy lives, (ii) anti-selection means buyers may expect to claim → improvement may be less marked
Adjustment for socio-economic group and region improves relevance
CI-specific issue: population double-counts (e.g. heart attack + bypass surgery = one claim but two records in population data) — overlap must be adjusted for
**Credibility — HIGH**
Large volumes make population data statistically credible
**Other drawbacks**
Accuracy/reliability may be questionable (subjective definitions, double-counting)
May not be in appropriate format
May be out-of-date before publication
5. Reinsurers' Data
**Relevance — MODERATE (adjustments needed)**
Reinsurer wants data to be accurate as profits under treaty reflect premiums calculated
Must adjust for insurer's specific underwriting and claims management practices
Typically provided in return for a reinsurance treaty (share of risk/profits); explicit fee less common
**Credibility — MODERATE TO HIGH**
Reinsurers draw from multiple companies across the market — excellent for new product launches
Example: reinsurers drove CI insurance development in the 1980s using data from other markets weighted by local population data
Credibility increases as the market for the product grows
6. Market Data
**6a. Insured Lives (Industry / CMI) Data**
Agreement among companies to pool statistics → industry-wide information more credible than individual company data
UK example: Continuous Mortality Investigation (CMI) — covers IP and CI markets
Standard morbidity tables produced from this source; useful as base reference for experience monitoring and assumption setting
Standard tables not published frequently — require significant preparation; new standard considered when simple adjustments to existing table are no longer adequate
**Advantages**
Represents insured experience (more relevant than population data)
Sufficient volume → statistically credible
Reflects local companies and market practices; compiled by experts (resolves individual company data issues)
**Disadvantages**
Only a **market average** → not strictly relevant to any individual company; adjustment needed
May take years to publish → may be out-of-date
Adjustments for company-specific underwriting/claims management needed; caution where launching new health product — strict mortality underwriting may not translate directly to morbidity margins
Limited detailed data available for short-term classes (PMI) compared to long-term classes (CI, IP)
**6b. Returns to Insurance Supervisor (Regulatory Returns)**
Produced by all insurers; primary purpose is supervisory solvency assessment, NOT to assist competitors
Contains: policy numbers, amounts, premiums, benefit breakdowns by policy class; on/off-book movements by cause
Accuracy not questioned but limited detail for individual risk cell estimation
Most useful as: **high-level check** on premiums derived from other sources; checking competitiveness on launch
Key shortcoming: typically no breakdown by age, gender, smoker status etc.
7. Other Sources
**Overseas Data**
Method: estimate home insured lives experience = (home population morbidity) × (overseas insured lives / overseas population) — broad starting point only
Always likely to be **less relevant** than home territory equivalents

Adjustments needed for: different cultures; different State healthcare provision; different market practices; different legislation/policy conditions; different underwriting and claims management; different time periods; heterogeneity of overseas industry data
May be credible in volume
**Actuarial Consultants**
Similar to reinsurers — access to national and international data sources
Key difference: consultants do NOT share in the risk; charge a fee directly
**Rate Table Software**
Provides market premium rate comparisons for individual business
Useful for: gathering initial data; checking reasonableness and competitiveness of office premiums post-calculation
More detailed than trade magazine equivalents
**Trade Magazines**
Provide types/levels of benefits and premium rates charged in the market
Do NOT normally include claim statistics
Useful as a broad reasonability check

⬡ - For overseas PMI: national statistics, local regulator data, hospital expense data from overseas providers, competitor pricing data, and reinsurers' multi-national databases are key external sources. Own domestic data may give some relevant baseline but requires adjustment for local conditions. [src:IAI_0523]
8. Data Required by Product Type
**Income Protection (IP)**
Dates of: birth, entry, exit, diagnosis, first stopping work, notification, start/end of sickness claim
Reasons for exit / end of claim payments
Construct central exposures for healthy (E^H_x) and claiming (E^C_x) policyholders
Estimate transition intensities: claim inception (σ), recovery (ρ), deaths from healthy (μ), deaths from claiming (ν)
Note: deferred period requires inclusion/exclusion adjustments at investigation boundaries
**Critical Illness (CI)**
Dates of: birth, entry, exit, diagnosis/treatment, notification, claim payment
Reason for exit; type of disease/procedure
Construct central exposure for healthy lives (E^H_x)
Count claims by disease/procedure type (I^j_x)
Include: IBNR (incurred but not reported) and RBNS (reported but not settled)
Exclude: claims relating to diagnoses prior to investigation period
**Long-Term Care Insurance (LTCI)**
Same as IP but with exposures separately for EACH benefit level (claim state)
Requires dates of transition between benefit levels
No return transitions normally occur
Estimate transition intensities between each pair of benefit levels (e.g. Benefit level 1 → 2)
**Private Medical Insurance (PMI)**
Assuming annual renewals: count policies taken out/renewed each year by age (N_x)
Count claim incidences by age and benefit/procedure class (I^k_x)
Record total claim amounts by cohort (C^k_x)
Estimate: claim incidence rate (î^k_x = I^k_x / N_x) and average claim cost (ĉ^k_x = C^k_x / I^k_x)
Include IBNR and RBNS; exclude prior-period claims
9. Policy Data Risk (Chapter 23)
Insurer's own data may be inaccurate, inadequate or incomplete → models relying on these data will be similarly affected [src:IFoA_0922]
(from model points): Model points used to represent in-force business may not adequately capture portfolio characteristics → invalid results and incorrect business decisions
Key actuary responsibility: carry out checks on data quality; must at minimum be aware of any data problems
Extreme consequence: company declaring solvency incorrectly; lesser consequence: incorrect business decisions (e.g. underestimating available capital)
Internal data may be inadequate due to: missing data; inaccurate data; insufficient volume for credibility
Even good-quality internal data may be inappropriate if based on an unsuitable class of lives
External data (industry, population, overseas) may also be inadequate, unreliable, or inappropriate due to population mismatch
10. Summary: Relevance vs Credibility by Source
High relevance, often low credibility
Low-moderate relevance, high credibility
Moderate relevance (adjustable), moderate-high credibility
More relevant than population, less relevant than own; more credible than own, less than population
Limited relevance (market average, low detail), reasonable accuracy
Low relevance, may be credible in volume
Similar to reinsurers; fee-based rather than risk-sharing
Useful only as high-level reasonability checks
Using External Public Data for Reserving

⬡ - Publicly available geographic data (e.g. drug prescribing rates, statutory sick pay records by region) can be used to improve IP reserve segmentation by mapping policyholder postcodes to regional risk indicators — identifying concentrations of higher/lower morbidity risk not captured in the original underwriting data. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Drug prescription data by region can serve as a forward indicator of claim incidence trends: areas with high prescribing of drugs associated with musculoskeletal or chronic conditions are likely to have elevated IP claim inception rates and longer claim durations. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Using geographic public data to refine IP reserves can improve: IBNR estimates (estimating sickness incidence in unreported periods), claims-in-deferred-period reserves (predicting which incipient sicknesses will become payable claims), and claims-in-payment reserves (estimating expected claim duration by region). [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Ethical and regulatory concerns with using public data for reserving/pricing: (a) cross-subsidies between policyholders reduce, potentially making cover unaffordable for higher-risk lives; (b) guaranteed premium policyholders cannot be repriced — inequity arises if only reviewable premium policyholders bear repricing; (c) regulator may require approval; (d) TCF issues if data usage is not clearly disclosed. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Customer acceptability of public data usage should be tested: the insurer can announce its approach or use focus groups and customer surveys to gauge acceptance. If the data is publicly available and only used at area level (not individual), acceptability risk is lower — but continued monitoring via focus groups is advisable. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Operational challenges when integrating external public data: (a) public data may be incorrect, incomplete, or biased (e.g. geographic areas skewed by care homes or unemployed populations); (b) postcode of long-term policyholders may be out of date; (c) data may have been collected for a different purpose (government funding), requiring a check on permissibility of use; (d) granularity of geographic data may limit precision or, conversely, unintentionally identify individuals when combined with age data. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Legacy systems challenges with external data: matching policyholder data to public datasets requires postcode linking across different systems; policy, claims, and lapse data may sit on separate legacy platforms; processing volume may exceed existing system capacity; data security must be ensured for any new external data stored. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Staff skill requirements for external data projects: actuarial teams may lack data science capability; hiring data scientists is expensive; an alternative is to pilot the approach using an external consultant before committing to in-house capability. [src:IFoA_0922]
fit for the intended purpose [src:IAI_1124]
Also consider: **cost** of obtaining/analysing data and **suitability of format** [src:IAI_1124]

⬡ - Post-launch performance monitoring of health products requires tracking: enrollment/market penetration, premium revenue growth, claims experience/loss ratios, customer satisfaction/retention, risk pool composition (adverse selection), and long-term sustainability. [src:IAI_1124]

⬡ - Electronic Patient Records (EPR/EHR) store a wide array of data including: demographics, insurance coverage, medical/medication/immunization history, lab/diagnostic results, treatment history, encounter information, care plans, and health behavior/lifestyle data. [src:IAI_1124]

⬡ - Advantages of introducing EPR/EHR: marketing efficiency, more complete data for pricing, timely IBNR estimation, improved clinical outcomes (reduced duplication/error), and faster claims processing. [src:IAI_1124]

⬡ - Disadvantages/Risks of EPR/EHR: low uptake, legal/regulatory privacy issues, IT security/hacking risk, high development/training costs, and potential reputational damage if systems fail or increase doctor admin burden. [src:IAI_1124]
Modelling Framework (SP1 Chapter 11)
1. Uses of Models
Enable the actuary to give appropriate advice so the insurer can be run in a sound financial way. Almost all actuarial work involves a model of some sort.

Pricing and product design; setting statutory and management account reserves; costing and reserving for options and guarantees; assessing profitability of new and existing business; analysis of surplus; embedded value calculations; determining capital requirements; assessing return on capital; assessing reinsurance needs; setting investment strategy (ALM); ongoing financial projections (revenue account, balance sheet, solvency); expense budgeting; valuing the business for merger/acquisition.
Usually a variation on a common theme: projection of the company's balance sheet and revenue account (income statement / profit and loss account).
2. Types of Model (by business scope)
Projects expected cash and profit flows from a single policy from date of issue. Used for: pricing and product design.

Projects all expected cash and profit flows arising from future sales of new business. Used for: assessing future capital requirements and overall return on capital; calculation of goodwill (appraisal value).
Projects all expected cash and profit flows from existing business at a particular time. Used for: assessing embedded value; testing solvency of existing business; analysing surplus.

Sum of new business model and existing business model. Used for: assessing impact of future management decisions on financial development; new business projections; embedded values; solvency; takeovers.
3. Objectives & Basic Features of a Health Insurance Model

A model point is a data record representing either a single policy or a group of policies, containing data on the most important policy characteristics. The number of model points is influenced by: computing power available; variability and complexity of contracts; age of the company; whether stochastic or deterministic; purpose/importance of investigation; time available; sensitivity of results to more/fewer points.

Model must be: adequately documented; easy to appreciate, communicate and display clearly; capable of independent verification for reasonableness; not overly complex (results must be interpretable and model must be cost-effective to run); capable of development and refinement; supported by a range of implementation methods for testing and parameterisation.

Must allow for all cashflows that may arise — premiums, benefits (including discretionary benefits such as options to convert, extend or increase cover without evidence of health), expenses, commission, tax.
Must also allow for cashflows arising from any supervisory requirement to hold reserves (notional cashflows — no physical exchange of money) and to maintain an adequate margin of solvency capital.

Must project cashflows separately from different states and reflect transitions between them (e.g. under IP: healthy lives paying premiums vs. disabled lives receiving benefit). Further subdivisions may be needed (e.g. different benefit levels under LTCI).

content.bold: Health insurance specific: interactions & dynamic links
content.text: Cashflows must allow for interactions between assets and liabilities. The model should be dynamic — asset and liability parts programmed to interact as they do in reality (e.g. switching to matching assets if solvency deteriorates). Dynamic links are especially vital in stochastic models.

Ability to use stochastic models and simulation must be allowed for where appropriate (e.g. to simulate distribution of claims outgo or cost of guarantees). Stochastic modelling may be more important in health and care than pure life insurance, because future incidence experience is far less predictable (benefit amounts may vary with policy-specified inflation, medical inflation, or changes in accepted medical protocols).
4. Deterministic vs Stochastic Models
Each parameter has a fixed value. Model produces a point estimate. Sensitivity testing done by re-running with different parameter values. Essentially a 'one-question-one-answer' model.

Some parameters are allowed to vary with their own distribution functions. Model must be run many times using random samples. Produces results in the form of a probability distribution. Essentially a 'distribution-in-distribution-out' model.

(1) Assessing impact of guarantees; (2) When the variable of interest has a reasonably stable and predictable probability distribution (e.g. investment returns in a stable economy); (3) Indicating effect of year-on-year volatility (random fluctuations) on risk; (4) Identifying potentially high-risk future scenarios by tracing events leading to worst simulated outcomes.

(1) Time and computing constraints — may require a simplified version of the model; (2) Sensitivity of results to the deterministically-chosen parameter values (e.g. assumed mean and variance if normal distribution used) — can lead to spurious accuracy: rubbish in = rubbish out.

Typically used for valuation purposes, especially where there are options and guarantees. Focus: replicate market prices of actual financial instruments using a risk-neutral probability measure. Parameters chosen so average PV of modelled cashflows closely matches known market prices. If the model can reproduce prices of quoted assets, it should also give market-consistent values for unquoted cashflows.

Typically used for projecting into the future (e.g. calculating capital to hold under extreme adverse scenarios at a given confidence level). Focus: assumptions reflect realistic long-term expectations and observable real-world probabilities and outcomes. Parameters determined using expectations of the future; assets and liabilities projected under each stochastic scenario. Key difference: under real-world calibration equities would be expected to outperform bonds on average; under risk-neutral calibration, both have the same average present value.
5. Sensitivity Analysis

If an adequate set of model points has been chosen, model-point error testing is usually unnecessary. However, where a reduced number of model points is used (e.g. for efficiency), the effect of a different choice of model points should be assessed.
Effect of mis-estimation of parameter values investigated by varying each parameter value and observing the impact on model output. Any correlation between parameters must be allowed for.
Sensitivity testing where several correlated parameters are changed together in a consistent way (e.g. in a low-investment-return scenario, expense inflation assumption would also be reduced).

content.bold: Parameters to sensitivity test (return on capital / profitability)
content.text: Claim inception rates; claim recovery rates; rates of transfer between claim states; mortality rates; lapse and non-renewal rates; average claim size (PMI); future expense levels; future expense inflation; future benefit/claims inflation; investment yield; volumes and mix of new business; taxation basis.

Helps determine what margins need to be incorporated into parameter values. If profitability is overly sensitive to a factor (e.g. withdrawal rates), product redesign or other measures may be needed (e.g. revise commission scales, revise reinsurance programme). Sensitivity tests should be performed at all stages of the pricing process.

Enables the actuary to quantify the effect of departures from chosen parameter values when reporting to the company. Where a probability distribution can be assigned to a parameter, the variance of profit or return on capital may be derived analytically or via Monte Carlo simulation.

Stochastic modelling may be obligatory for supervisory reserve calculations in some territories. Sensitivity analysis can also help determine appropriate risk margins, global reserves or capital requirements.
6. Equation of Value / Formula Approach

Based on the equivalence principle: income must equal outgo (or exceed it, with surplus going to capital providers). Premium is derived by equating discounted income (premiums + investment income) against discounted outgo (claims, expenses, commission, tax). An explicit profit allowance may also be included.

Equivalent to the return achievable by investing policy proceeds as they arise. May vary between optimistic (higher rate → cheaper premium) and conservative depending on whether result needs to be competitive. Tax and other outgo can be allowed for by adjusting the discount rate.

Value of claims (discounted future claims outgo, including claims handling expenses and inflation); Value of premiums (discounted income net of initial/renewal commission, allowing for escalation, persistency, premium waiver); Value of expenses (non-premium-related expenses, discounted with inflation); Value of investment income (timing differences between receipts and outgo within the year).

(1) Does not allow for proper timing of events; (2) Reserves are ignored completely; (3) Capital needs cannot be reflected (desired shareholder return cannot be incorporated); (4) Net negative cashflows in any period are implicitly assumed to be borrowable at the discount rate; (5) Cannot separately inspect premium-related or claim-related cashflows by year; (6) Cannot easily vary assumptions over time (one fixed discount rate used); (7) Cannot easily measure sensitivity of profit to changes in assumptions; (8) Cannot handle more complex product structures (e.g. unit-linked).

Most drawbacks are only significant for long-term contracts. Formula approach is useful and commonly used for pricing short-term (annually renewable) contracts (e.g. PMI), where investment income is relatively insignificant and reserving issues are minor.
7. Cashflow Approach

Developed to overcome the drawbacks of the formula approach. All health and care insurance models include some element of cashflow and profit projection. Can be deterministic or stochastic. For products with complex features (e.g. options), stochastic cashflow modelling may be required. Each significant item of cashflow is projected allowing for interactions.

Model points chosen to represent expected new business. For each model point, cashflows are projected (including reserving and solvency capital requirements) using base parameter values, then discounted at the risk discount rate to calculate net present value of future profits. Premiums/charges are set to meet the company's profit criterion — either for each model point individually, or in aggregate across model points (accepting some cross-subsidy). Once acceptable premiums are determined for model points, rates for all contract variations are derived (e.g. by interpolation).
Premiums; expenses (initial, renewal, claim); commission; claims; contribution to reserves; contribution to capital requirements; interest on cashflows and reserves; tax.

Cost of unit allocations; bid-offer spread; cost of paying claims in excess of unit fund value; risk charges (morbidity/mortality); unit charges (initial charges, policy fees); fund management charges; unit fund growth; unit funds paid out on claim or surrender.

Net projected cashflows are discounted at the risk discount rate, which takes into account: the return required by the company; the level of statistical risk attaching to the cashflows. A separate rate should theoretically apply to each cashflow component, but in practice a single rate is used for simplicity. The rate used for existing business assessment is typically lower than for pricing new business (some risks, e.g. new business volume/mix, are reduced).

content.bold: Cashflow approach — assessing profitability of existing business
content.text: Full policy data set or representative model points may be used. Present value of projected cashflows calculated for each policy/model point. Results scaled up and totalled to give expected present value of future profits from existing business (the 'value of in-force business'). Main use: determining the insurer's embedded value. Alternative uses: assessing relative profitability by product/distribution channel; identifying products needing repricing.

Defined as the value of the future profit stream from existing business together with the value of any net assets separately attributable to shareholders. Regarded as the best measure of an insurer's real worth. For sale purposes, goodwill (value of future new business) is added to give the appraisal value.

Net cashflows for model points are grossed up for expected new business to assess the capital required to write the product (on regulatory or economic capital basis). One-off development costs can be added. Total capital requirement compared with expected profits to determine expected return on capital.

Full model office used to determine capital required to write new business. Net cashflows from new business model points (scaled up for expected new business) incorporated into a model of the whole company. Actuary assesses timing and amount of capital requirement; may lead to product redesign to reduce financing needs.

Now the most widely used approach for long-term insurance pricing. Not widely used for PMI (one-year term) as the formula approach is sufficient. For short-term products, cashflow techniques may still be used to: spread high initial expenses over expected renewals; assess adequacy of premiums over a longer time horizon (e.g. 10-year projection with assumptions for renewal rates and premium increases).
8. Multi-State Modelling

Used where policyholders can exist in different states, each associated with different cashflows. Particularly relevant for IP insurance and LTCI where a claim does not terminate the policy (at minimum: claiming and not claiming states exist).

Healthy; Sick but not yet claiming (within deferred period); Sick and claiming; Lapsed; Dead. In practice, the 'sick but not claiming' state is often omitted and the deferred period handled by explicit adjustments, leaving: Healthy; Sick (claiming); Lapsed; Dead.

Written as μ_x^{ij} = transition intensity at exact age x from state i to state j (i ≠ j). Represent the annual rate at which lives are moving between states. Applied to each status each month to determine numbers in each state for the next period.

Requires determination of the proportion of lives in each status using duration-based transition intensities. Value of claims outgo = number of lives in benefit-receiving sub-cohorts × relevant average sum insured. Balanced against: premiums from premium-paying state + investment income − expenses and other outgoings. Premium calculated by equating EPV of claims and EPV of premiums.

Similar tools apply for reserving and reporting. Assumptions underpinning transition intensities must be adjusted according to the purpose (e.g. statutory reserving assumptions may differ from those used in a company sale valuation).

Model accuracy improves with finer sub-cohort divisions (e.g. by gender, sickness duration, claims history). However, this quickly leads to large numbers of sub-cohorts and data requirements that cannot be met reliably — risk of spurious accuracy. In practice: sub-cohorts are combined and fewer but more credible transition intensities are used. The goal is to avoid results being heavily dependent on parameters with very little credibility.

Amount of credible data is a key factor in deciding whether to use multi-state modelling. Example: a health and care insurer might use multi-state modelling for individual IP (reasonable data) but the inception/disabled annuity approach for group IP (insufficient data for multi-state). The value of sensitivity testing as a tool should never be underestimated — even without confidence in a single projected outcome, the effect of varying parameter assumptions is extremely illuminating.
9. Main Components of Common Models (Appendix)

Takes model points (or full policy data) and parameter values as input. Two key functions: (1) Projects forward the in-force portfolio to end of each future projection year (function of: existing in-force portfolio; projected new business; future state transitions; future mortality, lapse and non-renewal rates; policy expiries) — needed for supervisory reserves and solvency capital calculation; (2) Projects total policy cashflows each year — used with asset model to calculate projected year-end assets and annual profit.

Full model office: calculates total expected expenses of the insurance company. Single policy/new/existing business models: restricted to expenses related to those parts of the business modelled. Fixed overhead expenses are difficult to model for profit test and new business models; for the full model office they should be modelled globally.

Complexity varies with model type and purpose. Profit test (simplest): future total annual expected investment return only. Full model office assessing investment strategy (most complex): stochastic asset model producing a distribution of returns for each asset class, projecting income and gains separately, reflecting the changing asset mix. Asset and liability models must interact consistently (e.g. inflation-linked benefits and inflation-linked investment returns must be handled on a consistent basis).

Outputs of the policy liability model, expense model and asset model are consolidated (added up) at a higher level. Allows for factors not in the detailed model (e.g. adjustments for un-modelled business, tax, or separately determined contingency reserves). Results verified by comparison with: supervisory valuation; previous model results; and highly simplified model results.
Group Health & Care Insurance Framework (SP1 Chapter 5)
Definition & Structure
Collection of individuals combining to make single proposal for uniform insurance cover.
(1) Eligibility definition, (2) Clearly defined benefits by size/definition/period, (3) Uniform cover terms.
Employer; often pays part/all premium. Can be affinity group, club membership, credit card linked.
Purposes of Group Insurance
In some territories, IP insurance compulsory for employers.
Positive message to prospective/existing employees; competitive benefit.
Can appeal to all employees or selected groups (e.g., managers only).
Encourages health management; facilitates speedy return following operation.
IP insurance mechanism for managing early retirement of unhealthy employees.
Employer contribution may be tax-deductible; employee benefit may have tax-favourable treatment.
Benefit perceived as worth more to employee than cost to employer (tax efficiency).
Flexible Benefit Schemes (Cafeteria Benefits)
Core benefits + employee choice from menu of alternatives (some requiring staff contribution).
Employees select benefits matching personal needs; greater flexibility.
Anti-selection risk (members choose benefits they expect to claim); increased administration; difficulty determining basic risk costs.
Depend on benefit type and jurisdiction; some benefits treated as taxable salary.
Underwriting & Acceptance
Underwriter assesses group on average; some poor health lives accepted if balanced by excellent health lives.
Group's experience used to derive premiums if sufficient volume credibility; can accept lives without significant portfolio changes.
May assess IP/PMI/CI claims in payment or about to be paid (liability pass to new insurer); critical for scheme takeovers.
When employer moves group to new insurer, new insurer may assume current + future liabilities; detailed assessment of current claims needed.

⬡ - For innovative aggregate-trigger group products (where payout depends on scheme-wide morbidity exceeding a threshold): employer benefits include ease of purchase and no individual underwriting; key risks to employer are short-term contract uncertainty, mismatch between payout and actual sick-pay liability, and moral hazard. [src:IAI_0523]
Anti-Selection Controls
Applying exclusions
Setting free cover limits (IP/CI only, not PMI)
Ensuring 'actively at work' qualification
Setting take-up rates on voluntary schemes
Laying down take-over terms from previous insurer
Minimum member numbers, compulsory membership, benefit formula linked to salary. Result: more generous free cover limits, smaller premium loadings.
Anti-selection typically lower for groups than individuals; depends on acceptance rules and flexible benefit structure.

⬡ - For aggregate-trigger products, employers have strong incentive to select adversely (buying only when they expect a payout). Controls include: requiring employee name lists (not just counts), restricting eligible industries/geographies, ongoing monitoring with right to revise trigger percentages, and using reinsurance if available. [src:IAI_0523]
Free Cover (Non-Selection Limits)
Amount of cover automatically granted without underwriting to group members. Only those exceeding limit need medical underwriting.
Group IP and CI only (not PMI, as indemnity-based benefit amount unknown until claim).
Reduce UW cost; avoid inconvenience of mass medical screening.
Typically for large groups (sufficient risk units that no individual is significant); compulsory membership usual requirement.
Aimed to enable most lives in most schemes to be accepted without UW, provided compulsory membership. Higher-paid employees likely exceed limit (need individual UW).
Lower free cover levels on smaller schemes (avoid single member carrying large proportion of total risk); larger schemes support higher limits.
Vital to success of non-underwritten cover. Employer recruitment process achieves health screening rather than explicit insurer UW.
Example: Employee must have worked continuously preceding 2 months at cover start.
Special rules for members failing 'actively at work' qualification (off sick); can return to scheme once requirements re-met.
Group IP Insurance Specifics (Chapter 5)
Gross salary basis (simpler admin) or net pay basis (up to 90% net pre-disability income).
Employee/employer pension contributions, State welfare contributions.
While in claim, benefits escalate to maintain claimant's standard of living.
Employees leaving employment can convert to individual cover without health evidence; limited to group benefit levels. Anti-selection risk high.
Employers insure liability to pay statutory sick pay (e.g., UK: 4 days–28 weeks). Common 28-week DP matches statutory period.
Group IP can cover sick pay after statutory period; employer discretionary top-up.
Group CI Insurance Specifics
Appropriate (members have varying sum insured, usually salary multiple; some over limit need UW).
Usually salary-based multiples (e.g., 2-5x salary).
Compulsory for free cover schemes.
Group PMI Specifics
NOT applicable (indemnity-based; actual benefit unknown until claim). Concept of FCL does not work.
Budget vs comprehensive cover; comprehensive underwritten, budget not. Not called 'free cover' in practice.
Renewal terms can be rate-at-risk (each group renewed at own experience) or pooled (experience spreads across groups).
Groups 1000+ offer scale benefits; better pricing, more flexibility.
Pricing Methodology (Group Products)
Based on group's past claims experience; more credible with size.
Groups without sufficient experience use community (territory/occupation) rates.
1 or 3-year guarantees typical; premium fixed during guarantee period.
IRDAI 2024: renewal loadings applied at portfolio level, not individual policy level.
Distribution & Administration
Sponsor; may facilitate payroll deduction; communicates to employees.
Varies by scheme structure; free cover reduces UW burden.
Can support return-to-work initiatives; employer partnership on rehabilitation.
Aggregate / Index-Trigger Group Products

⬡ - An aggregate-trigger (index-trigger) group IP product pays the employer a lump sum when the overall morbidity of the covered pool exceeds a defined threshold (e.g. total sick days across all registered employees). [src:IAI_0523]

⬡ - Employer benefits: fast and simple to buy (no named employee list), no individual underwriting, scalable (adjustable headcount), and potential windfall payment in bad years. Risks: short-term contract (not guaranteed renewal), payout not linked to actual sick-pay cost, and exposure to pooled experience of other employers. [src:IAI_0523]

⬡ - Insurer risks: legal uncertainty around novel design, difficulty defining the trigger percentage appropriately, moral hazard (employers manipulating sickness figures), anti-selection (employers buying only when expecting a claim), adverse mix by industry/occupation, and potential inability to obtain reinsurance. [src:IAI_0523]

⬡ - Insurer controls: require named employee lists (not just counts), define trigger and claim terms robustly, restrict eligible industry sectors/geographies, carry out ongoing verification, monitor experience closely, and review trigger percentage at renewal. [src:IAI_0523]

⬡ - When expanding from group IP into additional health lines within the same market: group CI and hospital cash plans are relatively easier to launch (simpler products, lower data requirements, similar distribution); PMI is more complex, requiring hospital/provider networks, managed care infrastructure, and cashless processing capability. [src:IFoA_0425]
Self-Insured / In-House Employer Schemes

⬡ - Employers may close in-house (self-insured) medical schemes due to: claims costs higher than budgeted (driven by moral hazard or medical inflation), higher-than-expected administration costs, inability to source or renew reinsurance, or a strategic decision to exit insurance risk entirely. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Additional triggers for closing an in-house scheme: increased regulatory compliance burden, adverse change in tax treatment, desire to offer employees greater plan choice, difficulty managing claim declines for own employees (industrial relations sensitivity), or shareholder pressure to reduce non-core costs. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - A subsidised or fully-funded employer scheme whose cost is not visible to employees may be under-valued by staff, reducing the perceived benefit and motivating the employer to restructure delivery via an insurer. [src:IFoA_0425]
Scheme Transitions & Stakeholder Management

⬡ - Key stakeholders in an employer scheme-to-insurer transition: employees and family members, the incoming insurer (and its reinsurer), the medical schemes regulator, care partners (hospitals, managed care providers, emergency services), actuarial consultants and scheme auditors, the employer's HR department, and staff employed in running the current scheme. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Employees may perceive a transition from an employer scheme to insurer as a loss of a previously 'free' benefit; salary restructuring may be required to maintain take-home pay and avoid staff relations issues. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Staff with larger families, pre-existing conditions, or chronic illness face the greatest risk from transition: moratorium underwriting now applies, cross-subsidies from the closed employer scheme disappear, and insurer premiums may be higher due to open-market anti-selection exposure. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Staff may lose influence over benefit design and pricing under an insurer scheme. Conversely, they gain: a wider plan choice, potential for added benefits (dental, optical), insurer economies of scale, and removal of restrictions on when family members can join. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Employer mitigation actions at transition: (a) grandfather existing members on the old scheme while new staff join the insurer plan, (b) increase salaries to offset new medical costs, (c) offer a subsidy for the insured plan, (d) make benefits visible via payroll without reducing net pay, (e) negotiate a plan that matches existing benefits as closely as possible, (f) negotiate bulk acceptance without underwriting for the initial transition cohort. [src:IFoA_0425]
Managing Group PMI Costs

⬡ - When an employer seeks to reduce group PMI costs, the approach depends on whether premiums are effectively employee-funded (cost-to-company basis) or employer-funded; in either case, the employer must balance cost reduction against impact on staff retention and industrial relations. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Options for reducing group PMI costs: (a) remove PMI for new entrants only, (b) reduce benefits or increase deductibles, (c) introduce more plan tiers giving employees flexibility to self-select lower cover, (d) renegotiate the contract with the existing insurer, (e) rebroke the arrangement to another insurer (often with specialist benefits consultants), (f) increase employee contributions via payroll deduction. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Stopping PMI benefits entirely risks significant staff turnover — particularly the loss of high earners who can obtain PMI cover via a new employer. Offering salary increases in lieu of PMI may not be cost-effective if group purchasing provides material economies of scale. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Tax considerations must be assessed when restructuring group PMI: shifting from employer-funded PMI benefits to salary augmentation may create adverse tax consequences for employees in some jurisdictions. [src:IFoA_0425]
Investment & ALM Framework (SP1 Chapter 28)
Overview & Importance
Investment strategy affects commercial success, financial results and security of the insurer.
Most health products are protection products with low reserves — investment returns far less important for these.
Pre-funded LTCI has high reserves — investment returns of considerable importance.
Higher returns benefit shareholders (higher profits) and/or policyholders (lower premiums). Unit-linked policyholders also benefit from good asset performance in the unit fund.
High return assets are more volatile. Insurer must balance return vs security given the nature of its liabilities.
Asset Characteristics (Major Types)
Nominal return guaranteed if held to redemption. Most secure asset type (after cash). Most marketable; very low dealing costs. Market value fluctuates but redemption value unaffected.
Coupon linked to a price index. Smaller issuance than conventional bonds — slightly less marketable. Best match for index-linked liabilities.

Higher expected return than government bonds of equal term (reflects default risk and lower marketability). Security can be a problem for low credit-rated issuers. Marketability often poor; dealing costs high.

Dividend income and capital value both expected to grow in real terms. Income yields low (due to growth expectation). Highly volatile — problem if dividends used to meet liability outgo or market value used for solvency demonstration. Highly marketable in many markets; overseas equities an option where local market is small.

High expected return; low income yield but grows in real terms. Secure but rental income can be interrupted; market value varies significantly. Very illiquid; high dealing and maintenance costs. Long-term investment; not practical to buy and sell quickly. Valuation difficult — each property is unique.
Most secure; least variable. Highly liquid; negligible dealing costs. Lowest expected return.
Principles of Investment
Select investments appropriate to the nature, term and currency of the liabilities.
Maximise overall return on assets (income + capital gains).
Extent of departing from Principle 1 to achieve Principle 2 depends on the company's free assets and risk appetite.
Maximise return subject to an acceptable level of risk, given financial resources available.
Asset-Liability Matching — Nature of Liabilities
each requires a different matching strategy:

*Guaranteed in monetary terms* (e.g. fixed CI lump sum): Match with government bonds (immunisation); zero-coupon bonds if available. Problem: liability outgo rarely known with certainty; asset terms often shorter than liabilities.

*Guaranteed in terms of an index* (e.g. IP benefit linked to earnings/prices): Ideal match is index-linked bonds on the same index. Alternative: real assets (equities/property) as substitute — not a perfect match due to volatility.

*Indemnity* (e.g. PMI): Short-tail business; low reserves; cash and short-term fixed interest bonds appropriate. Longer-tail provisions (known future treatment costs) matched by real assets expected to give real return.
*Investment-linked* (e.g. unit-linked LTCI): Invest in the same assets underlying the investment link. Normal practice; deliberate mismatch is possible but loss falls on insurer not policyholders.
Treated as index-linked (move broadly with price/earnings inflation).
Treated as negative benefit payments — fixed monetary or index-linked.
Theoretical concept; usually neither achievable nor desirable (eliminates investment profit). Desirable only if free assets are very low and insolvency risk is unacceptably high.

⬡ - For IP liabilities, benefit payments may increase with salary inflation; for CI/death benefits, liabilities are fixed in money terms. The asset strategy should reflect this distinction — index-linked assets for escalating benefits, fixed-income for guaranteed amounts. [src:IAI_0523]
ALM — Term and Currency
Match asset proceeds to timing of liability outgo — assets available at the right times.
Liabilities in a given currency should be matched by assets in the same currency. Currency options or hedging can manage any mismatch from overseas asset investment.
Free Assets and Investment Freedom
Free assets act as a cushion — allow insurer to depart from a matched position and invest in higher-returning (riskier) assets.
Greater free assets → greater freedom in asset selection → more variability of return can be accepted.
Free assets themselves back capital obligations — typically invested in higher-yielding assets.
Mismatch of investment-linked liabilities is possible using free assets but risky: if chosen assets underperform the linked assets, the loss falls on the insurer not policyholders.

⬡ - When the solvency ratio is marginally above the regulatory minimum, cash and short-dated gilts are preferred to reduce market risk. When the surplus is substantial, more return-seeking assets (equities) may be appropriate for the excess over the regulatory minimum. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - For ULIP-linked business, the RSM moves with the market value of unit fund assets. The shareholder fund backing the ASM should hold matching equity assets so that RSM and ASM move together, preserving the solvency ratio through market cycles. [src:IAI_0722]
Regulatory Constraints on Investment
Restrictions on types of assets (e.g. equities, property, derivatives may be limited or forbidden).
Restrictions on amounts of asset types counting toward demonstrating solvency (e.g. equities capped at 25% of assets).
Restrictions on maximum exposure to a single counterparty or country.
Requirement to hold a proportion of assets in a specific class (e.g. government stock).
Requirement to match assets and liabilities by currency.
Requirement to hold a mismatching reserve (higher mismatch → higher reserve → lower free assets).
Limits on extent of mismatching allowed.
Restrictions on valuation method used (e.g. book value vs market value).
Custodianship restrictions on who may hold assets.
Inflation and Investment Strategy

⬡ - In a high-inflation environment, market reaction may reduce the attractiveness of existing asset classes and compress real yields; inflation-linked bonds may become expensive or illiquid as demand rises. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Inflation swaps can be used to limit extreme inflation exposure risk, but their cost increases in a high-inflation environment — the cost-benefit of hedging must be carefully assessed against the cost of holding matching assets. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - IP liabilities with unknown benefit amounts and unknown timing and duration are harder to match with assets in a high-inflation environment than fixed CI lump sums — this is a reason to prefer writing more CI or short-term products during prolonged inflationary periods. [src:IFoA_0924]
Developing Investment Strategy — Model Office Approach
1. Allocate a portion of free assets to support the assets underlying the reserves.

2. Project assets and liabilities forward using ALM model (stochastic investment model for asset paths; inflation model for liabilities). Ensure consistency between inflation, interest rates and market values.
3. Check excess of assets over liabilities exceeds solvency capital requirement throughout projection period for most scenarios (e.g. 99% of model runs).
4. Calculate aggregate profitability measure (e.g. shareholder distributions for a proprietary company).
5. Repeat for different investment strategies until target insolvency probability is achieved.
6. Identify strategy with equal insolvency risk that produces the highest profitability.

Model risk (conclusions only as valid as the model/parameterisation); using only a portion of free assets may be limiting; solvency capital requirement may not be constant and should ideally be modelled dynamically.
Take current strategy + actual free assets → estimate probability of insolvency.
Other Practical Considerations

If claims fluctuate widely or insurer is in run-off, maintain ready liquid funds. Reinsurance can reduce liquidity requirement but gross claims may need to be paid before reinsurance recoveries — creates tension with matching strategy.

Assess against (a) targets for overall return, (b) riskiness of strategy undertaken, (c) performance of peers with similar briefs. Assess over several periods to eliminate luck. External managers require even more careful monitoring.
Higher investment return → lower premiums or additional product features at the same price. More significant for longer-term contracts.

Two approaches — (1) Discounted cashflow (consistent with liability valuation basis); (2) Market value (objective; now the norm in many territories). PMI (short-term liabilities): cash valued at market price. Long-term liabilities: either method applicable depending on purpose and prudence required.

Policyholders of reviewable fixed-benefit products (e.g. reviewable CI) do not expect premium increases driven by insurer's investment underperformance — raises TCF concerns. Exception: unit-linked products where benefit/premium explicitly depends on investment performance.
Liquidity Considerations
⬡ - A rapidly growing insurer can use incoming premiums to meet claims in the short term (natural liquidity), but must hold liquid reserves for future strain as the book matures. [src:IAI_0523]

⬡ - Liquidity needs increase with claim volatility. Where OP claims are high-frequency/unpredictable, a larger liquid buffer (short-dated gilts or cash) is required than for low-frequency IP/CI claims. [src:IAI_0523]

⬡ - Capital requirements (ASM backing) should be held in low-risk liquid assets to minimise exposure to market risk on the regulatory minimum. Excess free surplus may be invested more aggressively. [src:IAI_0523,IAI_0722]

⬡ - Rising gilt prices (a flight-to-safety during recession) reduce gilt yields, which in turn reduces discount rates and increases the value of long-duration liabilities — individual CI policy liabilities are more sensitive to this than short-term group CI liabilities. If the insurer mismatches assets and liabilities it may be partially insulated, but at the cost of greater basis risk. [src:IFoA_0422]

⬡ - When large-scale policy lapses reduce premium income, the investment policy must adapt: short-term liquidity becomes critical (forced asset sales at recession prices may be unavoidable); liability duration shortens as longer-duration policies are disproportionately retained; matching assets should be reviewed to reflect the changed liability profile; and appetite for risk should be reassessed given reduced free assets and market volatility. [src:IFoA_0422]
Professional Actuarial Duties
Following APS/TAS (e.g. TAS 100), ensuring advice is clear, comprehensive, and unbiased.
Statutory duties (Chief Actuary / With-Profits Actuary), Peer review requirements.
Whistleblowing, managing conflicts of interest, handling data quality issues responsibly.
Independent Expert in Portfolio Transfer

⬡ - An independent expert (IE) is required by regulators in many jurisdictions when insurance liabilities are transferred between companies. The IE's role is to provide an independent opinion on whether the interests and reasonable benefit expectations of all affected policyholders are adequately protected by the proposed transfer. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - The IE must be approved by the relevant regulator and owes their primary duty to the court or regulatory body approving the transfer — not to either of the insurers involved. The IE will consult actuaries at both the transferring and receiving insurers and engage with both home and host country regulators where a cross-border transfer is involved. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - The IE's report considers the likely impact of the transfer on: (a) transferring policyholders (benefit security, continuity, terms of cover); (b) remaining policyholders in the transferring insurer if only part of the portfolio is moved; (c) existing policyholders of the receiving insurer (dilution of security, management capacity). [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Key IE concerns: security of policyholder benefits and benefit expectations; solvency and capital adequacy of the receiving entity post-transfer; whether the regulatory framework of the receiving jurisdiction is at least as protective as the current one; and broader TCF considerations including servicing standards and claims handling. [src:IFoA_0922]
Capital Assessment
Role of Capital & Solvency II
Provide confidence to policyholders and regulators, buffer against shocks.
Pillar 1 (Quantitative: BEL, Risk Margin, SCR), Pillar 2 (Qualitative: ORSA, Governance), Pillar 3 (Disclosure).
Capital Management & Solvency Ratio
Optimizing capital via Reinsurance or ALM, Return on Capital (ROC) thresholds.
⬡ - Impact on solvency of new market entry: short-term deterioration from start-up costs, new business strain, and capital requirements; long-term improvement if business is profitable.
⬡ - Solvency Ratio = ASM / RSM. A small change in SR ≈ ΔASM/RSM − SR × ΔRSM/RSM. This formula shows SR is more sensitive to changes in RSM than ASM when SR > 1.
Private Medical Insurance (PMI) Framework (SP1 Chapter 4)
Definition & Nature
Indemnity-based product reimbursing cost of private medical treatment. Short-term, annual renewable.
Fixed benefit/cash plan (insurer knows max exposure; policyholder bears cost variation).
Health cash plans, dental/optical plans, major medical expenses, waiting-list plans.
Product Scope & Coverage
(must be explicitly defined): Inpatient (IP), Outpatient (OP), Day care, Domiciliary, AYUSH (all systems equal per IRDAI 2024).
OP cover has much higher frequency/lower severity than IP. Major operational readiness required before OP launch (high-volume, low-value processing).
Life insurers in India restricted to benefit basis only; general/health insurers offer both.
Key Product Features

Offered as cumulative bonus (increased SI without premium increase) or renewal premium discount. Policyholder chooses at renewal. Self-selection by healthy lives; pricing must account for selection benefit.
Applies to new illnesses only (not pre-existing); annual or per-claim basis. Critical design choice affecting benefit adequacy/claim frequency.
Room/board limits, surgery limits, ICU limits, consultant limits, organ transplant limits. Room limits incentivize cost-effective hospitals.
Cost-sharing (reduce moral hazard; increase policyholder engagement). Must be clearly disclosed in CIS.
Second opinion, home nursing, day-care procedures, domiciliary care, ambulance, pre-authorisation (mandatory 1-hour TAT under IRDAI 2024).
(IRDAI 2024 limits):
PED waiting period: max 36 months continuous coverage
Disease-specific waiting period: max 36 months
General waiting period: typically 30 days (acute conditions)
No waiting on accident-related claims (cash plans)
IRDAI 2024 mandated; difficult to diagnose/control; higher fraud risk.
Underwriting & Anti-Selection
Reduces adverse selection; high admin cost. Balance evidence cost vs experience benefit.
Simpler but risks claim disputes post-60-month moratorium (non-contestable except fraud).
Cross-subsidy across ages; requires open enrolment + risk equalisation to prevent adverse selection spiral.
Intermediate approach; balance community spirit with experience-rated reality.
Attracts adverse selection; if first-mover without premium adjustment, competitors cherry-pick healthier lives.
