Detailed medical assessment at proposal; clearer policy position; explicit exclusions avoid later disputes.
Waiting periods, PED exclusions, pre-authorisation (controls moral hazard, ensures care pathway). [src:IFoA_0922]
Pricing Framework
Key risk (~12-15% p.a. in India historically; varies by treatment type). Super-inflation trend observed. Failure to trend forward leads to rapid deterioration.
Frequency × Severity; split by IP/OP, age, gender, duration, UW method, channel.
Adverse mix, anti-selection, moral hazard, medical inflation, new treatments, poor persistency, aggressive initial pricing.
Decompose frequency/severity/mix vs assumptions; sensitivity test; care to avoid adverse selection spiral.
Healthy lives selecting NCD are better risks; model take-up, selection benefit, behavioural response.
Annual repricing essential but risks selective lapsing if competitor pricing lags; competitor response analysis critical.

⬡ - When deciding whether to reprice immediately for an emerging risk (e.g. climate/flood): consider whether claims experience will deteriorate immediately or gradually; weigh profitability risk of not repricing against competitive and lapse risk of repricing ahead of the market; assess solvency position; note that PMI's annual renewability reduces urgency vs long-term products since rates can be adjusted at next renewal. [src:IFoA_0923]
Claims Management & Cost Control
Controls moral hazard; ensures care pathway. Mandatory 1-hour TAT (IRDAI 2024).
Agreed tariffs; cashless settlement. 100% cashless target (IRDAI 2024).
Capitation (fixed per covered life; provider bears utilisation risk) harder to negotiate except OP/dental. Fee-for-service (insurer bears utilisation risk). Bundled payment (procedure bundles).
Day clinics, second opinion (reduces unnecessary procedures), benefit limits (room, surgery, chronic), chronic disease management, wellness incentives.
Characteristic of PMI; strict requirement (no claim repudiated without PMC approval or 3-member CRC; reason must cite specific policy terms).
Robust high-volume, low-value systems essential. Different underwriting/settlement approach vs IP (lump sum vs multiple small claims).
Market Dynamics & Distribution
No UW/claims expertise, competitive pricing pressure, high fixed expenses, pricing uncertainty, adverse selection spiral risk.
Split by channel, age, gender, UW method, duration. A/E on frequency/severity/persistency; expense analysis; check UW standards; review reinsurer recoveries.
Revise UW, expense control, redesign benefits, reprice, improve claims control, change target market, run-off or sale.
Attracts younger, healthier lives; different risk profile than traditional broker/agent channels. [src:IFoA_0922]
Regulatory concerns; IRDAI compliance required; transparency/fairness challenges.
FCL avoids individual UW; rate-at-risk vs pooled renewal; large groups (1000+) offer scale benefits.
Adverse Claims Experience — Causes & Remediation

⬡ - PMI claims can deteriorate due to: business mix shifts (older or higher-risk lives, new distribution channels), lapses by healthier lives, higher-than-expected medical inflation, new/more expensive treatment protocols, large individual claims (cancer, medical evacuation), lax claims management, provider price increases, reduction in state healthcare quality driving demand to private sector, or random fluctuation. [src:IFoA_0422]

⬡ - Actions to reduce PMI A/E deterioration include: add pricing margins for anti-selection; tighten claims management (GP referral requirements); review benefit utilisation and introduce or increase deductibles/co-payments; tighten underwriting; diversify business mix toward healthier segments; incentivise healthy behaviours; negotiate fixed-fee schedules with providers; introduce managed care for chronic/high-risk patients; review policy wording to limit unexpected claims; and consider reinsurance. [src:IFoA_0422]
Capitation vs Fee-for-Service

⬡ - Under capitation, the insurer pays a fixed annual amount per covered life to a provider network, transferring utilisation risk to the provider — this provides cost certainty for the insurer and predictable income for the provider, but incentivises the provider to reduce care quality to maximise profit and creates counterparty risk if the provider cannot meet demand. [src:IFoA_0422]

⬡ - Risks of a capitation arrangement for the insurer include: provider reputation damage reflecting on insurer; policyholders losing choice of hospital; poor-quality data from the provider impairing future pricing; regulatory/competition concerns; provider capacity constraints; and the arrangement costing more than fee-for-service if the provider over-estimates the capitation fee. Mitigants: due diligence, regular reviews, carve-outs for high-cost/complex procedures, co-payment options for alternative hospitals, and monthly rather than annual fee payments to reduce counterparty risk. [src:IFoA_0422]
Government Subsidy Schemes

⬡ - A means-tested government subsidy paid directly to healthcare providers for low-income patients can expand healthcare access without requiring those patients to buy insurance — but risks reducing demand for HCP and lower-tier PMI products, causing selective lapses among subsidised segments and adverse volume/expense impacts for insurers targeting that market. [src:IFoA_0423]

⬡ - Limiting a government healthcare subsidy to inpatient-only treatment allows the government to: target the highest-cost needs, limit fiscal exposure, contract with a manageable number of hospital providers, standardise fees for set procedures, and rely on more readily available cost data — outpatient subsidies are harder to administer, more prone to fraud, and have a much broader and less predictable cost base. [src:IFoA_0423]
India-Specific Context (IRDAI 2024)

All credits (SI, NCB, waiting periods, moratorium) transfer to new insurer; 72-hour data transfer via IIB. Acquiring insurer decides within 5 days. Alters experience significantly (waiting periods do not re-apply).
Can offer both indemnity and benefit products (life insurers: benefit basis only).
Mandated at par with physical illness; difficult to diagnose, fraud risk, higher frequency.
Ayushman Bharat covers bottom 40% with basic indemnity. Private PMI fills gap for middle/upper income: higher sum, wider choice, faster settlement.
Improves UW and fraud detection; voluntary; uptake low; data privacy risk (DPDP 2023).
Repricing risks selective lapsing but essential; climate risks affecting healthcare costs.
Detailed CIS, 30-day Free Look, mandatory 15-day grievance resolution, Board-governed commission, TCF principles, Ombudsman interface, 1-hr/3-hr TAT, no claim denial without CRC approval.
Related Products
Cover only when public healthcare cannot provide treatment within specified period (e.g., 6 weeks). Meets customer need to avoid waiting.
Defined-benefit, defined-premium; low premium (~$2/week); specific payouts for dental, optical, physio, maternity, hospitalisation, recuperation, hearing aids, consultation.
Lump-sum benefit on surgery (fixed schedule by procedure class/severity); less than indemnity premium. UK example: covers in-patient costs + incidentals. Does not cover out-patient.
Outpatient (OP) Cover — Design & Pricing

⬡ - OP cover is characterised by high claim frequency and low claim severity, the opposite of IP. This creates different risks: greater scope for fraud/waste/abuse, higher claims administration costs, and more complex seasonality (e.g. dental/optical in Q4, health screens in Q1). [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - OP product design principles: start with minimal benefits and significant co-pay/deductible; restrict to GP-referred specialist consultations to control abuse; exclude cosmetic treatments; define OP explicitly to avoid overlap with day-surgery IP claims. [src:IAI_0722]
⬡ - Consider no-claim bonuses (premium reduction at renewal) as a differentiator and fraud deterrent. Health check-up packages can enhance appeal and reduce long-term morbidity. [src:IAI_0722]
⬡ - Dental, optical and maternity costs must be priced separately — they have distinct frequency/severity profiles and require sub-limits or separate benefit schedules. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - OP reserving requires care over: seasonality in claim patterns, shorter and different reporting lag (most OP claims have no pre-authorisation), IBNER which differs from IP (initial pre-auth estimates exist for IP but not OP), and system capacity for large claim volumes. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - OP reinsurance: catastrophe XL is less relevant (claims are high-frequency/low-severity). Quota share is more appropriate — it transfers volatility and volume risk and may give underwriting capacity/expertise from the reinsurer. [src:IAI_0722]

⬡ - Financial advisor distribution introduces churning risk: advisors may move policyholders to competing insurers to earn fresh commission, causing selective lapses and reducing portfolio persistency. Commission clawback terms are the primary mitigant. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - IFA-distributed business competes on both price and policy terms/conditions — not on price alone. A new entrant could disrupt existing business by offering more comprehensive terms or a broader conditions list, even at a similar premium. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - If the government introduces free public healthcare provision, the market for short-term products (MME, PMI) may contract sharply; insurers concentrated in these products face mass lapse risk and loss of new business. Mitigation: product diversification and monitoring of policy developments. [src:IFoA_0922]
Indemnity-based product reimbursing cost of private medical treatment. Short-term, annual renewable. [src:IAI_1124]
Fixed benefit/cash plan (insurer knows max exposure; policyholder bears cost variation). [src:IAI_1124]
Annual repricing essential but risks selective lapsing if competitor pricing lags; competitor response analysis critical. [src:IAI_1124]

⬡ - Uncertainty in estimating PMI claims for new territories arises from: lifestyle/morbidity prevalence differences, idiosyncratic demographics (e.g. aging), medical innovation/technology costs, policyholder/provider behavior, claims control/fraud effectiveness, and macro-economic factors like currency fluctuation. [src:IAI_1124]
Critical Illness (CI) Insurance Framework (SP1 Chapter 2)
Definition & Nature
Dread disease insurance, serious illness insurance, crisis cash, living assurance. All refer to critical illness insurance (CI).
Lump sum payment typically payable on diagnosis of defined conditions. Payment can be in instalments (reduces fraud risk) or used to purchase impaired-life annuity for regular income. [src:IFoA_0425]
Sum insured chosen by policyholder independently—not designed to return policyholder to pre-condition position. Unlike IP (addresses income loss) or PMI (reimburses medical costs).
No guarantee sum insured will be paid (not certain that insured events occur). No surrender value, no maturity benefit. Policy ends on first claim or at term end.
Product Variations & Structures
Sum insured paid only on diagnosis of insured condition; no payment on death.
Sum insured paid on diagnosis of insured CI or death, whichever occurs first. Death benefit reduced by any CI payment made.
(1) Lump sum (event-based, independent of severity), (2) Degree of Impairment (e.g., loss of ability to walk unaided), (3) Surgical Procedure (e.g., major organ transplant, heart bypass).
Installment option with outstanding amount payable on death; can help claimant manage finances; reduces fraud potential.
Characteristics of Insurable CI Conditions

1. **Perceived Serious & Occurs Frequently**: Public perception drives coverage (sometimes disproportionate to actual incidence). 'Lifestyle-threatening' if not life-threatening. Must not be so rare as to be negligible. [src:IFoA_0425]

2. **Clearly Defined**: Avoid ambiguity at claim time. Use precise medical terminology (e.g., cancers restricted to malignant tumours invading adjacent tissue; heart attacks require enzyme changes in blood). Prevents windfall payments for minor conditions. [src:IFoA_0425]

3. **Sufficient Pricing Data**: Must predict future incidence accurately; difficult due to medical advances (vaccines, diet changes, treatments). New conditions take time for sufficient claims data to build up.
Asymmetry of information (e.g., occupational exposure to cancer not picked up by UW). Mitigated by waiting periods (e.g., 6 months for cancer-related claims).
Major CI Conditions (Core List—~90% of Claims)
Cancer, coronary artery bypass surgery, heart attack, kidney failure, major organ transplant, multiple sclerosis, stroke.

Death of heart muscle due to inadequate blood supply requiring: typical symptoms (chest pain), characteristic EKG changes, specific enzyme/Troponin levels. Excludes other acute coronary syndromes and angina.
Additional CI Conditions

(variable by market): Alzheimer's disease, AIDS/HIV (via transfusion or occupation), aorta graft surgery, benign brain tumour, blindness, coma, deafness, heart valve replacement/repair, loss of limbs, loss of speech, motor neurone disease, paralysis/paraplegia, severe burns, severe head injury.

Some markets split conditions into 'Core' and 'Additional'; others use limited Core conditions (cancer, heart attack, stroke) with insurer-added options. Some markets standardise wording across insurers for consistency and faster settlement.
Risks & Claims Issues
Complex medical terminology increases dispute risk. Precise wording essential to avoid litigation.
Medical advances enable earlier diagnosis (sometimes before severe deterioration). Shifts claim timing; impacts frequency/severity balance.
Screening programmes may prompt claims that might have otherwise lapsed.
New markets lack sufficient experience data; proxy from reinsurers or population data (adjusted for insured population selectivity).
Reduced compared to IP due to lump-sum nature; monitoring of recoveries still important.
Rider vs Standalone Capital Considerations
Separate pricing/reserving; own capital assessment; distinct profitability analysis.
Rider on life cover; interacts with term life capital requirements; reduced sum at risk if CI paid before death.
Market Standardisation Benefits
Insurers/reinsurers cooperate on condition definitions; reduces ambiguity; faster claims settlement; fewer disputes.
Customers understand products; sales staff explain clearly; better industry-wide data collection.
Better information allows lower premiums, increasing market size and profitability.
Additional Cover Extensions
**🔹 Terminal Illness**
Illness expected to result in death within 12 months (in opinion of a hospital consultant). Triggered by severity and effect on life expectancy — NOT by diagnosis of a specified CI condition.
Fills gaps where a severe illness significantly reduces life expectancy but does not qualify as a listed CI condition; perceived as equitable benefit.

Main effect is to bring forward (accelerate) the death benefit — additional cost is very small (approximately 12 months' investment return on sum insured). Cost tip: benefit often ceases 12–24 months before policy end to avoid additional claims near term end.
Terminal illness IS a genuine extra cost — no death benefit exists to accelerate; incidence by cause of terminal illness must be priced and reserved separately.
**🔹 Total and Permanent Disability (TPD)**

'Permanent' = beyond hope of recovery in policyholder's lifetime. 'Total' = major and substantial part of role or function failed (not every single element). Permanency distinguishes TPD from IP (which also covers temporary disability).
Often used before claim accepted as permanent; IBNS (incurred but not settled) category maintained while outcome remains unclear. Benefit paid immediately if diagnosis is unequivocal.
Many CI conditions (paralysis, dementia) can also qualify as TPD claims — significant overlap means TPD can be added to extensive CI products for relatively little additional cost.

*Occupation-based*: Inability to perform own occupation / all occupations suited by education, training or experience / any occupation. Caution: lenient interpretation in practice means claims often paid more generously than priced — experience monitoring must feed back into pricing.

*ADL-based*: Inability to perform (unaided) a required number of Activities of Daily Living — feeding, dressing, washing, toileting, mobility, transferring. Can be applied beyond retirement; less subjective than occupation-based. TPD written on this basis throughout life often called 'loss of independent existence'.
*ADW/FAT/PCA-based*: Skills-based definitions (dexterity, mobility, communication); can be more objective, particularly if skills relate to occupational class.
Physically strenuous occupations (e.g. building trade) typically only offered TPD on 'any occupation' definition — cost of more comprehensive cover is high and difficult to quantify.
**🔹 Children's Benefit**

Cover for each child of the policyholder, typically until age 18. Child claims do NOT terminate the policy — cover continues for subsequent child claims and the policyholder's own claim. Only the policyholder's own claim terminates cover.
Early product designs excluded conditions that could be caused intentionally (e.g. third-degree burns, paralysis) due to concern that parents might take less care. These fears proved unfounded.
Typically only pre-existing congenital defects excluded.

Actual cost is minimal — childhood CI incidence is low (e.g. 1 in 10,000 children diagnosed with cancer per year in Britain; most other serious childhood diseases also rare). However, perceived value to parents is very high, making it a strong marketing and sales feature.
CI Customer Needs & Use of Benefit
⬡ - The CI lump sum can fund immediate and ongoing medical costs including treatment, surgery, and hospital stays — the primary motivator for many policyholders. [src:IFoA_0425]
⬡ - Benefit can be used to repay a mortgage or other loan on diagnosis of a critical illness, protecting the policyholder's key financial commitments. [src:IFoA_0425]
⬡ - Benefit can fund other insurance premiums or continuing retirement savings when the policyholder cannot work — preserving long-term financial planning. [src:IFoA_0425]
⬡ - Provides financial support when a dependant requires care as a result of the policyholder's illness (e.g. parent unable to care for children). [src:IFoA_0425]
⬡ - Funds day-to-day living costs (rent, food, utilities) during a period of recovery or reduced income. [src:IFoA_0425]
⬡ - Funds adaptations to the home environment (e.g. wheelchair access), domestic help, lifestyle changes, and recuperation or rehabilitation treatment. [src:IFoA_0425]
⬡ - Ensures the policyholder is not reliant on state benefits or family financial support following a critical illness — an important product positioning point alongside IP. [src:IFoA_0425]
Consumer Value & Product Governance

⬡ - A low CI claims payout ratio (e.g. 55–60%) is a key indicator of product inadequacy: it may reflect overly restrictive condition definitions, unreasonably harsh claims management, or widespread policyholder misunderstanding of coverage. Regulators treat persistently low payout ratios as evidence of poor consumer outcomes. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Increasing the number of CI conditions does not automatically improve consumer value: if added conditions are rare or have low incidence, the payout ratio barely improves while the product appears more comprehensive — creating perceived but not real value, which regulators may classify as deceptive design. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - CI conditions should only be included if the policyholder has a realistic chance of contracting them; conditions that are rare, clinically ambiguous, or costly to administer add premium burden without delivering meaningful protection. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Rising claim settlement times are a significant consumer detriment — policyholders typically need the CI benefit quickly to fund medical treatment; delays generate complaints, ombudsman referrals, and regulatory scrutiny. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - If expected lifetime premiums materially exceed the maximum benefit payable on the policy, the product fails the fair value test; regulators and consumer advocates treat this ratio as prima facie evidence of over-pricing. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Rising distributor commission without corresponding policyholder value is a regulatory concern; high commission incentivises churning and mis-selling and will be scrutinised as a driver of poor value. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Consumer-outcome regulation (e.g. Consumer Responsibility / TCF) requires insurers to define 'good outcomes' and 'fair value' for their own CI products, review them periodically, and demonstrate compliance via monitoring and Board reporting. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - CI product review under consumer responsibility requires: (a) target market review with assessment of changing customer needs (longevity, earlier diagnosis, lifestyle changes), (b) product design review — conditions, benefit levels, introduction of severity-based partial payments, (c) clear product literature and definitions, (d) appropriate distribution strategy and commission governance, (e) fair pricing benchmarked against competitors. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Severity-based partial payments (as an alternative to the standard binary all-or-nothing lump sum) can better match benefit design to degree of illness and reduce the proportion of legitimate claims that fail the standard threshold. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - CI benefit levels should be reviewed regularly to ensure they keep pace with inflation; a benefit that does not increase in real terms provides progressively less financial protection over the life of a long-term policy. [src:IFoA_0425]
Return of Premium (ROP) Options on CI

⬡ - Return of Premium (ROP) options on CI policies can be triggered by: surrender/lapse, end of term or age expiry, death (outside a CI claim), conversion to another policy, claim decline, claim within a moratorium/survival period, or partial claim — each trigger has different anti-selection implications and pricing complexity. [src:IFoA_0423]

⬡ - Pricing ROP options requires assessing: the probability and timing of each trigger event; expected policyholder behaviour (e.g. selective lapse by healthier lives for surrender ROP); investment return on pre-funded reserves; amount of premium returned (full vs partial, net of charges); and whether terms are fair between policyholders with and without the option. [src:IFoA_0423]

⬡ - ROP options increase CI premiums, reducing affordability and potentially widening the protection gap — regulators may intervene if prevalent ROP usage makes standard CI unaffordable for lower-income policyholders or raises commission-driven mis-selling concerns. [src:IFoA_0423]

⬡ - From an IFA perspective, recommending ROP requires balancing: client need and affordability; the option's value as a quasi-savings vehicle or tax-deferral tool; commission implications (higher premium = higher commission if commission-linked); and TCF/regulatory risk if the added complexity leads to policyholder misunderstanding. [src:IFoA_0423]
Lapse Risk & Recession Management

⬡ - In a recession, selective lapsing of individual CI is driven by affordability pressure — healthier lives (who feel they are less likely to claim) are more willing to lapse, leaving a worse risk pool, higher expected claims, and potential anti-selection spirals if premiums are then increased to compensate. [src:IFoA_0422]

⬡ - Adverse lapse consequences extend beyond direct claim cost: unrecovered initial expenses and commission; fixed costs spread over fewer policies; forced asset disinvestment at unfavourable recession prices; reinsurance premiums becoming relatively more expensive per remaining policy; and potential regulatory scrutiny if large-scale lapsing is processed slowly. [src:IFoA_0422]

⬡ - To minimise lapses on existing CI policies in a recession: proactively communicate policy value; offer flexible options (reduce sum assured, convert escalating to level premiums, pay-up options); introduce premium holidays or payment deferrals; target retention efforts at shorter-duration policies where unrecovered expenses are greatest; and approach distributors to discourage churning. [src:IFoA_0422]

⬡ - Premium holiday (Option A: no catch-up) retains the customer relationship and avoids immediate selective lapse effects but permanently reduces premium income and expected profitability; premium deferral (Option B: catch-up later) recovers missed income but relies on the policyholder's ability and willingness to pay at a future point. [src:IFoA_0422]
Mental Health Conditions in CI

⬡ - Mental health conditions can be suitable for inclusion in a CI product if they: (a) have a severe and debilitating impact comparable to traditional CIs, (b) occur with sufficient frequency, (c) can be clearly and precisely defined to avoid claim ambiguity, and (d) have sufficient data to support pricing. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Adding mental health conditions to CI applies only to new business — existing policyholders will not be covered, which is likely to cause dissatisfaction among those who requested the addition. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Mental health conditions that are temporary or curable are unsuitable for CI inclusion as payment would constitute a windfall rather than a protection benefit — only persistent, seriously debilitating conditions with objective measurable triggers (e.g. inability to work for 12 months, hospitalisation for specified period) should be considered. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Adding mental health conditions requires new underwriting questions that are clear, relevant, proportionate, and sensitive; specialist medical advice is needed to develop both the definitions and the underwriting criteria; questions must comply with local regulation and anti-discrimination law. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Reinsurers are a key resource when adding mental health to CI: they can assist with condition definitions, underwriting criteria, pricing data from other markets, and confirmation of reinsurance availability and cost. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Replacing a defined list of CI conditions with a catch-all term (e.g. 'serious infectious diseases') increases consumer certainty and product simplicity but creates extreme pricing uncertainty, makes past data unusable, raises reserves and capital requirements, and generates legal/dispute risk around what constitutes a qualifying condition. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - If a catch-all CI definition leads to materially higher-than-expected sales, the insurer may face capital strain; over-prudent pricing makes the product unaffordable; under-prudent pricing risks insolvency — the pricing tension is structurally unresolvable without a specific condition list. [src:IFoA_0924]
Income Protection (IP) Insurance Framework (SP1 Chapter 1)
Definition & Nature

Long-term insurance contract providing regular (short- or long-term) payments during periods of incapacity due to illness or accident. Returns regular premiums; multiple claims possible during policy term.
Seen as complex product; general policy conditions must be clear.
Unlike CI (fixed lump sum) or PMI (reimburse medical costs), IP replaces lost earnings. Benefit amount tied to pre-disability income.
Premium Structure & Guarantees
Waived during periods when benefits payable.
Subject to increases in line with regular benefit increases (e.g., earnings-linked benefit escalation).
Reduced/waived premiums if no IP claims made during period.
Fixed throughout policy term (no changes).
Changed on review dates only as result of portfolio experience (not individual experience); must be specified in policy.
Benefit Design & Restrictions
Benefit divided by pre-disability income (net of tax on both). Usually capped max 50-75% to maintain work incentive; over-insurance prevented via maximum benefit formula.
Fixed term or up to specified age (usually retirement, e.g., age 65). Varying duration structures available.
Fixed percentage or published price/earnings index from start; often with corresponding premium increases.
Specified rate applied while benefits payable (maintains claimant's standard of living).
Reduced-rate benefit if policyholder returns to part-time work or lower-paid job.
Definition of Incapacity
Unable to earn income as result of illness or accident. Does NOT cover unemployment, redundancy, early retirement, reluctance to return to work.
Inability to perform own occupation
Inability to perform own occupation + any other suited occupation (by education, status, training)
Own occupation for initial period (e.g., 2 years), then any occupation thereafter
Inability to perform any occupation
Regardless of occupation—
Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)
Activities of Daily Working (ADWs)
Functional Assessment Tests (FATs)
Personal Capability Assessments (PCAs)
Functional tests used for housepersons, students, temporarily unemployed.
Deferred Period & Linked-Claim Period
Chosen by policyholder; no benefits payable during this period (e.g., 4, 8, 13, 26 weeks). Longer DP = lower premium.
Imposed by insurer (e.g., 4 weeks). Periods of incapacity separated by less than linked period treated as one continuous claim.
Options & Riders
Increase benefit amount without further health evidence if 'life event' occurs (e.g., marriage, children, home purchase), provided opted at outset with extra premium.
Regular benefit increases guaranteed or reviewable.
Risk & Rating Factors
Age, gender (if permitted), occupation (risk classes), deferred period, smoking status, medical history.
Typically not used as rating factor (despite evidence of effect); residency restrictions may apply for full cover.
Policyholder must inform insurer of changes; may trigger premium adjustment or cover modification.
Product Variations
Annual bonus declared, builds to maturity benefit on death/expiry.
Morbidity charge deductions made from unit fund.
Annual renewable; benefit payments limited to specified period (e.g., 1 year); often used for loan protection ('creditor insurance', 'payment protection insurance').
Group IP Specifics
Based on gross salary (2/3 to 3/4 typical) or net pay schemes (up to 90% net pre-disability income).
Pension contributions (employee/employer), State welfare contributions.
While benefits paid, escalate to maintain claimant living standards.
Employees can convert group cover to individual without health evidence on leaving employment; anti-selection risk high (left due to health); benefits capped to group level.
In jurisdictions with statutory sick pay (e.g., UK 4 days–28 weeks), employers insure liability; deferred period often matches statutory period (e.g., 28 weeks DP in UK).
Key Risks for Underwriting/Pricing
Incentive to remain disabled if replacement ratio too high; return-to-work incentives essential.
Claim rates surge during economic downturns; unemployment masquerades as incapacity.
Own-occ vs any-occ disputes; occupational changes may shift claim likelihood.
Policyholder may terminate if income improves or family circumstances change.
Early intervention, proportionate benefits for partial return to work, rehabilitation funding.
Expanding to Individual IP Business

⬡ - Expanding from group to individual IP requires a distinct distribution strategy (retail/independent brokers rather than group brokers), individual underwriting (age rating, medical evidence at inception), and carefully considered differential pricing — cross-subsidies between group and individual books must be explicitly assessed. [src:IFoA_0425]
⬡ - Reinsurers can provide valuable product development support, pricing data, and claims management expertise when an insurer enters the individual IP market for the first time. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Existing group IP data can be used as a starting point for individual pricing with adjustments for: greater adverse selection at individual level, higher lapse sensitivity to premium changes, and likely different deferred period mix compared to group schemes. [src:IFoA_0425]
IP Loss Ratio Investigation

⬡ - When the IP loss ratio is higher than competitors, investigate: higher claim inception rates, lower recovery rates, higher claim amounts, lower premium income, and changes in business mix (age, gender, smoker, occupation, scheme size, free cover limits). [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Business mix changes that can drive a deteriorating loss ratio include: loss of large group schemes with favourable experience, marketing campaigns attracting poorer risks, declining new business causing the average age of the book to rise, and cross-subsidies in the pricing basis being exposed. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Product terms and conditions changes to investigate: incapacity definition, deferred period, benefit limits, replacement ratios, inclusion of partial/rehabilitation benefits, underwriting criteria — any of which may have shifted relative to market norms. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Also investigate: early claims or claims by notification date (anti-selection), cause of claim mix (mental health/stress claims have lower recovery rates), outlier/abnormal claims, ex-gratia settlements, and any large group schemes with disproportionate influence on the overall ratio. [src:IFoA_0924]
Active IP Claims Management

⬡ - Setting up a dedicated active claims management team for IP requires estimating: current and future claim volumes, proportion where intervention could improve outcomes, cause and duration of claims (to determine skill mix required), staff salaries and inflation, treatment costs, system development costs, and external specialist costs. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Benefits of an active IP claims management team include: one-off reduction in reserves for existing long-duration claims with return-to-work potential, long-term arrest of the deteriorating loss ratio trend, and potential brand/new business uplift from positioning treatment management as a value-added service. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Risks of setting up an active IP claims management team: costs exceeding estimates, benefits failing to materialise, difficulty recruiting specialist medical staff, reputational risk from actively directing treatment, and regulatory non-compliance if treatment management is not within permitted insurer activities. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - Mitigants for active claims management risks: pilot or outsource first to test cost/benefit assumptions; retrain existing claims staff; negotiate network agreements with doctor or hospital groups; introduce conservatively (clear return-to-work cases only); update terms and conditions and sales processes so policyholders understand the insurer may direct treatment. [src:IFoA_0924]

⬡ - IP is well-suited to families with dependants and mortgage obligations where state disability benefits are limited — it replaces lost earned income during incapacity, complementing CI (lump sum) and any state support. IP is not appropriate for non-earners (retired grandparents, children) or those already covered by adequate employer sick pay. [src:IFoA_0923]
Rating Strategies
Rating Variables
Classic variables (though gender restricted in EU).
Adjusting renewal premiums based on past group claims.
Premium Structures
No Claims Discounts (common in PMI) to prevent trivial claims but hurts retention if lost.
Guaranteed (fixed) vs Reviewable (can hike rates if experience worsens over the block).
State vs Private Interaction
Universal coverage (PMJAY / NHS), means-tested social care.
Que-jumping, luxury facilities, covering non-critical procedures.
Aging populations increase LTC and medical costs, shrinking taxpayer bases.
QALYs (Quality Adjusted Life Years) to decide drug funding via agencies like NICE.

⬡ - A government proposal to replace private PMI with a compulsory social insurance scheme forces insurers to: discontinue existing products (with costly system redesign), diversify into complementary lines (e.g. CI, overseas cover, supplementary benefits), and reassess long-term viability as the addressable market shrinks dramatically. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - From the government perspective, a mandatory social insurance scheme can equalise access to care, improve economic productivity, and unlock pooled purchasing power — but risks unsustainable fiscal strain if medical inflation exceeds tax revenue growth or if demand surges when previously uninsured populations access services. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - Citizens currently using private healthcare may face higher tax burdens under a social scheme that could exceed their existing PMI premiums, loss of provider choice (only registered hospitals covered), and quality concerns if private practitioners leave or are overburdened by the influx of new patients. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - Social insurance schemes can be financed via: general taxation, income-related payroll deductions (employee/employer contributions), sales tax increases, government borrowing, or redirection of existing tax revenue. Payroll deductions are predictable and link contributions to income but exclude the self-employed; sales taxes are broad-based but regressive. [src:IFoA_0424]
Low Insurance Take-Up in Developing Markets

⬡ - Low private health insurance take-up in a developing country typically reflects: low financial literacy and awareness of product benefits, over-reliance on limited state provision, unaffordable premiums relative to incomes, poor distribution access (limited internet, few advisors), industry reputation concerns from past mis-selling, and products that are too complex or not designed for the local market. [src:IFoA_0423]

⬡ - Government actions to increase private health insurance penetration include: restricting or means-testing state benefits to create a protection gap; offering tax relief on premiums/benefits for individuals and employers; mandating or incentivising employer group schemes; running public education campaigns; strengthening regulation and ombudsman powers to build consumer confidence; introducing premium caps; and allowing overseas insurers to enter the market to stimulate competition and product innovation. [src:IFoA_0423]

⬡ - Allowing overseas insurers to enter a developing market can bring expertise, product innovation, and competitive pressure that benefits consumers and domestic insurers — but risks foreign insurers dominating the market and extracting profits, undermining domestic players and reducing long-term tax revenues if profits are repatriated. [src:IFoA_0423]
Long-Term Care Insurance Framework (SP1 Chapter 3)
Definition & Scope

All forms of continuing personal or nursing care and associated domestic services for people unable to care for themselves without support. Provided in own homes, day centres, or State-sponsored/private care homes.
Designed for people NOT expected to recover. Focuses on day-to-day living (getting up/bed, washing, cooking, shopping) rather than curing medical conditions.
While SP1 focuses on elderly, LTCI benefits payable for care need at any age (disabled from birth, accident, illness).
Informal (family/spouse) has indirect cost (lost economic activity, replacement care cost). Formal provided at home or residential care.
Cost Components (Must Separate)
Food, clothing, heating, amenities (extra needed for care needs).
('Hotel' Element): Rent, mortgage, council tax, accommodation adaptations (bath hoist). Higher for care recipients.
Additional costs from frailty/disability; narrower 'nursing care' (qualified nurse knowledge/skills) more expensive than other personal care.
Typically cover EXTRA costs (difference from non-care scenario); not full costs.
Long-Term Care Provision
Majority home-based (preferred by individuals, government policy); private/charity residential homes for others.
Private care providers, nursing homes, care homes run by charities (serve specific communities).
Recuperative services post-acute event (heart attack, stroke, accident) to prevent hospital readmission; bridges hospital-home gap.
Uncertainties in Care Needs Assessment
Medical advances cure previously debilitating illnesses; genetic predisposition variable; published statistics may not apply to individuals.
Depends on medical advances, technological advances (equipment enabling independence), speed of deterioration, availability of informal support.
Longevity unknown; life expectancy based on averages, not individual factors; mortality improvements increase uncertainty.
If underestimated, accumulated wealth insufficient to meet care needs.
Types of LTCI Products

Purchased by relatively healthy people to protect against future disability. Secured by single premium at outset or level annual premiums throughout life (or to specified age). Premium waiver during claim.
Purchased by long-term care claimants (already needing care). Single-premium product; benefit payable from start of care.
Ideally reimburses additional costs of day-to-day living when in need of care. May subject to overriding maxima to control claims costs.
Not indemnity; provide fixed cash benefits (insurer knows exposure).
Benefit Triggers & Structure
Benefit payable on entry to care (ADL loss: Activities of Daily Living loss; e.g., unable to bathe, toilet, dress without assistance).
Independent → Partial Dependency → Full Dependency. Transfers between levels tracked; potential for down-grade (improvement) and up-grade (deterioration).
Long-term contracts need inflation-protection. Real assets (equities) for inflation match; investment importance significant (large reserves).
Product Design Considerations
Deferred period reduces risk-premium. Longer DP = lower cost (e.g., 0, 13, 26, 52 weeks). Policyholder pays care costs during DP from own resources.
Savings element justifies surrender value design (unlike pure protection IP/CI). Return-of-premium rider possible.
Long-term contracts offered both guaranteed and reviewable variants.
Single premium or level annual premiums (spreads cost; amortization risk if lapse); premium waiver during claim.
Complementarity with Other Products
Many critical illnesses in old age lead to LTCI need. Good overlap but distinct triggers (CI: diagnosis; LTCI: ADL loss).
LTCI benefits similar to PMI but cover longer-term chronic care; PMI covers acute treatment. Different benefit profiles.
IP + LTCI combination covers earnings loss (IP) + care costs if retirement (LTCI). No link between IP benefit level (earnings-based) and LTCI benefit (care-cost-based); problems in integrated design.
Good overlap (care benefits viewed as 'longevity insurance'). LTCI often added as rider to life cover.
Adding LTCI (low consumer interest) to CI/life (high consumer interest) increases attachment; complicates experience analysis, claims management, capital assessment.
Risks & Underwriting
LTCI has high per-capita claim rates compared to life insurance.
Average claims duration 3-5 years; significant reserve build.
Reduced (fixed benefit); but monitoring needed for claim verification/duration.
Perception of '100% care coverage' attracts those expecting to need care. Underwriting at entry critical.
Can prolong life in disability (increasing costs) or hasten recovery (reducing costs); unpredictable.
Detailed record-keeping on dependency level transitions (up-grade/down-grade).
Key Actuarial Challenges
Significant financial impact due to high inception/duration; investment importance high (real asset allocation for inflation, fixed-interest for guarantees).
New markets lack historical experience; proxy from reinsurers or population data (adjusted for insured selectivity).
Must model claim inception rates, duration by dependency level, transitions between levels, inflation impact.
LTCI strains capital at launch (negative asset share early); significant SCR impact due to guarantees/policyholder options.
Pre-Funded LTC vs Self-Funded Care

⬡ - Pre-funded LTC insurance (Option A) transfers longevity, care cost inflation, and morbidity risks to the insurer and provides known, budgetable premiums — but includes a profit margin, possible premium tax, and the risk of losing funds if the insured dies before needing care (mitigated by a return-of-premium death benefit). [src:IFoA_0923]

⬡ - Self-funded care (Option B) preserves liquidity and flexibility, avoids insurer profit margin and expenses, and allows funds to be redirected if care is not needed — but leaves the family fully exposed to care cost inflation, longevity risk, investment underperformance, and the physical and mental burden of caregiving on working-age family members. [src:IFoA_0923]

⬡ - A key risk of self-funded care is that care needs may exceed the family's capacity to provide them (e.g. complex dementia requiring 24-hour specialist input) — at which point external professional care must be funded privately at full market cost, eroding the apparent cost savings of Option B. [src:IFoA_0923]
Government Policy & Tax Incentives for LTC

⬡ - Governments may provide income tax relief for individuals who self-fund and self-provide care for elderly relatives to: reduce pressure on the state healthcare system (freeing hospital beds occupied by those with unmet care needs), encourage workforce retention (carers stay employed rather than leaving to provide full-time care), stimulate the private care industry, and redistribute wealth by making care affordable for lower-income families. [src:IFoA_0923]

⬡ - An opposing rationale for tax relief on pre-funded LTC insurance premiums is to: grow the private insurance and care industry (creating jobs and GDP), provide a market-based alternative to unaffordable state provision, offer families a known-cost budgetable solution, and reduce the long-term fiscal burden on the state by shifting care funding to private insurance. [src:IFoA_0923]
Tax & Law Impacts
Are premiums tax deductible? Are benefits tax free? Drives product demand.
Incentivizes Group PMI / Group IP if treated favorably as business expense.
Equality acts preventing pricing based on genetics, gender, or disabilities without pure statistical proof.
General Protection Framework
Selling CI or Death cover attached to a savings/mortgage plan.
Charging mortality/morbidity costs by deducting units from a fund (reviewable by nature).
Conversion options, Guaranteed Insurability Options (GIOs) without medicals.
Admin & IT Systems
Can legacy IT handle complex tiered benefits or unit-deductions?
Quotation portals, premium collection methods (Direct Debit).
Using Third Party Administrators (TPAs) for claims, call centers. Retaining oversight.
Monitoring & Feedback into the Control Cycle (P5: Valuation & Data)
📌 Why Monitor Experience?
Update assumptions for future pricing, reserving and financial planning
Detect adverse trends early (e.g. rising morbidity, medical inflation) and take corrective action
Compare actual vs expected (A/E) experience and quantify financial impact of divergences
Provide management information to support business decisions
Make better-informed decisions on pricing adequacy and reserve sufficiency
Feed results back into the actuarial control cycle
📋 Data Requirements
Requires a reasonable volume of **stable, consistent data** from which future experience and trends can be deduced
Data must be in the same form across groups — consistent age definitions, smoker status, etc. Inconsistencies distort comparisons
Divide data into **sufficiently homogeneous risk groups** (by age, gender, smoker status, occupation, duration, region, etc.), balanced against the need for credible cell sizes
Investigation period must be long enough for credible data, but short enough to remain relevant — typically **3–5 years** for morbidity; **12 months** for expenses
Where own data is thin, apply **credibility weighting**: blend actual experience with reinsurer, market or population data (adjusted for relevance)
🆕 New Business Analysis
Monitor volume of sales vs targets to assess **new business strain** and capital adequacy
Check **business mix** within risk groups against pricing assumptions — cross-subsidy effects (e.g. age mix in PMI) can materially affect profitability
Verify **staffing levels** (numbers and competence) remain adequate for the volume written
Compare **commissions paid** against pricing assumptions — deviations may indicate distribution mix changes
Rapid increase vs market may signal adverse selection, lax claim definitions, or under-pricing
Falling volumes reduce return on capital and distort per-policy expense assumptions
🔁 Renewals & Persistency Experience
(in approximate order of importance):
Type of contract (long-term vs short-term renewals behave differently)
Duration since inception (lapses typically highest in early years)
Sales method and target market (broker sales → highest persistency; direct response → lowest)
Premium frequency and size (monthly = more lapse opportunities; large annual premium = affordability risk)
Premium payment method (cash payments → lower persistency vs direct debit)
Original policy term
Age and gender (younger policyholders show lower persistency)
NCD level / claims record (for PMI — those with poor claims record less likely to renew)
First-year persistency = policies surviving to first anniversary ÷ policies issued; repeated for subsequent years; adjust to exclude deaths and maturities
worsen the remaining risk pool — healthy lives lapse, sick lives stay — increasing morbidity experience
(not in past data but must be considered): current economic conditions, competitive environment, perceived product value
Short-term products (PMI) typically only profitable after 2–3 years of renewals; lapse monitoring is critical to profitability
🏥 Mortality & Morbidity / Sickness Experience
Analyse claim **inception rates** and **termination rates** (recovery, death) separately
Segment data by: contract type, age, gender, duration, smoker status, underwriting status, occupation, deferred period, geographical location, benefit conditions

also segment by: excess amount, NCD level, hospital band; investigate claim frequency and severity separately; monitor medical inflation vs embedded assumption; track provider pricing changes and state healthcare system changes

investigate: higher inception rates, lower recovery rates, higher claim amounts, changes in business mix, deferred period effects (heavier morbidity at shorter deferred periods), economic conditions (recession → more claims and more lapses), linked-claims periods
break down by cause of claim (cancer, heart attack, stroke, etc.); monitor socio-economic and lifestyle factors
monitor geographical variation in care costs, claims inflation, evolving business mix profile
Compare A/E ratios: identify **systematic bias** (model error) vs **random fluctuation**; repeated negative surplus from same source signals assumption error
Use market data and reinsurer data to supplement own experience and spot trends earlier — but be cautious of heterogeneity and publication delays
Monitor **competitor activity**: pricing, underwriting methods, risk segmentation — competitors using more rating factors can adversely select against you
💷 Claims Amount Experience
compare actual vs expected claim amounts by risk cell and by type of procedure; drill down to hospital band, hospital chain, individual surgeons/consultants if needed
Key focus: the **trend (rate of inflation) of claim escalation** relative to standard indices and market data — long-term inflation driver often matters more than day-to-day volatility
analyse incidence by broad sum insured bands — reveals anti-selection (higher sums insured having higher claim rates) and underwriting/sales technique issues
💼 Expense Experience
Allocate total costs (salaries, property, computer, investment) to **cells**: initial / renewal / termination / claims / investment expenses
Further split by **expense driver**: per policy, per unit premium, per unit sum insured
Exclude commission (known and priced explicitly); exclude one-off exceptional items (allow for via margins)
Compare actual per-policy and per-premium expenses to pricing assumptions; identify trends over 3–5 years
Reconcile total allocated expenses back to published accounts as a consistency check
Expense investigation typically performed **annually**; seasonal distortions require at least a full year of data
📈 Investment Experience
Assess actual return on assets vs expected; analyse by main asset classes, both gross and net of investment expenses
Investment expenses usually allowed for as a **reduction in yield** by asset class
📊 Analysis of Surplus / Profit
Reconciles actual surplus arising over the period to that expected under the valuation basis; breaks it into component sources [src:IFoA_0922]
Mortality/morbidity surplus: actual vs expected claims
Persistency surplus: actual vs expected lapses
Expense surplus: actual vs expected (split acquisition, admin, claims management, inflation)
Investment surplus: actual vs expected return
New business strain: reserves exceed premiums at outset — requires capital
Release of prudential margins: margins unwinding as policies run off
Change of basis: effect of assumption or methodology changes on reserves
Interest on opening surplus
[src:IFoA_0922]
Show the financial effect of divergences between valuation assumptions and actual experience — exposing which assumptions are most financially significant
Show the financial effect of writing new business
Provide an independent check on the valuation data and process
Identify non-recurring surplus components to guide appropriate distribution decisions (dividends to shareholders; distribution to with-profits policyholders; retention as buffer)
Comply with regulatory requirements
On a **prudent supervisory basis**: expect surplus to emerge each year — alarm bells ring if any component is negative
On a **realistic basis**: negative components are normal; concern arises only if large, or repeated year-on-year
100% of surplus attributable to shareholders [src:IFoA_0922]
💎 Analysis of Embedded Value (EV)
Reconciles opening to closing EV over a period; standard components:
Opening EV
Expected return on EV (unwinding of discount at risk discount rate)
Value of new business (VNB) written in the period
Experience variances: actual vs assumed mortality, lapses, expenses, investment return
Assumption changes: effect of updating long-term assumptions on VIF
Economic variances: changes in risk-free rates, equity returns
Other operating variances
Closing EV
= PV of future after-tax profits on new policies after deducting cost of required capital; VNB margin = VNB ÷ APE or PVNBP
Validate calculations, assumptions and data used
Reconcile values for successive years (essential given model complexity)
Provide management information (value of new business by product; profit/loss by source)
Provide data for executive remuneration schemes (EV change is a better performance measure than supervisory surplus, which is depressed by new business strain)
Publish in accounts to give a truer picture of financial position than statutory accounts alone
typically shows impact of ±1% risk discount rate, ±10% mortality, ±10% lapses
Used to assess which products, channels and geographies create shareholder value
Experience variances in EV analysis reconcile to the same drivers investigated in experience analysis (incidence, lapse, expense, investment)
🔄 Using the Results — Feedback into the Control Cycle
e.g. deteriorating morbidity → increase premium rates (new business only, unless premiums are reviewable)
basis normally reflects past experience subject to regulatory constraints; frequent changes complicate year-on-year comparisons
poor new business volumes in a product → review advertising spend or distribution approach
commission structures, distributor selection, literature format, clawback mechanics
rapid sales growth may require additional or differently skilled staff
e.g. more stringent underwriting for high benefit amounts if morbidity is elevated
e.g. more frequent checks on IP claims during economic downturns
over-priced product → review capital loading → lower profit loading → more competitive price
better data quality feeds directly into more reliable assumptions
remove options rarely used or adversely selected; improve CI condition definitions
especially for CI contracts where conditions covered are ambiguous

This is an **iterative process** — assumptions gradually home in on reality, but the ever-changing nature of actual experience means monitoring must be continuous and assumptions must retain margins for model error
🎯 Key Exam Points
Always distinguish between **supervisory basis** (expect surplus; negative = alarm) and **realistic basis** (negatives are normal)
Analysis of surplus → impact on **current year profits** only; Analysis of EV → also captures impact on **future profits** — richer information set
Selective lapses always **worsen** the remaining risk pool — an important direction of effect to state
Economic downturns: **increase** claim inceptions (IP), **reduce** recoveries, **increase** lapses (selective), **increase** fraudulent claims
For A/E analysis: always state that **both claims data and exposed-to-risk data** are needed — a common mark-scoring point
New business strain arises even on **profitable** business if reserving basis is prudent — state the mechanism clearly

202104 Q3(ii) — experience analysis framework; 202409 Q3(i) — IP monitoring; 202204 Q2(i)/(ii) — PMI claims; 202209 Q2(iv–vi) — surplus analysis; 202009 Q3(iii) — EV reconciliation; 201909 Q1(vii)/(viii) — EV components
Consumer Outcomes & TCF Monitoring

⬡ - Under consumer responsibility / TCF regulations, insurers should produce regular MI (e.g. monthly or quarterly) covering key consumer outcome metrics: (a) proportion of CI/health claims paid out, (b) value of claims paid vs premiums received, (c) average time to settle claims, (d) benefit adequacy vs inflation, (e) margin levels, (f) distributor commission levels, (g) service levels, (h) customer complaints and ombudsman referrals. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Senior management should review consumer outcome statistics with defined tolerance limits; breaches should trigger a documented action log with remediation owners and timelines. Key statistics and trends should be reported to the Board. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Trend analysis over time (not just point-in-time statistics) is critical for TCF monitoring — a deteriorating trend should trigger review and remediation before formal thresholds are breached. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - A formal stakeholder feedback process (customers and distributors) and a structured dispute resolution mechanism are important inputs to ongoing consumer outcome monitoring — they surface issues not captured in quantitative MI. [src:IFoA_0425]
Surplus Uses — Key Exam Points

⬡ - Surplus arising from a change in reserving methodology (e.g. improved data segmentation) should be treated with caution: if the new methodology turns out to be less accurate, the surplus may need to be reversed at a later date. Retaining a portion of the surplus as a buffer rather than distributing it immediately is prudent. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Additional surplus uses: repay a securitisation or reinsurance loan (where the block of business has been financed via a surplus relief arrangement); offset anticipated losses on another line of business; fund a portfolio acquisition (buying blocks of business from other insurers). [src:IFoA_0922]
TCF Obligations on Premium Reviews

⬡ - Before adjusting premiums following improved risk data: (a) verify that any premium change is within the terms of the reviewable premium contract and communicated consistently with those terms; (b) conduct policyholder research (focus groups) to check that changes are within reasonable expectations; (c) discuss the risk of adverse PR and selective lapsing with the risk board before acting. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - TCF obligations when increasing reviewable premiums: communicate clearly and concisely to affected policyholders; signpost to independent advice; offer an alternative to premium increase (e.g. reduced benefit — extend deferred period, reduce replacement ratio, or move to less generous occupational definition) so policyholders are not forced to lapse. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - If the insurer decides to increase premiums for higher-risk (lower socio-economic) segments: consider simultaneously reducing premiums for lower-risk segments to demonstrate symmetric TCF treatment; ensure commission clawback terms are not enforced on policyholders who choose to lapse following a premium increase. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - The insurer may conclude that no premium action is warranted even where risk differentials are identified: if overall portfolio profitability is adequate and the observed cross-subsidy between risk segments is within reasonable bounds, taking no action avoids the PR, lapse, and regulatory risk of repricing. [src:IFoA_0922]
Reporting Framework (P5: Valuation & Data)
Solvency & Capital Reporting

European prudential framework; three-pillar structure. Pillar 1 (quantitative capital): two capital requirements — SCR (Solvency Capital Requirement) and MCR (Minimum Capital Requirement); Pillar 2: supervisory review; Pillar 3: disclosure.
Moves away from fixed factor-based solvency (current Indian regime: ASM ≥ 150% RSM, where RSM is factor-based); RBC links capital requirement directly to insurer's own risk profile.
Accounting & Value Reporting

Replaces IFRS 4; effective for annual periods beginning on or after 1 January 2023; Indian equivalent is Ind AS 117. Key measurement models: GMM (General Measurement Model / Building Block Approach), PAA (Premium Allocation Approach), VFA (Variable Fee Approach for participating contracts).

Measure of consolidated value of shareholders' interests in an in-force life (and long-term health) insurance business; equals adjusted net worth plus value of in-force business (VIF); used in M&A and management reporting.
Model Office Framework
Definition & Model Types
A full model office is a projection model of the entire company — the sum of the new business model and the in-force business model — used to project future financial conditions.

(1) Single policy profit test — cash/profit flows from one policy from issue; (2) New business model — projects a cohort of new policies; (3) In-force model — projects existing portfolio; (4) Full model office — combines (2) and (3) plus asset and expense models.
Uses & Comparison
New business projections; embedded value calculation and projection; solvency testing; takeovers/mergers; capital management; investment strategy assessment.
Full model office focuses on projections of the supervisory balance sheet and distributions of profit, where the profit test focuses on profitability of a single policy or product.
Components
Takes model points (or full policy data) and parameter values as input; projects policy liabilities and cashflows.
Calculates total expected expenses of the company; fixed overhead expenses modelled globally; per-policy expenses derived from expense investigation.
Complexity varies by purpose — simple (profit test): single expected annual investment return; complex (ALM/stochastic): full asset cashflow projection with reinvestment.
Outputs of sub-models aggregated at a higher level; allows for factors outside the detailed model such as reinsurance, tax, and shareholder transfers.
Investment Strategy & Capital Management
(1) Allocate a proportion of free assets to support reserves; (2) Run asset-liability matching; (3) Derive an investment strategy for the remaining free assets.
Hundreds or thousands of sample paths of future solvency positions generated; output is a distribution of outcomes, enabling risk quantification.
Full model office used to determine capital required to write new business; model point cashflows projected under different scenarios to assess capital adequacy over time.
Limitations & Cross-References
Model points may not represent the in-force business accurately enough; grouping is a simplification; future experience assumptions are uncertain; model error and parameter error both present.
After completing a profit test and setting premiums — run model office with the expected tranche of new business to check overall company profitability and solvency impact.
Embedded value and appraisal value (Ch.13); costing/reserving for options (Ch.20); reserves — statutory and realistic (Ch.18); expense investigation (Ch.16).
Assumptions Framework (SP1 Chapters 13–16)
General Principles & Process (Ch.13)

When setting assumptions, consider: (1) the purpose for which assumptions will be used (pricing, reserving, EV); (2) extra care over assumptions with the greatest financial significance; (3) consistency between all assumptions; (4) legislative/regulatory constraints; (5) the client's needs; (6) accuracy and relevance of data to the insured lives; (7) flexibility of bases to reflect changing risk circumstances.

Four-step framework: (1) Investigate historical experience and form best estimates; (2) Consider future conditions (commercial/economic environment); (3) Determine best estimate assumptions for the expected future period; (4) Adjust best estimates to include a margin for prudence, sized according to the purpose of the model and degree of risk for each parameter.

Assumptions must be considered in totality, not in isolation. Key correlated pairs include: morbidity & lapse rates; morbidity & claims expenses; morbidity & investment return; new business volume & fixed expenses; lapse/non-renewal rates & investment return; investment return & inflation.
Pricing, Reserving & EV Assumptions

Starting point is best estimates of future experience. Margins must be added to guard against adverse experience. Under a cashflow model, risk can be allowed for via: (a) margins applied to expected parameter values; (b) a stochastic approach; or (c) a risk element in the risk discount rate.

content.bold: Pricing vs Reserving vs EV Assumptions
content.text: Pricing uses best estimates + margins (or a higher RDR). Supervisory reserving may use prudent assumptions or best estimate + explicit risk margin / SCR. EV/profitability bases are broadly realistic, with risk allowed for via a risk discount rate.
Demographic Assumptions (Ch.14)

(1) Collect data for an appropriate period (typically 3–4 years); (2) Group data into homogeneous risk cells by age, gender, smoker status, occupation, benefit size, distribution channel, class of product, region; (3) Calculate rates as an adjustment to a standard table; (4) Adjust rates if the target class of lives differs from the data.

Required for all product lines – IP, CI (per illness separately), LTCI, PMI (per benefit category). Key influences: State welfare policy; economic outlook/unemployment; inflation. Industry, reinsurer and population data supplement own data.
Rates decrease with duration since onset of sickness. Key influences: motivation to return to work; economic environment; policy conditions; insurer rehabilitation and claims management.
Separate rates needed for healthy (non-claiming) lives and lives in claim. CI survivorship captured as an adjustment to the CI claim incidence rate.

Own company experience is the primary source. Key influences: economic affordability; State benefits; competitive alternatives; after-sales service. Lapse and re-entry risk arises when premiums are falling.
Financial Assumptions (Ch.15)
PMI: indemnity-based so both claim incidence and average claim amount must be estimated. Benefit inflation for long-term contracts must be explicitly modelled as it affects per-unit pricing.
Categories: initial acquisition; initial medical underwriting; initial administration; renewal administration; renewal commission; investment; withdrawal; claim administration; termination.
Expenses largely reflect staff/salary costs, hence linked to a mix of price and earnings inflation.
Higher initial vs renewal commission reflects extra sales effort. Clawback of initial commission on early lapse must account for intermediary credit risk.
Must be consistent with all inflation assumptions. Assets backing the contract are typically fixed interest or index-linked bonds. PMI: investment return of minimal importance.
Three main types: (1) Taxes on profits; (2) Taxes on investment income less expenses (I–E basis); (3) Taxes on premiums.
New Business & Risk Discount Rate (Ch.16)
New business volume affects expense loadings and capital requirements at product launch. Incorrect mix assumptions expose the insurer to anti-selection.

Reflects: (a) shareholders' required return – risk-free rate plus a risk premium quantified using CAPM; and (b) product-specific statistical risk. Features increasing statistical risk: lack of historical data; high guarantees; policyholder options; overhead costs; complex design; untested market.
Health Cash Plan Framework (SP1 Chapter 4)
Definition & Nature
Defined-benefit, defined-premium insurance product. Very low premium (~$2/week).
Provides specific cash payouts for healthcare events (not indemnity); insurer knows maximum exposure.
Purchased in 'units'; equivalent benefit levels with contribution increase per unit.
Coverage & Benefit Types
Dental (treatment/cleaning), optical (spectacles, contact lenses, tests), physiotherapy, maternity, hospitalisation, recuperation, hearing aids, consultation.
May reimburse 50% of physio costs up to max £200/year; defined caps on each benefit type.
Limits ensure policyholder claims not more than e.g. 50% of medical bill (cost-sharing between policyholder and insurer).
Rating Approach
Typical approach—no individual-level medical underwriting. Uses group/community-level information (area, occupation class).
Unlike PMI, no assessment of applicant age, gender, health status at individual level. All members with same values of community factors charged same premium.
No individual applicant meeting eligibility conditions will be refused (no individual UW).
Common premium charged may increase year-to-year due to claims cost inflation.
Sometimes even community-level information ignored; identical premium charged to all choosing particular benefit level.
Waiting Periods & Pre-Existing Conditions
Typically 6 months before benefit eligibility.
No waiting on accident-related claims (reduces anti-selection risk).
Special waiting period, usually at least 9 months.
May impact ability to claim in first period.
Reduce anti-selection risk (define cash benefit < full indemnity cost reduces incentive to take out if expecting claim).
Profitability & Sustainability
Product profitability depends on long-term policyholder loyalty; fail to renew = fixed costs unrecovered.
Process must be streamlined; manual assessment costly relative to small claim amounts.
Large in-force number spreads fixed costs (product development, marketing) across policies.
Long renewal chains treat like longer-term contract; amortize initial expenses
Cost-efficient claims admission reduces impact on profits
Large policy volume spreads fixed costs; small per-policy expense loading
Much smaller expense loading in premium than competitors.
Market & Distribution
Simple health insurance product (low complexity vs comprehensive PMI).
Direct, broker, employer group, credit card linked.
Easier to explain, understand, compare vs complex PMI; lower operating costs.
Can be part of cash plan or standalone policies; may apply waiting period/PEC exclusions.
Distinction from Indemnity Products
Policyholders not fully reimbursed for medical costs (may be 50% coinsurance); differ from indemnity PMI.
Unlike PMI (indemnity = open-ended), cash plan cap limits insurer liability per service.
Fixed benefit (not full cost) < perception; less incentive to claim if known to incur medical expense soon.
Non-Traditional Distribution & Free Product Campaigns

⬡ - A free health cash plan offered via a retail/grocery partnership (requiring minimum customer spend) can build brand awareness and create a lead pool for upselling paid products — but exposes the insurer to claims cost without premium income, requiring the campaign to be funded via marketing budget or partner contribution and capped on volume to manage capital strain. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - Regulatory concerns with a free health cash product include: financial soundness (no premium income; adequacy of reserves), mis-selling risk (minimum spend condition; product limitations not understood by customers), data usage (access to spending patterns without explicit consent), and whether automatic opt-in is permissible. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - Design mitigants for a free promotional health cash product: cap total policies written or terminate campaign early based on take-up or emerging claims; pilot in limited stores/regions first; ensure clear product literature and FAQs; position staff in stores to explain limitations; consider accident-only benefit to remove waiting period complexity and mis-selling risk. [src:IFoA_0424]

⬡ - An advertising campaign (TV, radio, social media, in-store) has greater reach beyond the partner's customer base and a certain, controllable spend — but a free product creates direct product experience, generates testimonials, and produces a trackable lead pool with proxy income data (minimum spend qualifier), making conversion rates easier to measure. [src:IFoA_0424]
Microinsurance Products

⬡ - Microinsurance provides fixed-benefit, low-premium cover to low-income populations with variable/irregular income streams. A typical product pays a fixed cash benefit on hospitalisation (e.g. minimum 3-night stay) with no individual underwriting, covering accident or illness. The social objective — maximising benefit payments to policyholders — is as important as financial sustainability. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Premium risks in microinsurance: (a) inconsistent premium collection (customers without bank accounts; stop-start payment patterns) makes it difficult to manage the expense base; (b) low per-policy premium requires large scale to cover fixed costs; (c) agent commission-based remuneration makes revenue volatile; (d) gaps in cover without re-underwriting create anti-selection risk — policyholders can reactivate cover when hospitalisation is anticipated. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Claims risks in microinsurance: (a) anti-selection from gaps in cover (policyholders restart premiums in anticipation of a claim); (b) moral hazard — policyholders may pressurise hospitals to keep them admitted for the minimum qualifying period, or leave and re-admit to make multiple claims; (c) fraud risk is higher due to inability to validate claims for low-literacy or remote populations; (d) windfall payments if benefit materially exceeds income loss or cost of hospitalisation. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Product design mitigants for microinsurance: (a) impose a waiting period when cover restarts after a gap exceeding a threshold (e.g. 90 days); (b) structure as group product via employer or community group to assist premium collection and reduce acquisition cost per life; (c) introduce cashback or loyalty incentives to drive consistent payment; (d) partner with hospitals to streamline claims validation and reduce fraud; (e) structure benefit as per-night-of-stay (not fixed lump sum) to align benefit with need and reduce windfall risk; (f) limit claims per month; (g) require a statement of good health on restart after gap. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Reviewable premium rates and benefit terms are essential in microinsurance: the insurer must be able to react quickly to adverse claims experience given the thin margins and limited capital buffer typical in this market. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Main reserves for a microinsurance hospitalisation product: IBNR (incurred but not reported — estimated from recent premium/exposure data if claims data is thin), UPR (unexpired premium reserve for in-force monthly cover), URR (unexpired risk reserve if UPR insufficient for expected claims), and RBNS (reported but not settled — including claims where the patient has not yet completed the minimum qualifying stay). [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Investigating a low claims ratio in a microinsurance product (social objective not met): examine (a) operational data — declined claims rate, claim turnaround time, percentage of claims with complete documentation, whether customers without active cover are attempting to claim; (b) lapse data — percentage lapsing during waiting period, average duration before first lapse; (c) business mix — age, gender, location, occupation versus pricing assumptions; (d) distributor feedback — consumer interest, understanding, and conversion rates at point of sale. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Options to increase claims ratio in a microinsurance product: (a) increase sum assured — raises claim amounts but not incidence; lower uncertainty as incidence rates are already known; risk of windfall/fraud if benefit significantly exceeds need; (b) relax hospitalisation definition (e.g. 2-night minimum) — increases eligible policyholders but introduces pricing uncertainty and potential windfall for short stays; (c) reduce premiums — improves affordability and may attract higher-risk segments, but erodes margin and may make the product commercially unviable; (d) education and claims awareness programme — helps customers understand coverage and submit valid claims; low capital cost but may require investment in process and communication. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Adding an outpatient benefit to a microinsurance hospitalisation product: increases comprehensiveness and reduces complaints when hospitalisation threshold is not met — but adds pricing uncertainty (limited data), increases claims administration cost disproportionately relative to the low benefit amounts typical in this market, and may require deductibles/co-insurance that are impractical for the target market. Overall commercial and customer viability is questionable unless the benefit level is material relative to out-of-pocket costs. [src:IFoA_0922]
Accident & Sickness Insurance Framework (SP1 Chapter 1)
Definition & Nature
Short-term insurance product with similarities to income protection.
Income for specified period (often one year) following deferred period, payable while claimant remains disabled (usually 'inability to perform any occupation').
Fixed lump sum may be paid if policyholder loses a limb through accident (similar to personal accident cover).
ASU insurance (Accident, Sickness, Unemployment); can include creditor insurance or payment protection insurance (PPI).
Key Characteristics
Annual renewable; insurer has opportunity to review premium and terms/conditions each year (benefits offered, deferred periods, conditions).
Benefits payable for fixed period (often 1 year) vs IP (to retirement age).
Often no deferred period or short DP (e.g., ASU covers IP's waiting period where IP unavailable/expensive).
Usually not subject to individual underwriting; useful for those unable to access long-term IP except at prohibitive rates.
Relatively low due to short duration and limited underwriting.
Distribution & Use Cases
Available through employer-sponsored schemes and affinity groups; may not be individually underwritten at group level.
Often sold alongside life/unemployment benefits to protect loan repayments (creditor insurance/PPI).
Can include critical illness or total/permanent disability definitions.
Sold alongside long-term IP where IP has long DP (e.g., 52 weeks); ASU provides short-term bridge cover.
Definition of Disability
Inability to perform any occupation (no occupational distinction as in long-term IP).
May use ADLs, ADWs, FATs, or PCAs (flexible for non-earners).
Benefits & Conditions
