Regular payments during disability within specified period.
Fixed amount on loss of limbs/specified body parts (distinct from personal accident).
Underwriting opportunity each year; terms/benefits can change.
Shorter than typical IP (if any); variable by insurer.
Relationship to Other Products
Both provide income during incapacity; both renewed annually.
Shorter benefit duration (often 1 year max), often no/short DP, minimal UW, lower premium.
Used as bridge when IP unavailable/unaffordable; covers loan repayment risk.
Fills gap for people unable to access long-term IP or seeking short-term bridge protection.
Major Medical Expenses (MME) Framework (SP1 Chapter 4)
Definition & Nature
Lump-sum payment when policyholder undergoes surgery. Benefit size varies by procedure class/severity; estimated to cover in-patient costs + incidentals/recuperation.
Benefit not guaranteed to cover extreme surgical complications within class (unless policy explicitly states + insurer has fixed-price hospital agreement).
Fixed benefit schedule limits claims-stage work (admin efficiency vs indemnity complexity).
Coverage Scope
Covers in-patient surgical procedures; does NOT cover out-patient episodes.
Limited scope (no OP) but compensated by significantly lower premium. [src:IFoA_0922]
Fixed benefit schedule simplifies claims adjudication; reduces disputes; clear eligibility.
Product Types
Annually renewable product (like PMI); insurer can review terms/benefits/premiums each year.
Reviewable premium product; multi-year terms with premium subject to review.
Comparison to Other Products
MME—requirement for one of listed operations. CI—diagnosis of specified serious illness (not necessarily operation).
Some CI benefits payable on specific operations (very serious: heart-bypass). Overlap for operation-based CI benefits; no overlap for diagnosis-based CI benefits.
CI includes life-threatening conditions not requiring operations; MME includes relief operations (non-life-threatening: hip replacement).
MME provides fixed benefit (known exposure); PMI indemnity reimburses actual costs (open-ended).
Cash plan covers multiple small benefits (dental, optical, physio); MME targets single major-cost event (surgery).
Surgical Procedure Schedule
Defined surgical procedures triggering benefit (heart-bypass, organ transplant, hip replacement, etc.).
Different procedures/classes assigned different fixed benefit amounts.
Benefit scaled to complexity/cost; simple procedures lower benefit, complex procedures higher.
Geographic Context
Term 'MME' more specific to UK market. In USA, 'major medical expenses' closer to UK 'private medical insurance' (broader coverage).
Naming/scope conventions vary by territory.
Underwriting & Claims
Fixed benefit schedule pre-determined; minimal assessment needed at claims stage. [src:IFoA_0922]
Procedure either on list or not; reduces disputes.
Varies by product variant (short-term may be less rigorous; long-term may require fuller UW).

⬡ - MME suits individuals who prefer to self-fund routine healthcare costs but want protection against catastrophic surgical costs — a rational choice for affluent or younger policyholders who view day-to-day costs as affordable but cannot absorb major one-off surgical expenses. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - Customers with pre-existing conditions may prefer MME to PMI because MME's simpler underwriting is less likely to apply condition-specific exclusions — making it accessible where comprehensive PMI would impose onerous terms. [src:IFoA_0922]

⬡ - MME sold online or direct benefits from a simpler, faster application process than PMI: fewer medical questions, fixed sum assured, and a straightforward benefit schedule reduce the underwriting and advice burden and make online distribution viable. [src:IFoA_0922]
Disability Definitions Framework (SP1 Chapters 1 & 2)
Overview
(1) Occupation-based, (2) ADL-based, (3) Functional/Skills-based (ADWs, FATs, PCAs).
Occupational definitions cannot be applied to housepersons, unemployed, or those past retirement age — non-occupational definitions fill this gap.
IP (income protection), TPD (within CI products), and LTCI.
Occupation-Based Definitions

Unable to perform the specific occupation of the insured. Most generous to policyholder; highest claims cost. Unsuitable where minor disability could trigger claim frequently (e.g. pianist with hand injury — cost would be prohibitive). Not offered to high-risk/manual occupations.

content.bold: Any Occupation Suited by Education, Training or Experience
content.text: Middle ground; potential for dispute at claims stage. Common compromise definition. Offers a fair alternative where own occ is unsuitable.

content.bold: Own Occupation (initial period) then Any Occupation
content.text: e.g. own occ for first 2 years, then switches to any occ. Balances early claim generosity with long-term cost control.
Most restrictive for policyholder; lowest claims cost for insurer. Standard for high-risk/manual occupations (e.g. building trade) where cost of broader cover is high and difficult to quantify.
Own occ (highest cost) → Suited occ → Any occ (lowest cost).
Lenient claims interpretation in practice means claims paid more generously than priced — experience monitoring must feed back into pricing (actuarial control cycle).
Offering multiple definitions extends the range of insurable lives; those ineligible for own occ can be offered an alternative.
Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)
Inability to perform (unaided) a specified number of standard everyday tasks — commonly 3 or 4 out of 6.
Feeding, Dressing, Washing, Toileting, Mobility, Transferring.
Applicable to non-workers (housepersons, unemployed, retired); usable beyond retirement age; single definition throughout policy term; less subjective.
May be harder to satisfy than an occupational definition.
TPD written on ADL basis throughout life = 'loss of independent existence'.
Typically 50% benefit on failure of 2/6 ADLs; 100% on failure of 3+/6 ADLs (or cognitive impairment). More recent plans separate ADL triggers with individual benefit levels per ADL failed.
Activities of Daily Working (ADWs)
Skills-based tests relating to workplace and daily work activities — dexterity, mobility, communication.
More objective than occupational definitions; can be linked to occupational class. Applicable to non-earners as well as employed lives.
Functional Assessment Tests (FATs)
Structured physical tests of capability; incapacity triggered by failing a specified number of tests (e.g. 3 out of 7).

Getting up from a chair unaided, standing for 10 minutes, walking 200m on flat ground, lifting a 2kg weight from counter height, walking up/down 12 stairs, bending/kneeling to floor, using hands (turning tap/knob with one hand).
Overriding test of mental incapacity usually applied alongside the physical FAT.
Applicable to unemployed and any age; easily understood by policyholders; precisely defined; easier to apply in practice; no occupation-specific expertise needed.
Personal Capability Assessments (PCAs)
Hybrid test — pays if policyholder cannot perform a specified number of defined personal capabilities OR suffers one of a list of serious illnesses.
Rising from chair, standing, walking, stairs, bending, lifting & carrying, transport (in/out of car), manual dexterity (keyboard/pen), fits & blackouts (driving licence retention).

Blindness (total permanent irreversible loss of sight in both eyes), Terminal illness (life expectancy ≤ 12 months per consultant), Complete dependency (24-hour medical supervision in hospital/nursing home).
Must fail 3+ personal capabilities OR meet a serious illness criterion.
Choosing the Right Definition — Key Considerations
Employed → occupational preferred. Non-earner/retired → ADL/FAT/PCA needed.
IP → typically occupational (own/any). TPD within CI → often ADL or any occupation. LTCI → ADL standard.
Own occ most expensive; any occ cheapest; ADL/FAT intermediate depending on design.
Suited occupation and own occupation definitions carry higher dispute risk at claims stage.
Offering all definitions extends range of insurable lives; prefer flexibility in underwriting philosophy.
Overseas & Expatriate Business
Overseas PMI — Key Risks

⬡ - Data risk: no own-experience data in a new territory; must rely on external data (national statistics, local regulator, hospital data, reinsurers) that may lack relevance or credibility. [src:IAI_0523]
⬡ - Exchange rate risk: premiums and claims may be in different currencies; adverse exchange rate movements can create significant loss if not hedged. [src:IAI_0523]
⬡ - Investment risk: assets may need to be held in unfamiliar markets with higher liquidity and regulatory risk. [src:IAI_0523]
⬡ - Accumulation/epidemic risk: expatriate communities are geographically concentrated and socially close-knit, increasing pandemic/epidemic exposure. [src:IAI_0523]
⬡ - Operational, tax, and regulatory risk: managing claims remotely, navigating unfamiliar legal frameworks, and exposure to sudden changes in tax law or regulation. [src:IAI_0523]
⬡ - Lapse risk: sudden withdrawal if exchange rate movements make premiums unaffordable for local employees covered under the scheme. [src:IAI_0523]
Overseas PMI — Risk Mitigants & Data Sources

⬡ - Data sources: national health statistics, local regulator publications, hospital expense data, competitor pricing data, reinsurer multi-national databases, and own domestic morbidity data (adjusted for local conditions). [src:IAI_0523]

⬡ - Risk mitigants: compulsory scheme membership (reduces anti-selection); reinsurance/coinsurance (risk transfer and technical assistance); strong pre-existing condition exclusions; full individual underwriting for high-risk member classes; clear terms and conditions; strong claims controls. [src:IAI_0523]

⬡ - Solvency impact of market entry: short-term deterioration from start-up costs, new business strain and capital requirements; long-term improvement if business is profitable. Capital raising or quota-share reinsurance can manage the transition. [src:IAI_0523]
Market Entry Research Framework

⬡ - Before entering a new territory for group IP: research (a) the employment landscape (industry composition, employer sizes, role of state as employer, labour market dynamics), (b) population demographics, and (c) health indicators / burden of disease in that territory. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Research the target IP market: market size and growth, product features typical in the market (deferred periods, waiting periods, disability definitions such as own occupation vs any occupation), consumer preferences, state provision of equivalent benefits, and the competitor landscape. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Assess the regulatory environment: licensing requirements, capital and solvency requirements, reserving standards, investment constraints (availability of appropriate assets), tax landscape, profit repatriation rules, and distribution/commission restrictions. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Understand the distribution landscape in the target territory: whether independent brokers or tied agents predominate — tied agents require greater ongoing investment and servicing capability and may limit distribution flexibility. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Investigate: (a) availability of reinsurance for the target product in the new territory, (b) availability of skilled actuarial and insurance professionals locally, (c) operational norms for premium collection (e.g. payroll deduction by employers). [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Carry out a comparative analysis of the home vs target territory to assess the extent to which existing pricing data, underwriting experience, systems, and processes can be transferred or adapted — reducing development cost and risk. [src:IFoA_0425]
⬡ - Assess macroeconomic conditions in the target country (GDP growth, inflation, unemployment trends) as these directly influence IP claim rates and the addressable market size. [src:IFoA_0425]
Acquisition vs Portfolio Purchase

⬡ - Acquiring the whole target company provides: existing staff, systems and operations, insurance licence, regulatory approvals, distributor relationships, and established brand — but also acquires unwanted lines of business with potentially onerous capital, regulatory, and distribution requirements. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Purchasing only the target IP book provides a pure play on the desired product line, is a cleaner option for policyholders with multiple products, avoids exposure to additional capital requirements, and may allow the acquirer to realise scale benefits by absorbing the book into existing operations. Disadvantage: the vendor may be unwilling to sell only part of the portfolio. [src:IFoA_0425]

⬡ - Key considerations in the acquisition vs book-purchase decision: cross-subsidies between product lines in the target company (which unwind on a book-only purchase), brand sentiment (negative sentiment about the company favours book-only), and whether IP is sold as a cross-sell on another product line (breaking that linkage on book purchase). [src:IFoA_0425]
Joint Venture Due Diligence

⬡ - Before entering a joint venture in a new country, a multinational insurer should assess: the JV partner's reputation, financial strength, management quality, regulatory track record, and cultural fit; the target market's size, consumer affordability, financial sophistication, and demand for the specific products; the regulatory and tax environment including reserving requirements and profit repatriation rules; data availability for pricing; reinsurance availability; and the expected profit/loss profile over the JV term given development costs and payback period. [src:IFoA_0423]

⬡ - On exit from a joint venture (e.g. after a fixed 10-year term), the buyout payment for liabilities transferred to the domestic partner should reflect: best estimate liabilities plus a risk margin for uncertainty; prudential reserves if required by local regulation; any unresolved tax/regulatory liabilities; the value of goodwill and expertise contributed by the exiting party; and practical considerations such as payment timing, currency, and whether instalments are needed. [src:IFoA_0423]
IAI Actuarial Practice Standards (APS) — Relevant to SA1 Health & Care

The Institute of Actuaries of India (IAI) issues Actuarial Practice Standards (APS) that set binding professional obligations on Fellows holding a Certificate of Practice (CoP). A material breach of an APS is itself grounds for complaint under the IAI's Professional Conduct Standards. For SA1, the relevant APS govern the role and responsibilities of the Appointed Actuary of a health insurer, peer review, CPD obligations, Embedded Value methodology, and actuarial work on social security / government health schemes.
IAI Guidance Notes (GN) — Relevant to SA1 Health & Care

Guidance Notes (GNs) are issued by IAI to provide guidance to Fellows on the application of actuarial practice in specific contexts. Unlike APS, failure to comply with a GN is not itself a ground for complaint — however, in any disciplinary proceedings, the extent of compliance with relevant GNs will be taken into account. For SA1, candidates must be aware of any GN with provisions related to health insurance, particularly GN3 (valuation of non-life/health liabilities) and GN4 (role of the Appointed Actuary for general/health insurers).
GN3 — Guidance Note on Valuation of Policy Liabilities of General Insurance Business [PRIMARY — Reserving]

GN3 provides guidance to Fellows performing actuarial valuations of policy liabilities (reserves) for general insurance business in India. It applies directly to health insurers writing indemnity-based health products (hospitalisation, OPD, group health) since such products are classified as 'general insurance' under the Insurance Act, 1938 and regulated as such by IRDAI. Standalone health insurers and general insurers writing health business must follow GN3 principles in addition to the binding requirements of APS 21.

(i) Outstanding Claims Reserves (OCR) — case reserves for reported but not yet settled claims, including an IBNER (Incurred But Not Enough Reserved) margin. (ii) IBNR Reserves — for claims incurred but not yet reported as at the valuation date. (iii) Premium Deficiency Reserve (PDR) — where unexpired premium is insufficient to cover future claims and expenses on existing policies. (iv) Unearned Premium Reserve (UPR) — the pro-rata unexpired portion of written premium. For health insurance, IBNR is particularly significant due to the short reporting lags for hospitalisation but longer lags for OPD and chronic care claims.

GN3 discusses a range of IBNR estimation methods including: (i) Chain Ladder / Development Factor Method — projects cumulative claims using historical development triangles; most widely used for health IBNR. (ii) Bornhuetter-Ferguson (BF) Method — blends the chain ladder ultimate with an a priori expected loss ratio; preferred when data is sparse or immature. (iii) Average Cost per Claim Method — separately projects claim frequency and average severity; useful when claim sizes are volatile. (iv) Expected Loss Ratio Method — relies entirely on expected loss ratios; used for very immature underwriting years. The actuary should apply multiple methods and exercise judgment in selecting the final estimate.

GN3 emphasises that reserve estimates are inherently uncertain and actuaries should, where practical, provide a range of estimates and a central (best estimate) alongside a higher prudential estimate. Sources of uncertainty specific to health insurance include: claim reporting lags (especially for cashless vs. reimbursement claims), medical inflation volatility, changes in treatment protocols, panel hospital behaviour, and changes in the insurer's claims processing efficiency.

A PDR must be established when, for any product or segment, the unearned premium is insufficient to cover expected future claims and expenses. For health insurance, PDR risk arises when: (a) claim experience deteriorates mid-year and premium rates were set too low, (b) medical inflation is higher than assumed at pricing, or (c) a systematic adverse trend (e.g., post-COVID hospitalisation surge) is identified. The AA must certify PDR adequacy in the annual solvency statement under APS 21.

GN3 stresses the importance of data quality and segmentation. For health IBNR, claims development triangles should be segmented by: product type (individual/group, indemnity/fixed benefit), claim type (cashless/reimbursement), hospital category, geography (tier 1/2/3 cities), and underwriting year. The actuary must document data limitations and adjustments and disclose these in the valuation report.

Under GN3 and APS 21 together, the AA of a health insurer must certify in the annual accounts that: (i) IBNR reserves are adequate, (ii) IBNER provisions are reasonable, and (iii) the PDR is adequate. If the AA is unable to certify adequacy, they must communicate this to the Board and to IRDAI. The AA cannot be overridden by management on reserve adequacy.

GN3 is most likely to appear in questions on reserving methodology for health insurance. Key areas: (a) choice of IBNR method and justification (chain ladder vs. BF — when is each appropriate?), (b) what a PDR is and when it arises in health insurance, (c) how data segmentation affects the quality of IBNR estimates, and (d) the AA's certification duties. Candidates should be able to critique a given IBNR calculation and suggest improvements.
GN4 — Guidance Note on Appointed Actuary for General/Health Insurance Business [PRIMARY — AA Role]

GN4 provides detailed operational guidance to actuaries serving as (or advising) the Appointed Actuary of a general insurer or health insurer in India. It is a companion guidance note to the binding APS 21 standard — where APS 21 sets the mandatory obligations of the AA, GN4 provides amplification and practical guidance on how those obligations should be discharged. Candidates must read both together.

GN4 reiterates the eligibility requirements for appointment as AA of a health insurer (as prescribed under the IRDAI Appointed Actuary Regulations 2022): Fellow of IAI, current Certificate of Practice (CoP), passed SA1 or equivalent specialist subject in health insurance, minimum 1 year of post-fellowship experience in statutory valuation of a health or general insurer, and 7 years of total experience in the sector of which at least 2 years must be post-fellowship. GN4 also provides guidance on the AA's responsibilities when first taking up the role — including reviewing predecessor AA's work, ensuring continuity of actuarial records, and notifying IAI.

GN4 provides step-by-step guidance on the AA's reserving obligations: (i) certify IBNR and IBNER adequacy in the annual solvency statement, (ii) certify the PDR, (iii) for long-duration health contracts (where payment period exceeds 4 years), certify the adequacy of the prospective reserve. The guidance emphasises that the AA should use multiple reserving methods and reconcile results before certifying, and that the certification process should be documented with an audit trail.

GN4 amplifies APS 21 by providing guidance on the AA's review of premium rates. The AA must: (i) review the pricing assumptions used by the pricing actuary, (ii) test whether current premium rates are adequate given actual claim experience, (iii) consider the impact of medical inflation and trend changes, and (iv) certify to the Board and IRDAI that rates are neither excessive nor inadequate. If the AA disagrees with management's intended rates, GN4 sets out the escalation process — ultimately to IRDAI if unresolved.

GN4 provides detailed guidance on the content and frequency of the AA's Financial Condition Report (FCR). The FCR is a key deliverable of the AA, submitted to the Board annually. For a health insurer, the FCR should cover: (i) overview of the insurance portfolio (size, mix, profitability), (ii) claims experience analysis (actual vs. expected, trend analysis), (iii) adequacy of reserves (with sensitivity analysis), (iv) premium adequacy review, (v) assessment of solvency and capital adequacy, (vi) investment policy review, and (vii) identification of key risks and risk management recommendations. The Board must formally consider and respond to the FCR.

GN4 provides detailed guidance on the AA's escalation duty — a key area for SA1 examination. If the AA believes the company's financial condition is impaired or is likely to become impaired, the AA must: (i) first advise the company's senior management, (ii) if unresolved, escalate to the Board, and (iii) if still unresolved within a reasonable time, report directly to IRDAI. The AA must inform the company before reporting to IRDAI. The AA's whistle-blowing duty overrides confidentiality obligations to the employer.

content.bold: AA's Independence and Conflict of Interest
content.text: GN4 amplifies APS 21 on independence: (i) the AA must not have any material financial interest in the insurer (e.g., shareholding), (ii) the AA must not act as AA for more than one insurer simultaneously, (iii) the AA must not take instructions from the Board or management that would compromise their statutory duties to IRDAI and policyholders, and (iv) if a conflict arises that cannot be resolved, the AA must resign. GN4 notes that the AA's primary professional duty is to policyholders and IRDAI, not to the company's shareholders.

GN4 discusses the AA's relationship with the Board, senior management, and other functions (finance, risk, compliance, investment). The AA should attend Board and Audit Committee meetings to present actuarial findings directly. The AA should maintain professional independence even if employed directly by the insurer as an internal actuary — GN4 notes that an internal AA is not a 'management actuary' and should resist pressure to shade professional judgments in management's favour.

GN4 requires the AA to maintain adequate working papers and documentation for all actuarial work, including: assumptions used and rationale, data sources and adjustments, methodology choices and alternatives considered, and the basis for key judgments. Documentation must be sufficient to allow a competent peer reviewer to re-perform and validate the work. Working papers must be retained for a minimum period (aligned with IRDAI requirements) and be available to IRDAI on request.

GN4 is the single most important GN for SA1 and is likely to be tested in almost every exam diet. Key examination themes: (a) the AA's escalation/whistle-blowing duty and the step-by-step process when solvency is impaired, (b) the content of the Financial Condition Report for a health insurer, (c) conflicts of interest — when must the AA resign?, (d) the distinction between the AA's duties as a professional actuary vs. duties as an employee, and (e) documentation requirements. Questions often present a scenario and ask candidates to identify what the AA must do — answers should reference both GN4 and APS 21.
GN3 vs GN4: Quick Comparison

FOCUS: Technical reserving guidance. Covers IBNR/IBNER/PDR/UPR methodology, data requirements, development triangle methods (chain ladder, BF, average cost), uncertainty ranges, and the AA's certification process. Most relevant when a question involves reserving methodology, choice of IBNR method, or data quality for a health insurer.

FOCUS: Role and conduct of the AA. Covers appointment eligibility, duties (reserving/pricing/solvency/FCR), independence, escalation/whistle-blowing, Board relationships, and documentation. Most relevant when a question involves what the AA should do in a given scenario, professional independence, or the AA's reporting obligations.
APS 1 — Appointed Actuary and Life Insurance Business [REFERENCE — Long-Term Health Products]

Primarily governs the Appointed Actuary of a Life Insurance company. Relevant to SA1 when a health insurer writes long-term health products (e.g., critical illness, disability income, long-term care) that have life-insurance characteristics — or when the SA1 candidate needs to contrast the AA's role in a life vs. health insurer context.

Requires use of the Gross Premium Valuation method. Each valuation parameter must have two components: (i) a best estimate, and (ii) a margin for adverse deviation. The margin must be sized to reflect the AA's confidence in the estimate and the degree of potential adverse deviation. Liabilities must be at least sufficient to enable the company to close to new business one year after the valuation date.

The AA must advise the Board on how much surplus should be distributed to policyholders vs. transferred to shareholders under Section 49 of the Insurance Act, 1938. The AA must ensure Policyholders' Reasonable Expectations (PRE) are met.

The AA must ensure the Available Solvency Margin / Required Solvency Margin ratio is reasonable given the risk profile of assets and liabilities. If solvency is impaired, the AA must advise IRDAI — informing the company first. This duty cannot be restricted by the company's constitution or instructions.

APS 1 requires a Gross Premium Valuation with explicit margins for adverse deviation — more prescriptive than APS 21's general insurer approach which relies more on the AA's judgment given the short-term nature of most health contracts. For long-term health products, a gross premium valuation methodology (as per APS 1) may be more appropriate than a IBNR/run-off approach.

APS 1 is most likely to appear when SA1 questions involve: (a) a life insurer with a health rider, (b) a health insurer writing long-term CI or disability contracts that require prospective reserving, or (c) comparison of the AA's role across life vs. health insurers. Candidates should be able to identify when a health product is 'life-like' enough to warrant APS 1 valuation methodology.
APS 9 — Continuing Professional Development (CPD) [UNIVERSAL]
Applies to all Fellows of IAI. Fellows holding a Certificate of Practice (CoP) — which includes all Appointed Actuaries — have enhanced CPD obligations.

Must complete a minimum of 24 structured CPD hours per year (April 1 to March 31), of which: at least 12 hours must be technical (actuarial knowledge — health insurance, reserving, pricing, risk management, IRDAI regulations, etc.) and at least 6 hours must be on professional obligations (ethics, conduct, professionalism).
Fellows under age 60 require 12 CPD hours per year; aged 60–70 require 9 hours; those over 70 are exempt. Excess hours may be carried forward under specified conditions.

CPD must be achieved through verifiable activities such as IAI seminars, webinars, e-learning modules, conferences, peer-reviewed research, and IAI-approved programs. Self-directed reading may count toward a portion of the requirement.
The Appointed Actuary must submit a compliance questionnaire to IAI on or before 30th June each year confirming adherence to APS 9 and other applicable APS.

APS 9 is typically tested in the context of professionalism questions — e.g., what obligations does an AA of a health insurer have, or what would constitute a breach of professional standards. The 24/12/6 hour split for CoP holders is a commonly tested fact.
APS 10 — Indian Embedded Value (IEV) [RELEVANT for IPO/Investor Disclosure]

Governs the determination of Embedded Value (EV) of insurance companies for the purpose of an IPO or stock market listing in India. Formally scoped for life insurers under the IRDAI (Issuance of Capital by Life Insurance Companies) Regulations, 2015, but adopted as the reference framework by standalone health insurers (e.g., Star Health, Niva Bupa) for IPO and investor EV disclosures.
IEV = ANAV + VIF, where ANAV (Adjusted Net Asset Value) = Free Surplus + Required Capital; and VIF (Value of In-Force Business) = PVFP − TVOG − CNHR − FCRC.

(i) Free Surplus — assets held above the required solvency margin, freely distributable to shareholders. (ii) Required Capital — capital the company must hold, typically set at 150% of the control level of solvency per IRDAI solvency norms. Market value of assets is used; intangible assets such as goodwill are excluded.

(i) PVFP — Present Value of Future Profits: after-tax projected profits from existing in-force policies, discounted at the Risk Discount Rate (RDR). (ii) TVOG — Time Value of Options & Guarantees: cost of financial options such as guaranteed renewability clauses, valued using stochastic/Monte Carlo methods. (iii) CNHR — Cost of Non-Hedgeable Risks: explicit deduction for risks that cannot be hedged in the market (e.g., pandemic risk, morbidity shock, operational risk). (iv) FCRC — Frictional Cost of Required Capital: the drag from locking capital in the business = Required Capital × (RDR − after-tax investment return on that capital).

Best estimate assumptions must be internally consistent. Stochastic models must cover all material asset classes. Tax rates must reflect actual cash flows and tax position. Frictional costs must be applied to required capital. A minimum set of sensitivities (as prescribed in Appendix C of APS 10) must be disclosed.

Since Indian health insurance is predominantly annual/short-term, VIF is highly sensitive to renewal/persistency assumptions. Medical inflation (historically 10–15% in India) is a critical claim cost assumption. IBNR reserves must be excluded from distributable profits. No-claim bonus/cumulative bonus creates a future enhanced coverage liability that must be projected. Group vs. retail segmentation requires separate treatment due to different persistency and claim patterns.

An EV calculated in compliance with APS 10 is formally called the Indian Embedded Value (IEV). The Reporting Actuary may depart from APS 10 only if the effect is not material; materiality criteria must be set by the Board and disclosed. Results must be reviewed and signed off by an independent actuarial firm (e.g., Milliman, Willis Towers Watson).

APS 10 is most likely to be examined in the context of a health insurer pursuing an IPO, or when asked to discuss how a health insurer's value is determined. Key concepts to know: IEV = ANAV + VIF, the three VIF deductions (TVOG/CNHR/FCRC), and how annual/short-term health products create specific challenges (renewal assumptions, medical inflation, absence of long-term guarantees).
APS 20 — Actuarial Practice for Social Security Programmes [RELEVANT for Government Health Schemes]

Applies to actuarial work on Social Security Programmes (SSPs) prescribed by legislation — including ESIC (Employees' State Insurance Corporation), PM-JAY/Ayushman Bharat, and state government health schemes. Covers schemes providing benefits in respect of health, sickness, maternity, disability, work-related injury, death, and unemployment. Applies to both funded and pay-as-you-go (PAYG) financing structures.

content.bold: Principles of Actuarial Practice for SSPs
content.text: Financial projections of an SSP must be based on a thorough description of the programme's provisions (coverage, eligibility, benefit levels, financing). Assumptions must be set on a best estimate basis appropriate to the purpose of the investigation. Written reports must include sensitivity analysis around key assumptions and disclose data sources, limitations, and uncertainty.

Demographic assumptions (population growth, age structure, mortality, morbidity trends), healthcare utilisation rates, medical inflation, healthcare cost trends (hospitalisation, outpatient, medicines), coverage expansion over time, scheme financing mechanism (premium rates, government subsidy levels), and administrative expense loadings.

India's PM-JAY (Ayushman Bharat) is the world's largest government-funded health insurance scheme, covering over 500 million beneficiaries with a sum insured of ₹5 lakh per family. ESIC provides health benefits to workers in the organised sector. Actuaries working for government health schemes or TPAs administering these schemes must follow APS 20 principles.

APS 20 was issued in alignment with the International Actuarial Association (IAA) Guidelines of Actuarial Practice for Social Security Programmes (adopted by the IAA Council on 21 October 2002, effective 1 January 2003).

APS 20 is most relevant when questions involve government-sponsored health schemes, actuarial work for ESIC or PM-JAY, or the interface between the private health insurer and state/social health provision. Key themes: best estimate assumptions, long-term financial sustainability of SSPs, and the actuary's reporting obligations.
APS 21 (Ver 2.00) — Appointed Actuary and General & Health Insurance Business [PRIMARY]

Applies to the Appointed Actuary of any General Insurer, Reinsurer, or Health Insurer (as defined under Section 2(6B) and 2(6C) of the Insurance Act, 1938) appointed under the IRDAI (Appointed Actuary) Regulations, 2022. Also applies to all other actuaries who interact directly or indirectly with such an AA. Updated to Version 2.00 in October 2023 following the AA Regulations 2022 which formally separated health insurance from general insurance as a distinct category.

Must be a Fellow of IAI, hold a CoP, have passed a specialisation subject in health or general insurance (i.e., SA1 or SA3), have at least 1 year post-fellowship experience in statutory valuation of a health/general insurer, and at least 7 years' total experience in the sector of which 2 years must be post-fellowship.

The AA must certify the adequacy of (i) IBNR reserves, (ii) IBNER reserves, and (iii) Premium Deficiency Reserves (PDR) in the solvency statement, both for direct and inward reinsurance business. The AA must also attach a certificate to the Annual Accounts confirming the fairness of liability claims for contracts where the claim payment period exceeds 4 years.

The AA must certify that premium rates are neither excessive nor inadequate, applying sound actuarial judgment based on both claims frequency and claim size analysis, considering the insurer's own experience and industry data where own experience is insufficient.

The AA must assess premium deficiency (existing and future policies), review the investment policy for appropriateness given the nature and term of liabilities, conduct sensitivity analysis on key assumptions, and ensure data quality is adequate and documentation sufficient.

The AA must ensure the company maintains the required solvency margin. If the solvency position is impaired or likely to become impaired, the AA must advise the company immediately and, if unresolved, report to IRDAI — but must inform the company first.
An actuary with a material financial interest in the insurer must not accept (or continue in) the AA role. The AA must not be AA to more than one insurer simultaneously.

Questions frequently ask candidates to describe the AA's role and responsibilities in a health insurer context, or to assess whether a proposed action by a company is consistent with APS 21. Key themes: independence, IBNR certification, PDR, premium adequacy, solvency reporting duty.
APS 33 (Exposure Draft 2023) — Peer Review for General/Health Insurers [PRIMARY]

Applies to Appointed Actuaries and peer reviewers of general insurers, reinsurers, and health insurers. Issued as an Exposure Draft in August 2023 for public consultation; once finalised, it will be a binding Practice Standard.

To provide an independent opinion that the AA's actuarial work is in conformity with generally accepted actuarial practice, relevant APS/GNs, and Professional Conduct Standards. The peer review enhances confidence for the Board and IRDAI.
The peer reviewer must be independent — specifically, the AA and peer reviewer must not be from the same firm. Any prior relationships between the AA and reviewer must be disclosed.

Must cover: (i) data checks, (ii) methodology review, (iii) reasonableness of assumptions, (iv) reasonableness of calculations and checks applied, and (v) compliance with applicable APS and regulations. The scope is a minimum — the peer reviewer may expand it.

The AA retains the final say on all actuarial judgments. The peer reviewer's report is advisory; the AA uses discretion in deciding whether to act on it. However, the AA must document and justify any departure from peer reviewer recommendations.
The peer review report must be shared with the insurer's Board of Directors and, if required, with IRDAI. The AA must submit a separate compliance confirmation to IAI.

Questions may ask about the purpose and process of peer review, independence requirements, the AA's relationship with the peer reviewer, or what a peer review report should cover for a health insurer. Distinguish: peer review is not an audit — it is a professional review by a Fellow actuary.
Quick Reference: APS Applicability Summary

PRIMARY — Governs the AA of every health insurer. Covers reserving (IBNR/IBNER/PDR), pricing adequacy, solvency, investment policy, and all statutory actuarial obligations of a health insurer's AA under IRDAI (AA) Regulations 2022.

PRIMARY — Governs peer review of the AA's work at a health insurer. Requires reviewer independence (different firm), minimum scope covering data/methodology/assumptions/calculations, and reporting to the Board.
UNIVERSAL — Applies to all practicing actuaries. CoP holders (incl. all AAs) must complete 24 CPD hours/year (12 technical + 6 professional). Annual compliance questionnaire to IAI by 30th June.

RELEVANT (IPO/EV) — Governs the computation of Indian Embedded Value (IEV = ANAV + VIF). Used by standalone health insurers for IPO listings and investor disclosures. VIF deductions: TVOG, CNHR, FCRC.

RELEVANT (Government Schemes) — Governs actuarial work on ESIC, PM-JAY, and other statutory health SSPs. Best estimate assumptions, long-term sustainability projections, and sensitivity analysis required.

REFERENCE (Long-Term Health) — Life insurer AA standard. Relevant when a health insurer writes long-term products (CI, disability income, LTC) requiring prospective Gross Premium Valuation with explicit margins for adverse deviation and Policyholders' Reasonable Expectations considerations.
Star Health & Allied Insurance — Products & Features

India's largest SAHI by retail GWP (~32% market share). Exclusive health insurance focus since 2006. Largest hospital network among SAHIs; strong brand recognition across tier-1 and tier-2 cities. Products span every buyer segment: youth, family, senior, chronic disease, niche conditions, PA, travel.

Super Star (modular, OPD-inclusive, newest flagship), Star Comprehensive (no disease-wise sub-limits, mid-tier workhorse), Smart Health Pro (digital/young cohort), Star Health Premier (entry up to age 75). Each tier targets a different risk and price segment — understand how they differ on room rent caps, restoration, and OPD inclusion.

Family: Health Optima (floater, newborn Day-1), Assure (up to 25 members). Youth: Young Star (sub-40, wellness rewards, short PED waits). Senior: Senior Citizens Red Carpet (entry 60–75, no pre-policy medical, 30% co-pay, PED from Year 1). Niche: Diabetes Safe (Day-1 diabetes), Cardiac Care (pre-existing cardiac from inception), Cancer Care Platinum (post-diagnosis entry), Special Care Autism — breadth of condition-specific products is unmatched among SAHIs.

(1) Modular benefit selection (Super Star) creates adverse selection — higher-risk buyers pick more modules; price each module on its own incidence. (2) Floater pool: shared SI means one large claim exhausts cover for all family members — must model family composition and claim concentration. (3) Unlimited restoration mechanics need careful definition (same vs. different illness, from 1st vs. 2nd claim). (4) Condition-specific plans (Cancer, Cardiac, Diabetes) involve near-certain or already-known risks — standard morbidity assumptions do not apply; controls are co-pay, sub-limits, and restricted entry criteria. (5) IRDAI mandates: Arogya Sanjeevani (standard indemnity) and Saral Suraksha Bima (standard PA) must be offered alongside all proprietary products.

Star is the reference benchmark insurer for Indian health insurance — exam questions comparing SAHI products will frequently use Star as the baseline. Know Super Star, Comprehensive, Senior Red Carpet, and Diabetes Safe in detail.
Flagship Comprehensive Plans
Product Overview

Star's newest flagship (launched 2023). Highly modular — policyholders select benefit modules at inception. No room rent cap. Covers OPD, mental illness, HIV/AIDS, obesity treatment. Wellness-linked renewal discounts (step-count app). Unlimited restoration of SI. Available individual & floater. Exam angle: modular design shifts adverse selection risk — buyers self-select into high-benefit modules; pricing must adjust for this selection effect.

All-round indemnity plan — the 'workhorse' mid-tier product. Pre/post-hospitalisation: 60/180 days. Covers daycare, AYUSH, organ donor, domiciliary hospitalisation. No disease-wise sub-limits (key differentiator vs. older plans). Optional add-on covers (CI, maternity, OPD). Exam angle: 'no disease-wise sub-limits' is a product design feature that reduces basis risk for the policyholder but increases claim cost variance for the insurer.

Tech-focused comprehensive plan. Built-in OPD and wellness tracking via app. Targeted at digitally savvy, younger-entry cohort. Competitive pricing achievable because younger lives have lower baseline morbidity. Exam angle: age-band targeting is a form of risk selection — concentrating healthy young lives improves portfolio experience in short run but creates adverse selection as cohort ages.

Premium-tier plan targeting senior-adjacent buyers (entry up to age 75). High SI options available. No room rent capping. Designed for policyholders who need long-term comprehensive cover. Exam angle: extending entry to age 75 without co-payment requires very careful morbidity loading — experience at these ages is thinner and more volatile.
Key Design Features to Know
No room rent cap = no sub-limit on hospital room category; policyholder can choose any room without proportionate deduction.
Modular benefit selection (Super Star) = policyholder chooses which benefit modules to include at inception. Creates adverse selection concern — higher-risk buyers select more modules.
Unlimited restoration = SI is automatically refilled after exhaustion; can be used for same or different illness depending on plan variant.
Individual & Family Floater Plans
Floater Plans

One of Star's highest-selling family floater plans. Single shared SI pool for all family members. Covers newborn from day 1 (if mother insured 12+ months). Auto-restoration of SI after exhaustion. Maternity extension available as add-on. Entry: 3 months – 65 years; lifelong renewal. Exam angle: shared SI floater introduces within-family moral hazard — any member can exhaust the pool; actuarial pricing must account for the correlation of claims across family members.

Mid-market floater covering up to 25 family members on one policy (unique in Indian market). Unlimited restoration of SI. Wellness program and annual health check-up included. Targeted at large joint families. Exam angle: covering 25 members under one pool creates compound risk — expected claims scale with member count, but SI is a shared fixed pool. Premium must reflect both count and age mix.
Individual Plans

Long-standing individual indemnity plan — one of Star's original products. Covers inpatient, pre/post-hospitalisation, daycare. No disease-wise sub-limits (simplified claims management). Entry: 16–65 years; lifelong renewal guaranteed. Exam angle: as a legacy product, Medi Classic illustrates product persistence — many policyholders have held it for decades, meaning the insurer carries an older, potentially higher-morbidity block of business.
Exam Angles — Floater vs. Individual
Floater: shared pool means one large claim can exhaust SI for all — moral hazard and claim concentration risk. Premium must reflect family composition (ages, PED mix).
Individual: each member has own SI — more predictable per-head cost but total premium is higher. Preferred by policyholders who want to protect against one member exhausting the family pool.
Newborn-from-day-1 cover (Family Health Optima) is an anti-lapse mechanism — it ties the policyholder to renewal continuity.
Segment-Specific Plans
Youth Segment

Two variants: Silver and Gold. Exclusively for sub-40 buyers — captures early life-stage onboarding. Wellness points (from step-count app) redeemable for renewal premium discounts. Lower waiting periods than standard plans for PEDs. Exam angle: early life-stage product lowers adverse selection — younger, healthier cohort subsidises the pool. However, as these policyholders age, morbidity rises while renewals must be offered (guaranteed renewability). Long-tail pricing risk.
Senior Segment

Entry: 60–75 years. No pre-policy medical required (unique for senior product). 30% co-payment on all claims. Covers pre-existing diseases from Year 1 — but with the 30% co-pay as the anti-selection control. Disease-wise sub-limits apply to contain costs on common high-cost conditions. Exam angle: the combination of (1) no pre-medical, (2) PED cover from Year 1, and (3) co-payment + sub-limits is the actuarial solution — it widens access while mechanically managing adverse selection and moral hazard. The co-pay shifts moral hazard control to the policyholder.
Niche Segments

Women-specific floater. Covers maternity, infertility treatment, breast/cervical cancer screening. Personal accident cover included (covers entire family). Unique: covers surrogacy expenses. Exam angle: surrogacy coverage is near-unique in Indian market — pricing must use very thin data; significant model risk. Infertility cover (ART) involves high-cost treatments with high moral hazard — usually capped by sub-limit.

Low-cost micro-insurance for rural/agricultural populations. SI: ₹1–5 lakh. Simplified underwriting (no medical, minimal documentation). Meets IRDAI social obligation mandate (every insurer must write a proportion of rural/social sector business). Distributed via microfinance institutions (MFIs) and NBFCs. Exam angle: IRDAI's social obligation requirements force cross-subsidy from profitable lines. Simplified underwriting increases adverse selection risk; kept viable by low SI caps and simple benefit structures.
Disease / Condition-Specific Plans
Chronic Disease Plans

Covers Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes and related complications from Day 1 (no PED waiting period for diabetes). Individual and floater (self + spouse only — limited to 2 members). Also includes personal accident cover. Exam angle: Day-1 diabetes cover creates strong adverse selection — only people with diabetes buy it. Limiting pool to 2 members controls the size of the adverse selection pool. Elevated morbidity loading is essential. Pricing relies heavily on diabetic complication incidence rates (renal failure, neuropathy, retinopathy) which are not well-observed in Indian data.

content.bold: Star Cardiac Care / Cardiac Care Platinum
content.text: Covers cardiac AND non-cardiac ailments in the same plan. Standard: SI up to ₹4L. Platinum: SI up to ₹15L, broader benefit scope. Covers pre-existing cardiac conditions from policy start. First insurer globally to launch a cardiac-specific plan (2013 — market innovation). Exam angle: covering pre-existing cardiac conditions eliminates the standard PED exclusion mechanism — anti-selection is managed instead through premium loading, co-payment, and SI caps. Illustrates the actuarial challenge of pricing pre-existing disease cover with thin Indian cardiac incidence data.
Cancer & Specialist Plans

Covers first cancer diagnosis plus recurrence and a second primary cancer. Covers full treatment costs: chemotherapy, radiotherapy, surgery, targeted therapy, immunotherapy. Entry restricted to those already diagnosed with cancer. Exam angle: this is the most extreme adverse selection scenario — the insured event has already occurred. This is not insurance in the traditional sense (no uncertainty of loss) — it is a funding mechanism. Pricing = discounted expected treatment cost + expenses + margin. The 'risk' priced is treatment cost variability and survival duration, not diagnosis probability.

content.bold: Star Special Care / Special Care Platinum (Autism)
content.text: Covers children 3–25 years diagnosed with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD). Covers therapy, behavioural intervention (ABA), OT, speech therapy, and hospitalisation. Gold and Platinum tiers. Unique in Indian market — no other SAHI offers a comparable standalone autism plan. Exam angle: ASD is a developmental condition requiring long-term recurring therapy costs rather than acute hospitalisation. Pricing requires different actuarial model — frequency × session cost × duration, not frequency × severity (hospitalisation). Data is extremely thin in India.
Top-up, OPD & Supplementary Plans
Super Top-up Plans

Super top-up plans — activate when aggregate annual claims exceed the chosen deductible. Key distinction: super top-up uses aggregate annual claims as trigger (vs. top-up which requires a single claim to exceed threshold). No medical screening required at entry. SI options up to ₹95 lakh above the deductible. Cost-efficient route to achieving very high total cover. Exam angle: aggregate trigger means pricing must model the total annual claim distribution, not just the probability of a single large claim. Must consider correlated claims within the year (same illness requiring multiple hospitalisations).
OPD & Cash Plans

Standalone OPD plan. Covers consultations, diagnostics, pharmacy, physiotherapy, dental and vision (basic). AYUSH OPD included. Exam angle: OPD is the most challenging health insurance product line to price in India. (1) High frequency, low severity — moral hazard is dominant. (2) Indian OPD utilisation data is extremely thin — most insurers lack credible own-experience data. (3) OPD demand is elastic — coverage drives utilisation. Pricing approaches: annual sub-limit, per-consultation cap, network-only access, and deductible per visit.

Daily cash benefit (₹500–₹10,000/day) per day of hospitalisation — fixed benefit, independent of actual bills. ICU benefit: 2× daily rate. Covers up to 60 days per year per illness. Exam angle: fixed benefit products do not require bill verification, reducing claims processing cost and fraud detection complexity. However, they create a moral hazard incentive to extend hospitalisation beyond medical necessity. Counter-controls: minimum hospitalisation duration (24 hrs) and per-illness day cap (60 days).

Lump-sum benefit on diagnosis of listed critical illnesses. 'Multipay' feature: allows multiple claims across different CI categories within the policy tenure (benefit reinstated after 12 months if a different CI is diagnosed). Exam angle: multipay CI products are complex to price. Must model joint CI incidence with non-overlapping definitions to avoid double-counting. Need to estimate probability of a second independent CI event conditional on surviving the first — requires joint survival/incidence modelling.
Personal Accident & Travel Plans
Personal Accident

content.bold: Accident Care / Family Accident Care / Trauma Care
content.text: Covers accidental death (AD), permanent total disability (PTD), permanent partial disability (PPD), and temporary total disability (TTD). Trauma Care additionally covers hospitalisation expenses for accident injuries. Available individually and as family floater. Saral Suraksha Bima: IRDAI-mandated standard PA product — all insurers must offer; identical benefit structure across market. Exam angle: PA products are priced on accident incidence and disability rates, not morbidity. The mandated Saral Suraksha Bima removes competitive differentiation at the base level — product differentiation only possible in enhanced/supplementary PA products.
Travel Plans

content.bold: Star Travel Protect / Student Travel Protect / Corporate Travel
content.text: Trip-specific cover: medical emergency, evacuation, trip cancellation, baggage loss, passport loss. Student plan: adds study interruption and academic penalty cover. Corporate plan: designed for group employee travel policies. Exam angle: short-term nature means pricing relies heavily on global claims databases (own Indian experience is minimal). Pricing must account for destination-specific risk (medical cost profiles vary greatly by country). Reinsurance typically required for evacuation and large medical costs in countries with expensive healthcare (USA, Western Europe).
Key Exam Angles — PA & Travel
IRDAI mandates Saral Suraksha Bima (standard PA) and Arogya Sanjeevani (standard health) — these baseline products constrain competitive differentiation.
Travel insurance: short-duration, low lapse risk, but requires international claims management infrastructure.
PTD/PPD definitions are critical — partial disability payments require objective medical assessment and may be disputed. Clear definitions reduce claims litigation.
Niva Bupa Health Insurance — Products & Features

India's second-largest SAHI (~18% retail market share). Joint venture between Bupa (UK, global health specialist) and Fettle Tone LLP (Manipal Group). Bupa's international experience influences product design and data practices. Strong urban and HNI presence; known for claims service quality.

ReAssure 3.0 (current flagship): four room-tier variants (Classic → Select → Elite → Black) — room tier determines both entitlement and premium band. ReAssure Forever: SI reinstated after even the first paid claim, unlimited times — strongest restoration mechanic in the market. Legacy blocks: ReAssure 2.0 and Heartbeat still on large in-force portfolios, creating a product maintenance and pricing continuity obligation for the AA.

PED waiver option for 145 conditions (at extra premium) — deliberate adverse selection facilitation controlled by loading. Aspire: savings-linked cashback plan (claim-free years earn credits against renewal premium). Senior First: 50–30% co-pay structure for ages 61–75. Safeguard+ add-on: guarantees zero deductions at pre-authorised network hospitals. NivaBupaOne: premium service overlay (not insurance) targeting HNI/corporate.

(1) Room-tier pricing: each tier creates a different expected claims cost (room rate differential, private vs. shared ward). Must model the room-cost multiplier per tier. (2) ReAssure Forever (unlimited restoration from claim 1): stronger than competitors — liability is the full annual SI for every policyholder who claims; model frequency carefully. (3) PED waiver for 145 conditions: transforms underwriting — the insurer knowingly accepts adverse risks and must price the expected additional claims cost into the loading. (4) Legacy block management: in-force Heartbeat/ReAssure 2.0 policyholders cannot be lapsed — guaranteed renewability. Premium adjustment is the only lever; AA must monitor ICR by generation. (5) Senior co-pay ladder: co-pay reduces moral hazard but also shifts financial burden to policyholder — IRDAI requires fair balance.

Niva Bupa's ReAssure series is the strongest exam example of iterative product evolution — each generation adds features on top of the last, creating complex multi-generation in-force management for the AA.
ReAssure Series (Flagship)
Current Generation

Current flagship (2024). Four room-tier variants: Classic (shared/general ward), Select (twin sharing), Elite (private room; partial global cover), Black (any room incl. suite; full global cover; zero co-pay). The room tier determines room entitlement AND premium band — a direct reflection of expected hospital costs. Unlimited reinstatement of SI after first paid claim ('ReAssure Forever'). Walk-based step-count discount: up to 30% off renewal premium. Option to waive PED waiting period for 145 conditions. Entry: 18–65 years; lifelong renewal. Exam angle: room-tier structuring allows Niva Bupa to price-segment by expected claim cost and risk appetite without creating entirely separate products — one underwriting framework, four price points.
ReAssure Forever = SI reinstated after even the first paid claim, unlimited times. This is a stronger restoration benefit than most competitors (Star, Care Health) who activate from 2nd claim.

PED waiver option for 145 conditions (at extra premium) removes the standard 2–4 year waiting period. Exam angle: this is a form of adverse selection facilitation — buyers with known PEDs are being accommodated at a loaded premium. Pricing requires morbidity loading specific to the waived condition.
Legacy Generations

Still in-force for large policyholder bases. ReAssure 2.0: unlimited SI carryforward (up to 10× base), no room rent cap, 60/180 day pre/post-hospitalisation. Illustrates iterative product evolution — each version layers new features rather than replacing the product outright. Exam angle: pricing continuity challenge on renewals — the insurer must renew at the same or closely comparable premium whether the policyholder is on the older or newer version. 'Same-version' renewal expectations create a constraint on future premium revisions.
Heartbeat Series
Product Details

Older flagship — largely superseded by ReAssure for new sales but remains relevant as a large in-force block. Individual and floater variants. Core features: SI refill on exhaustion for a different illness, maternity add-on (floater only), annual health check-up from Year 1, 100% SI restoration for a different illness. Exam angle: legacy in-force block creates a data credibility issue — claims experience on Heartbeat is from an older cohort that has been selecting against the insurer over time (healthier lives will have lapsed or switched via portability). The remaining block may be adversely selected.
Exam Angles — Legacy Product Blocks

Portability (IRDAI-mandated) allows policyholders to transfer to a competitor while retaining PED waiting period credit. The acquiring insurer inherits a policyholder whose accumulated waiting period credit reduces its future claim deferral protection.

Legacy blocks with guaranteed renewability cannot be lapsed by the insurer — even if the block becomes loss-making. The only pricing lever is renewal premium adjustment within IRDAI's approved rate range.
Aspire & Rise Plans
Premium/Savings Plans

Premium plan with a savings/cashback design mechanic. Policyholders accumulate cashback for claim-free years — usable against future renewal premiums, deductibles, or OPD spend. Multi-year policy option (2–3 years) with combined SI across the full tenure. Gold+ variant includes maternity benefit. Exam angle: cashback/reward mechanics change policyholder behaviour — they incentivise lower small-claim submission (to protect the cashback balance). This mechanically reduces the insurer's frequency of small claims, improving the loss ratio on the cashback tranche. However, the deferred liability (cashback reserves) must be recognised in the insurer's balance sheet.
Mass-Market Plans

Entry-level comprehensive plan for mass-market buyers. Simpler benefit structure with lower SI ceiling. Designed to compete with general insurer health plans on price while offering the network and claims service of a SAHI. Exam angle: price competition in the mass market compresses margins — SAHI must leverage superior claims management and network hospital pricing to maintain viability at lower premium rates.
Senior Citizen Plan
Senior First — Structure & Exam Angles

Entry: 61–75 years. Gold variant: 50% co-payment (reducible to 30–40% over time). Platinum variant: ReAssure Forever active after first claim; NCB not reduced on claim. Annual health check-up from Day 1. Deductible option available to reduce premium. Exam angle: co-payment at senior ages is the insurer's primary tool for controlling both (a) moral hazard — the policyholder shares the cost and moderates utilisation, and (b) adverse selection — makes the plan less attractive to very high-utilisation individuals. The 50% co-payment on Gold is high by Indian market standards — reflects the extreme morbidity loading required for the 61–75 age band.
Senior Pricing Challenges
Age 61–75: morbidity rates rise sharply (typically 3–5× younger-age rates for hospitalisation). Limited Indian senior morbidity data — heavy reliance on reinsurer tables.
No pre-policy medical (in some senior products) = no data at underwriting stage. Anti-selection controlled entirely through co-pay, sub-limits, and PED waiting periods.
Guaranteed renewability at senior ages creates a long-tail liability — premium must be set conservatively at entry to fund future renewals.
Specialised / Companion Plans
Mid-Market & High-Value Plans

Mid-market workhorse plan. Three variants: Individual, Family Floater (shared SI pool), and Family First (separate SI per member). AYUSH, domiciliary, daycare covered. Exam angle: Family First variant gives each member their own SI — eliminates the risk of one member exhausting the pool. However, it increases the insurer's maximum liability vs. a floater. Pricing: Family First is priced per-member, not as a floater — actuarially cleaner but less attractive to families with healthy members who would 'subsidise' the sick member in a floater.

Premium-tier individual plan. Silver: ₹5–50L SI. Gold: ₹1–3 crore SI. Global emergency cover included. No room rent cap. Targets high-income individuals with international exposure. Exam angle: very high SI (₹3 crore) shifts the risk profile to catastrophic but low-frequency events. Data is extremely thin at these SI levels — pricing requires heavy reinsurer input and wide confidence intervals on expected loss.
Bundled Products

Combo products. Money Saver = Health Companion + Health Recharge (top-up) in one purchase. Super Saver = Pulse + Recharge. Bundling achieves lower effective premium for equivalent total cover. Exam angle: bundling masks the deductible structure — equivalent to buying a super top-up separately but presented as a single product. From a pricing perspective, the insurer must ensure the bundled premium reflects the marginal risk of the top-up layer above the base plan's deductible — these are not independent policies priced in isolation.
Critical Illness & Hospital Cash
Fixed Benefit Products

Covers 20 critical illnesses. Lump-sum benefit on diagnosis (fixed benefit — not linked to actual treatment cost). CI list includes: cancer, heart attack, stroke, kidney failure, major organ transplant, coma, and others. Standard 90-day survival clause (policyholder must survive 90 days after diagnosis to receive benefit). Exam angle: CI benefit = incidence rate × (1 − survival-clause lapse rate) × sum assured. The 90-day survival clause reduces payable claims (those diagnosed with terminal CIs who die quickly are excluded) — this is both actuarially and ethically significant.

Daily fixed benefit per hospitalisation day. Fixed benefit — no bills or receipts required. ICU: higher daily rate (typically 2×). Useful for income replacement and incidentals not covered under an indemnity plan. Exam angle: hospital cash is a frequency-driven product. The key pricing assumption is average annual hospitalisation days (not just whether a hospitalisation occurs). Moral hazard = extending length of stay. Counter-control: maximum days per year and per illness, and minimum hospitalisation duration threshold (usually 24 hrs).
Riders, Add-ons & Wellness
OPD & Chronic Management

Add-on to ReAssure 2.0. Covers OPD chronic disease management for diabetes, hypertension, asthma. Gold variant: unlimited teleconsultations + pharmacy cover. Bridges the indemnity/OPD gap for chronic conditions. Exam angle: chronic disease management programs (CDMPs) are designed to reduce downstream hospitalisation costs by better controlling chronic conditions in the community. The actuarial bet is that the cost of the CDMP (consultations, medications) is less than the hospitalisation cost savings from better-controlled chronic disease.

OPD/wellness extension. Unlimited tele/video consultations, physical consultations, lab tests, pharmacy. SI up to 5× of premium paid. Reflects IRDAI's push toward comprehensive health (not just hospitalisation). Exam angle: OPD utilisation is highly elastic — unlimited consultations create strong moral hazard. The 5× of premium SI cap is a critical cost control mechanism; it limits the maximum OPD liability to a multiple of premium received.
Premium Service & Claims Differentiators

Zero-deduction cashless guarantee. If policyholder follows the pre-authorisation protocol (48hr prior intimation + recommended network hospital), Niva Bupa guarantees no deductions from the admissible claim amount. Exam angle: this is a claims service differentiator — it addresses the major policyholder complaint of post-claim deductions. From an insurer perspective, it creates tighter pre-authorisation controls as the only mechanism to manage claims cost (cannot deduct post-admission). Requires robust fraud detection at pre-authorisation stage.

Not a separate insurance policy — a service overlay. Provides: Executive Health Assessment, priority claims support, dedicated personal care manager. Available as an add-on to qualifying plans. Targets HNI and corporate segment. Exam angle: service-tier differentiation is a non-benefit mechanism to retain high-value policyholders. Premium charged for NivaBupaOne must cover incremental service costs — not an insurance risk.
Care Health Insurance — Products & Features

Third-largest SAHI (~14% retail market share). Formerly Religare Health Insurance; rebranded Care Health after Manipal Group acquisition. Strong retail and mid-market presence. Known for innovation in renewal bonus mechanics and maternity/reproductive health products.

Care (original flagship): floater with unlimited SI auto-recharge (same or different illness — rare breadth). Care Classic: zone-based pricing (metro vs. non-metro) to remove geographic cross-subsidy. Care Supreme: Infinity Bonus — SI increases 100% each year, non-reducible on claim (SI at Year 5 = 6× base). Ultimate Care: India's first money-back health plan (5 claim-free years → Year 1 premium refunded; SI doubles after 7 claim-free years). These bonus mechanics are the primary exam focus.

PED-friendly: Care Supreme Shine (PED cover from Day 30), Care Freedom (diabetes/hypertension from Day 30, consumables in-built). Maternity: Joy plan (9-month wait, newborn + vaccinations), Surrogacy and Oocyte Care (near-unique in market). Senior: Senior Health Advantage (entry 61+, 20–30% co-pay, 2-year PED wait). Micro: Grameen Care (SI ₹50k–₹2L, MFI-distributed, meets IRDAI rural mandate). HNI: Care Advantage (SI up to ₹6 crore, global cover).

(1) Infinity Bonus (non-reducible 100% annual SI increase): at Year 5, SI is 6× original — premium was set for ₹5L but exposure is now ₹30L. The growing differential must be pre-funded in pricing. (2) Zone pricing (Care Classic): models geographic cost variation directly — removes cross-subsidy between metro and rural policyholders; improves pricing accuracy but requires zone-level claims data. (3) Money-back mechanics (Ultimate Care): transforms health insurance into a savings-linked product. Must reserve for both the claim probability AND the premium refund probability simultaneously. (4) Day-30 PED cover (Supreme Shine, Freedom): deliberate adverse selection; controlled by underwriting at entry, sub-limits, and co-pay. (5) Surrogacy/oocyte pricing: extremely thin data — reinsurer benchmarks plus conservative loading required; AA certification of 'fair premium' is challenging.
Care Health's Infinity Bonus and zone-based pricing are the two most-tested Care-specific mechanics in SA1 exam questions — ensure you can explain their actuarial implications clearly.
Core Comprehensive Plans
Core Plan — Care (Flagship)

Family floater indemnity plan — single shared SI pool. Covers inpatient, pre/post-hospitalisation (60/180 days), daycare, domiciliary, AYUSH. NCB: 10% per claim-free year up to 50% (reduced on claim). Unlimited auto-recharge of SI for the same or different illness — rare feature that most competitors restrict to different illness only. Unlimited teleconsultations. Entry: 91 days with no upper age limit. Exam angle: unlimited auto-recharge for same illness is a strong differentiator. Standard plans recharge only for a different illness. Pricing must model multi-event scenarios where the same illness causes repeated hospitalisations — e.g. cancer requiring multiple chemo/radiation cycles.

Zone-based pricing calibrated to city of hospitalisation: Zone 1 (metros/major cities), Zone 2 (Tier 1 cities), Zone 3 (rest of India). Same benefit structure as Care. Exam angle: zone pricing directly addresses the geographic cross-subsidy problem. Metro hospitalisation costs are 30–50% higher per DRG than non-metro. Without zone pricing, metro policyholders are under-priced and non-metro policyholders over-priced. Zone pricing creates an arbitrage risk — policyholders may register at a non-metro address to access lower Zone 3 premiums but then claim in metros.

Individual plan for adults 36+ with no upper age limit. No co-payment. Unlimited recharge for same or different illness. OPD cover available. Inflation Shield: SI auto-increases each year at CPI to counter medical inflation. Earn & Burn loyalty rewards. Exam angle: Inflation Shield is an actuarially significant feature — the insurer pre-commits to increasing SI at CPI without a proportional premium increase in the same year. Must be priced to fund the growing SI exposure over the policy lifetime; a form of deferred liability.

High sum insured plan — SI up to ₹6 crore. Targets HNI and affluent buyers. Global treatment cover. Unlimited recharge. No room rent cap. Exam angle: very high SI (₹6 crore) means the insurer retains catastrophic tail risk. Premium at ₹6 crore SI must account for: (a) thin claims data at extreme SI; (b) higher expected specialist/surgical costs for high-income policyholders who seek premium care; (c) global treatment cover costs (must use international claims data). Reinsurance essential for this block.
Care Supreme Series
Core Supreme Plans

Premium comprehensive plan. Infinity Bonus: SI increases by 100% each renewal year regardless of claims (not reducible on claim — permanent feature). Unlimited recharge. No room rent cap. OPD add-on available. Covers mental illness, HIV/AIDS, obesity/bariatric surgery. Exam angle: Infinity Bonus is one of the most aggressive SI growth mechanics in the Indian market. Unlike standard NCB (which is lost on claim), the Infinity Bonus is non-reducible. This creates an exponentially growing SI liability — a policyholder who holds the plan for 5 years has 6× the original SI. Pricing must fully fund this liability accumulation from Year 1.

Budget variant of Supreme — same core benefit structure but lower SI ceiling and some add-ons excluded by default. Targets price-sensitive buyers who value the Care brand's claims service. Exam angle: tiering allows the insurer to capture a wider price spectrum without creating a completely separate underwriting framework. Cross-selling opportunity: Value policyholders may upgrade to Supreme on renewal as income grows.

Variant for buyers with pre-existing conditions — covers listed PEDs (diabetes, hypertension) from Day 30 (not the standard 2–4 year wait). Exam angle: short PED waiting period is the key differentiator. This variant deliberately targets people with known PEDs — pure adverse selection facilitation, managed through premium loading. The loading must be sufficient to cover the expected cost of the PED claims in Years 1–4 that a standard plan would have deferred through its waiting period. Thin Indian PED-specific morbidity data makes this pricing challenging.
Top-up Within Supreme

Top-up layer on the Supreme base plan. Activates above a deductible. Allows policyholders to self-retain smaller claims and insure only the catastrophic layer. Exam angle: top-up vs. super top-up distinction. Supreme Enhance is a top-up (single-event trigger) rather than a super top-up (aggregate trigger). Single-event trigger is simpler to price but less protective against multiple moderate claims in a year.
Ultimate Care Series
Money-Back Mechanics

India's first money-back health insurance plan. Premium payback mechanic: if 5 consecutive claim-free years, the first year's base premium is refunded. Loyalty Benefit: SI doubles after 7 claim-free years (one-time in policy lifetime). Unlimited Care feature: covers one unlimited single claim in the policy lifetime (no SI cap for that one event). Exam angle: money-back health insurance changes lapse behaviour — policyholders are financially incentivised to retain the policy to qualify for the payback. This creates a more persistent, long-duration policyholder base. However, it also creates a deferred liability (premium payback reserve) that must be recognised from policy inception. The 'unlimited single claim' benefit creates an extreme tail risk — must be capped in reinsurance.

Senior-specific variant of Ultimate Care (entry: 60+). Smart Select add-on, co-pay and deductible options to manage premium for older buyers. Exam angle: extending money-back mechanics to seniors is actuarially challenging. Senior claims frequency is high — the probability of going 5 years without a claim is low, making the payback a less likely payout but still a liability that must be reserved.
Senior, Maternity & Niche Plans
Senior Plans

Entry: 61+ years. Co-payment options: 20–30% (reduces moral hazard and adverse selection at senior ages). Covers PEDs after 2 years (shorter than the standard 4-year IRDAI wait for senior products). Annual health check-up from Day 1. Air ambulance add-on available. Exam angle: 2-year PED waiting period (vs. 4-year standard) is a competitive differentiator for the 61+ segment. Pricing must frontload the expected PED claim cost into Years 1–2 premium if the plan is to remain financially viable.
Maternity & Reproductive Health

Standalone maternity plan. Covers normal delivery and C-section, newborn cover up to SI, first-year vaccinations. Waiting period: 9 months. Exam angle: maternity is a near-certain event for the target cohort (women planning a family), not a random risk. This makes it fundamentally different from other health insurance. Anti-selection controls are essential: (1) 9-month waiting period prevents buying when already pregnant; (2) SI sub-limits (₹50k–₹2L) cap the insurer's maximum liability; (3) claim count limit (typically 2 deliveries). Without these, the product would attract only pregnant women and become immediately loss-making.

Covers surrogacy-related medical expenses and oocyte (egg) donation procedures. Near-unique product in Indian market. Addresses a specific growing segment (infertility increasing with lifestyle changes, late marriages). Exam angle: data for pricing is extremely thin — limited Indian or global claims experience for surrogacy insurance. Model risk is high. Pricing relies heavily on unit cost data from fertility clinics and assumed utilisation rates. Sub-limits essential to cap liability.
Chronic & Micro-Insurance

Designed for buyers with diabetes or hypertension as PEDs. Covers these PEDs from Day 1 (30-day waiting period only vs. 2–4 years standard). Consumables (gloves, syringes, bandages) covered in-built. Exam angle: Day-1 diabetes/hypertension cover is strong adverse selection facilitation — managed through premium loading. The large Indian diabetic/hypertensive population (250M+ diabetic/pre-diabetic) creates a potentially large pool, improving data credibility over time. However, complication onset rates (e.g. diabetic nephropathy leading to dialysis) carry high severity and must be explicitly loaded.

Micro-insurance product. SI: ₹50k–₹2L. Premium under ₹2,000/year. Simple structure — inpatient only, no daycare. Distributed through MFIs and rural SHG networks. Meets IRDAI rural/social sector obligation. Exam angle: micro-insurance is a cross-subsidy product — profitable urban lines subsidise rural micro-insurance. The low premium leaves minimal margin for claims variance. Simplified underwriting means adverse selection is managed primarily through SI caps, not medical screening.
Top-up, Travel & Accident Plans
Super Top-up Plans

Aggregate-trigger super top-up plans. Activate when total claims in a policy year exceed the chosen deductible. Intended to complement the base Care plan — policyholder holds a lower-SI base plan + a Super Mediclaim layer for catastrophic coverage at lower combined premium. Exam angle: the aggregate trigger (super top-up) is more favourable to the policyholder than a per-event trigger (top-up) — it covers multiple moderate claims that collectively breach the deductible. Pricing requires full aggregate claim distribution modelling, not just the severity tail.
Student & Travel Plans

For students studying abroad. Covers medical emergencies, mental health hospitalisation, medical evacuation. Unlimited OPD option. Designed to meet visa/university requirements for international student health cover. Exam angle: student plans are priced on a young, relatively healthy demographic (low baseline morbidity) but with significant country-of-study risk exposure. A student studying in the USA has vastly higher potential medical costs than one in Europe — country-of-study must be a rating factor.

International travel plan. Covers medical emergency, evacuation, trip cancellation, lost baggage, passport loss. Explore Advantage: higher SI and broader cancellation cover.
