Explore Protect Plus: extends to global hospitalisation for India-diagnosed illness treated abroad. Exam angle: travel insurance is short-term (per trip or annual multi-trip). Pricing is highly dependent on trip duration, destination, and age. COVID-19 and pandemic exclusions are now standard after 2020 experience.
Mandated Standard Products

IRDAI-mandated standard personal accident product. Fixed benefit for accidental death (AD), permanent total disability (PTD), permanent partial disability (PPD), and temporary total disability (TTD). Standardised terms across all insurers — no product differentiation possible. Exam angle: mandatory standard products (Saral Suraksha Bima and Arogya Sanjeevani) are designed by IRDAI to ensure consumer comparability. They eliminate product differentiation at the base level, forcing competition to occur on price and service rather than benefit structure.
Aditya Birla Health Insurance (ABHICL) — Products & Features

Fourth-largest SAHI (~12% retail market share). Joint venture between Aditya Birla Capital and MMI Holdings (South Africa). Strategically differentiated by wellness-linked premium rewards — the only SAHI to offer up to 100% of annual premium back through the HealthReturns™ mechanic. Positioned as 'health first, not just sickness'.

Activ One NXT (current flagship): three variants (Value, Prime, Apex) with Day-1 chronic cover for 7 conditions (diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, asthma, obesity, CAD, COPD), in-built OPD, and HealthReturns™ up to 100% of premium. Activ Health Platinum Enhanced: established the wellness-integrated brand. Activ One MAX: SI grows to 6× base by Year 6 (Super Credit). Legacy: Activ One VIP/VIP+ still large in-force.

Policyholders earn Active Dayz™ points by meeting daily step-count targets (app/wearable). Points convert to HealthReturns™ credits redeemable against renewal premium, OPD spend, or pharmacy bills. Up to 100% of base premium returnable in top-tier plans. Creates a deferred premium discount liability that must be reserved for. Key actuarial debate: selection effect (already-healthy people sign up and earn credits easily) vs. causal effect (coverage incentivises genuinely better health behaviour and lower future claims). Evidence leans toward selection effect dominating.

(1) Day-1 chronic cover for 7 conditions: the most comprehensive PED-at-inception offering among SAHIs. Controlled by pre-policy medical, underwriting, and risk-stratified premiums per condition. (2) HealthReturns™ liability: up to 100% of premium becomes a contingent credit — must be reserved as a deferred discount; if selection effect dominates, the reserve is partially offset by lower expected claims. (3) Activ Care senior tiers (co-pay 0%/10%/20%): co-pay creates a pricing ladder — must model the behavioural response (how much does 20% co-pay reduce utilisation?). Entry up to age 80 — highest in the market — requires extreme morbidity loading. (4) SI growth to 6× by Year 6 (Activ One MAX): same long-tail liability challenge as Care Infinity Bonus — price at inception must fund future exposure growth. (5) IRDAI 2047 Wellness Vision: ABHICL is the primary exam example for wellness-integrated product design.
ABHICL's HealthReturns™ is the most-tested wellness mechanic in SA1 — be ready to discuss both the reserving treatment and the selection vs. causal effect debate in any product design question.
Activ One Series (Flagship)
Current Generation — Activ One NXT

Three variants: Value, Prime, Apex. Day-1 chronic cover for 7 conditions: diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, asthma, obesity, CAD (coronary artery disease), COPD. OPD included in-built. HealthReturns™: earn up to 100% of annual premium back by meeting Active Dayz™ step targets. Unlimited SI refill (2× base from Day 1; unlimited from 2nd claim). No room rent cap. Coverage for HIV/AIDS, mental illness, obesity/bariatric surgery. Exam angle: Day-1 cover for 7 chronic conditions is the strongest anti-adverse-selection concession in the Indian market. ABHICL manages this through premium loading + wellness incentives (high-activity policyholders tend to have better metabolic control). The 7-condition Day-1 cover is the primary competitive differentiator of the Activ One series.

Mid-range Activ One variant. Core Activ One features (chronic Day-1 cover, HealthReturns™) but with lower OPD depth and wellness reward ceiling than NXT. Super Credit benefit: SI grows up to 6× base SI by Year 6 (cumulative SI credit regardless of claims). Exam angle: SI growth to 6× by Year 6 creates a significant and growing liability. Must be funded through premium loading from Year 1 — a form of deferred coverage that must be recognised as a growing future claim exposure.
Legacy Generations

content.bold: Activ One (VIP, VIP+, VYTL) — prior generations
content.text: Older Activ One products still widely held. VIP and VIP+ remain in-force for large policyholder blocks. VYTL (You, Your Type, Your Life): personalised plan allowing selection of specific chronic conditions for Day-1 coverage — policyholder chose which of the 7 conditions to include. Exam angle: VYTL's personalised chronic selection is a very direct adverse selection mechanism — policyholders select Day-1 cover only for the conditions they actually have. Pricing must adjust for the selection intensity of each condition combination chosen.
Activ Health Platinum Series
Platinum Tiers

Entry-tier Platinum plan. All standard inpatient, daycare, pre/post-hospitalisation. HealthReturns™ up to 30% of premium. Chronic management program available as add-on. Exam angle: Essential provides the HealthReturns™ ecosystem access at a lower premium entry point — strategy is to onboard policyholders at lower tiers and upsell to Enhanced as income and health awareness grows.

Comprehensive Platinum plan. OPD chronic management program included in-built. Covers dental (inpatient/medically necessary), mental illness, obesity. HealthReturns™ up to 100%. Annual health check-up. The plan that established ABHICL's 'health first, not just sickness' positioning. Exam angle: the 'health first' positioning requires the insurer to invest in proactive wellness — a cost that precedes any claims benefit. The actuarial case for wellness investment rests on demonstrating that wellness spend (A) reduces future hospitalisation costs (B), where B > A at an acceptable discount rate.
ABHICL's HealthReturns™ — Core Mechanic
Active Dayz™ = days when step-count target is met (tracked via wearable/app). Points accumulate and convert to HealthReturns™ credits.
HealthReturns™ credits usable against: renewal premium, OPD spend, pharmacy bills. Up to 100% of base premium returnable in high-engagement plans.

Actuarial debate: selection effect (healthy people find step targets easy and sign up) vs. causal effect (coverage incentivises genuinely increased activity and lower morbidity). Evidence suggests selection dominates — see India Market Insights tab.
Activ Fit Series (Youth)
Youth-Targeted Plans

content.bold: Activ Fit / Activ Fit Plus / Activ Fit Preferred
content.text: Targeted at young adults (entry: 18 years). Lower SI options and lower premium. HealthReturns™ incentivises active lifestyle from early age. Preferred variant adds OPD cover and a higher HealthReturns™ reward cap. Exam angle: youth-targeted products are a portfolio management strategy. Capturing healthy young lives (low morbidity, low claims) subsidises the older, higher-morbidity cohorts in the portfolio. The long-term bet is that these policyholders renew as they age and their morbidity increases — generating profitable claims experience. Guaranteed renewability means ABHICL cannot exit even as morbidity rises. Strategic risk: if the young cohort lapses at high rates in middle age, the portfolio loses the cross-subsidy.
Activ Care Series (Senior Citizens)
Three-Tier Senior Plan

Three-tier senior plan (entry: 55–80 years). Standard: 20% co-pay. Premier: 10% co-pay + broader coverage. Elite: no co-pay + full room coverage + all conditions. All tiers cover PEDs from 90 days (Day-1 option at extra premium loading). Unique feature: Active Care Management — dedicated care manager for chronic condition support. Exam angle: PED cover from 90 days (vs. standard 2–4 years) at the 55–80 age band is very aggressive — most policyholders entering at 55+ already have at least one PED. The 90-day trigger means claims from PEDs are payable almost from inception. Premium loading must front-load the entire expected PED claim cost that would otherwise be deferred.
Key Exam Angles — Senior Pricing

Co-payment tiers (0%, 10%, 20%) create a pricing ladder — different premium rates for different cost-sharing levels. Actuarially, the co-pay reduces expected claim cost by more than its nominal percentage because it also reduces moral hazard.
Entry age 80 is the highest upper entry age among Indian SAHIs. Pricing at age 80 requires extreme morbidity loading and is feasible only with strong co-payment structures.

Active Care Management (care manager) is a proactive health management tool. The actuarial rationale: reducing avoidable hospitalisations through chronic condition management generates cost savings > cost of the care manager programme.
Activ Secure Series (Supplementary Covers)
Critical Illness & Cancer

Lump-sum CI plan covering major illnesses (cancer, heart attack, stroke, organ failure, coma, etc.). Fixed benefit on diagnosis. Available as standalone or rider on an Activ Health base plan. Second opinion benefit included. Exam angle: CI fixed benefit = incidence rate × probability of surviving survival clause × sum assured. The 90-day survival clause is standard. Pricing key inputs: CI incidence rates by age/sex (from reinsurer tables + IRDAI data), survival rates post-diagnosis, and claim frequency (multi-claim products require additional modelling).

Three-stage cancer cover: early stage (25% benefit), major stage (100% benefit), advanced stage (additional payment). Covers cancer at any stage of first diagnosis. Exam angle: stage-based cancer cover is the broadest cancer product in the Indian market. Most CI products only pay on advanced/major cancer. Early-stage detection (which is rising with better diagnostic technology) triggers the 25% payment — this creates an increasing claim frequency over time as cancer screening improves. Pricing must anticipate this trend-driven frequency increase.
Personal Accident & Hospital Cash

Covers accidental death, PTD, PPD, TTD. EMI Protection Benefit: if policyholder is on a loan and becomes disabled, the plan pays EMI instalments. Child Education Benefit: lump sum on policyholder's accidental death to fund children's education. Exam angle: EMI protection and child education benefits are innovative liability extensions that go beyond standard PA. They create correlation between the insurer's liability and external factors (loan balance, education costs) — requiring additional economic assumptions in pricing.

Daily cash benefit per day of hospitalisation. ICU: 2× daily rate. Convalescence benefit: additional lump sum for prolonged hospitalisation (>10 days). Exam angle: convalescence benefit for >10-day stays creates a binary trigger above the daily cash — must separately model the probability of extended hospitalisation (>10 days) and the distribution of stay durations.
Global & Travel Plans
Global Treatment Cover

Covers medical treatment outside India. SI in USD ($500k–$3M). Covers diseases diagnosed in India but treated abroad (cancer, cardiac, orthopaedic, neurological). Emergency medical evacuation. Exam angle: pricing challenge — no Indian insurer has credible domestic data on overseas treatment costs. Must rely entirely on reinsurer (Munich Re, Swiss Re) global healthcare cost databases. Key assumptions: country of treatment mix (USA costs 5–10× UK/Europe for same procedure), diagnosis-to-treatment timeline for covered conditions, and survival rates post-treatment.

Short-term international travel insurance. Medical emergency, evacuation, trip cancellation, baggage. Group version available for corporates. Exam angle: group travel policies are priced on expected group trip frequency and duration, not individual age-band morbidity — a different pricing paradigm from individual health plans.
HealthReturns™ — The Distinctive Mechanic
How It Works

Policyholder earns Active Dayz™ points for hitting daily step-count targets (tracked via app or wearable device). Points convert to HealthReturns™ credits redeemable against: renewal premium, OPD spend, or pharmacy bills. Up to 100% of base premium returnable in high-engagement plans (Activ One NXT Apex). Exam angle: this is ABHICL's defining product innovation. The actuarial question is whether it reduces claims (causal effect) or only attracts healthy policyholders (selection effect). Selection effect dominates in most studies — high step-count earners are already healthy. True causal effect on claims morbidity is difficult to isolate without a controlled trial. ABHICL accepts the selection effect as a portfolio management benefit — concentrating healthy lives is itself valuable for pricing and loss ratio management.
Actuarial Implications
HealthReturns™ liability: up to 100% of premium may become a credit obligation — this is a deferred premium discount that must be reserved.
If selection effect dominates: wellness policyholders have lower claims, making the discount cost-neutral or positive for ABHICL.
If causal effect exists: lower morbidity means lower future claims, also making the investment net-positive. The uncertainty is in quantifying the split between selection and causality.
Exam angle: IRDAI is pushing wellness integration in health insurance (IRDAI 2047 Vision) — this is an SA1 topical area. Be prepared to discuss the actuarial basis for wellness reward pricing.
ManipalCigna Health Insurance — Products & Features

Fifth-largest SAHI (~9% retail market share). Joint venture between Manipal Group and Cigna Corporation (US, global health specialist). Cigna's international expertise shapes chronic disease management and OPD integration. Known for high SI ceiling products and the Supreme Bonus mechanic.

ProHealth Prime series: three variants differentiated by chronic cover depth — Protect (entry, unlimited restoration from 2nd claim), Advantage (mid-tier, built-in OPD, 90-day wait for 5 chronic conditions), Active (chronic-first design, lower SI ceiling, for buyers with PEDs). Supreme Bonus: 100% guaranteed annual SI increase up to 800% of base SI, regardless of claims. Sarvah series (newer flagship): Uttam and Param, with enhanced OPD, mental health, and digital health services; Param targets HNI with global emergency cover.

LifeTime Health Plan: guaranteed lifetime cover with no upper SI cap — guaranteed renewability regardless of claims, age, or health change; actuarially the most complex product to price given the long-duration liability. Non-medical expenses (gloves, syringes, registration charges) covered as standard in ProHealth Prime — a cost loading few competitors absorb. Premium waiver on accidental death or CI diagnosis. Super Top-up Plus/Select: aggregate-trigger super top-up covering both related and unrelated illnesses; Select adds non-medical expense cover.

(1) Supreme Bonus (800% of base SI over time, non-reducible): at maximum, SI is 9× original — the largest SI accumulation mechanic in the Indian market. Pricing must solve for the full accumulated liability at each renewal year. (2) LifeTime Health: guaranteed renewability for life creates a perpetual liability. No other Indian health product has a comparable term. Pricing requires long-duration morbidity projection, lapse-free assumption, and significant uncertainty loading. (3) ProHealth Prime Active (lower SI ceiling for PED buyers): the SI cap is the anti-selection control — limits the insurer's exposure from the most adverse risks. Exam angle: why is a lower SI ceiling more appropriate than a co-pay for this segment? (4) Non-medical expense coverage: adds a predictable, inflation-linked cost to every claim. Must be modelled separately from the main benefit. (5) Super top-up aggregate trigger: requires modelling the full loss distribution to the deductible — more complex than per-event top-up pricing.

ManipalCigna's Supreme Bonus (up to 800% SI accumulation) and LifeTime Health (guaranteed perpetual cover) are the two actuarially most extreme product designs among SAHIs — exam questions on long-duration liabilities and SI accumulation will often reference these.
ProHealth Prime Series (Core Flagship)
Three Variants — Differentiated by Chronic Cover Depth

SI: ₹3L–₹1 crore. Covers inpatient, daycare, pre/post-hospitalisation (60/180 days), domiciliary, AYUSH, organ donor. Unlimited restoration from 2nd claim. Annual health check-up from Year 1. Step-count wellness rewards. Room: single private room. Exam angle: 'unlimited restoration from 2nd claim' is the entry-level restoration mechanic — compare with Niva Bupa ReAssure 3.0 which activates after the 1st claim. This is a material benefit difference that should be understood by exam candidates.

All Protect features plus built-in OPD (cashless consultations, diagnostics, pharmacy at network providers). Higher wellness reward cap. SI: ₹5L–₹1 crore. Any-room-category add-on available. 90-day waiting period for 5 chronic conditions (diabetes, hypertension, obesity, dyslipidaemia, asthma) vs. the standard 2–4 year wait. Exam angle: 90-day chronic cover vs. standard 2–4 years is a major product differentiator. It narrows the PED deferral window significantly — pricing must compensate for the accelerated claim exposure in Years 1–2.

Designed specifically for buyers with PEDs (diabetes, hypertension, obesity, dyslipidaemia, asthma). 90-day waiting period for these conditions. SI ceiling: ₹3–₹15L (lower than Protect/Advantage). OPD included in-built. Exam angle: the lower SI ceiling on Active (vs. Protect/Advantage) is a deliberate adverse selection control. Buyers with known PEDs who select this variant are higher-risk by definition; capping the SI limits the maximum loss per policyholder. This illustrates the principle of benefit design as an underwriting control.
Features Common Across ProHealth Prime

Supreme Bonus: 100% guaranteed annual SI increase (up to 800% of base SI) — regardless of whether a claim was made. Non-medical expenses (gloves, syringes, registration charges, stationery) covered as standard (vs. most plans which exclude these). Premium waiver on accidental death or CI diagnosis — one year's renewal premium waived. Unlimited teleconsultation. Domestic second opinion. Exam angle: 100% SI growth per year (capped at 800%) is one of the highest guaranteed bonus mechanics in the Indian market — compare Care's Infinity Bonus (also 100%, non-reducible). Both create significant future SI liability. The premium waiver on accidental death is a PA-linked feature within a health plan — creates a cross-product liability that must be reserved.
Sarvah Series (Newer Flagship)
Next-Generation Plans

Next-generation plan positioned to replace ProHealth Prime over time. Enhanced OPD, mental health cover, and digital health services (teleconsultation, app-based care navigation). Higher SI ceiling. OPD mental health coverage is a specific regulatory requirement under IRDAI's Mental Healthcare Act compliance. Exam angle: Sarvah series positions ManipalCigna as a 'full health manager' rather than just a hospitalisation reimbursor. IRDAI's 2025 product architecture guidelines encourage this broader positioning. The actuarial challenge: OPD + mental health are high-utilisation, hard-to-predict benefit categories.

Premium tier of the Sarvah series. Maximum coverage — any room, no room rent cap. Global emergency cover. Higher non-medical expense coverage. Targets upper-mid and HNI segment. Exam angle: the HNI segment is a strategically important but actuarially complex segment — high SI, global cover, and high-cost specialist access create claim size variance that requires reinsurance support.
Lifetime Health Plan
Long-Duration Health Cover

Designed for guaranteed lifetime cover. India variant: comprehensive indemnity with no upper SI cap. Global variant: international coverage for medical treatment abroad. Key feature: guaranteed renewability for life regardless of claims history, age, or health change. Exam angle: LifeTime Health is one of the most actuarially complex products in the Indian market. (1) No claim-based lapse risk — but significant adverse selection accumulation over time as healthy lives exit via switching. (2) No upper age exit means the insurer retains the policyholder through peak morbidity years (80–90+). (3) No SI cap means infinite theoretic liability for the India variant. (4) Pricing must fully fund future claims at all ages over the lifetime — a long-duration pricing exercise comparable to annuity pricing rather than annual renewable health insurance. Reinsurance essential.
Prime Senior Plan
Senior Variants

Entry: 56–75 years. Classic: SI ₹3–₹25L; 20% co-payment. Elite: SI ₹5L–₹1 crore; no co-pay; single private room. PEDs covered after 2 years (Standard Waiting Period — below the IRDAI standard 4 years for many conditions). Annual health check-up from Day 1. ReAssure benefit (unlimited SI reinstatement) on Elite variant. Exam angle: the 2-year PED waiting period on a senior plan is a significant benefit. At entry age 56–75, most policyholders have at least one PED. Covering it after 2 years (vs. 4) accelerates the expected claims timeline — the extra cost must be loaded into premiums from Year 1. The co-payment on Classic (20%) is lower than several competitors (Star Red Carpet: 30%; Niva Bupa Senior First Gold: 50%) — suggesting ManipalCigna is targeting a higher-income segment even within seniors.
Super Top-up Plans
How Super Top-up Works

content.bold: Super Top-up Plus & Super Top-up Select
content.text: Aggregate-trigger super top-up plans — activate when total annual claims in the policy year exceed the deductible (aggregate basis, not per-event). Plus variant: covers both related and unrelated illnesses. Select variant: adds non-medical expense cover. SI: up to ₹30L above the deductible. No medical screening required at entry. Exam angle: aggregate trigger pricing is fundamentally different from base plan pricing. The insurer must model the total annual claim distribution (not the severity tail of individual events) to determine the probability that aggregate claims exceed the deductible. This requires modelling claim frequency, severity, and correlation across multiple hospitalisation events within the same year for the same policyholder.
Top-up vs. Super Top-up — Key Distinction
Top-up: activates when a SINGLE claim exceeds the deductible. Multiple moderate claims in one year that each fall below the deductible are NOT covered.
Super top-up: activates when AGGREGATE annual claims exceed the deductible. Multiple moderate claims in one year can collectively breach the deductible and trigger the cover.

Exam angle: super top-up is more favourable to the policyholder but requires more complex actuarial modelling. The aggregate trigger means the insurer must model the full claim distribution, not just the tail.
Hospital Cash & Critical Illness
Fixed Benefit Products

Daily hospital cash (₹500–₹5,000/day) for up to 180 days per year. ICU: 2× daily rate. Quarantine sub-benefit included (added post-COVID). Fixed benefit — no bills required. Exam angle: 180 days/year maximum is higher than Star Hospital Cash (60 days/year) — creates a higher maximum liability for ManipalCigna. A policyholder with a long-term chronic illness requiring repeated hospitalisation could claim close to the 180-day maximum each year. Pricing must model the upper tail of hospitalisation duration distribution.

Lump-sum CI plan covering 30+ critical illnesses — includes early-stage cancer, stroke, Alzheimer's, Parkinson's. Multi-claim option: pays on each separate CI diagnosis across the policy tenure (different condition each time). Partial payment for early-stage cancer before full diagnosis confirmation. Exam angle: Alzheimer's and Parkinson's cover is rare in Indian CI plans — these are progressive conditions without clear 'diagnosis' dates, creating claims definition challenges. Partial early-stage cancer payment adds actuarial complexity — must model the probability of early-stage diagnosis escalating to full-stage, avoiding double-counting the benefit.
Personal Accident & Combined Plans
PA Plans

content.bold: Lifestyle Protection Accident Care / ManipalCigna Accident Shield
content.text: PA covers: accidental death, PTD, PPD, TTD. Accident Shield additionally covers accident hospitalisation expenses reimbursement. EMI Protection and Child Education Benefit on severe disability. Exam angle: the addition of hospitalisation expenses reimbursement to the PA benefit in Accident Shield creates a hybrid PA-health product. Pricing must account for the correlation between accident-related hospitalisation costs and disability severity — not independent events.
Combined & Standard Products

Combines health + personal accident + critical illness in a single policy. Designed for buyers who want comprehensive protection without managing multiple separate policies. Exam angle: combined plans require careful benefit demarcation between the three components to prevent double-counting (e.g. a heart attack could trigger both the health inpatient benefit and the CI lump-sum benefit). The underwriting and claims management processes must specify clear priority rules. Pricing is additive but must account for any correlation between the trigger events.

IRDAI-mandated standard indemnity plan. SI: ₹1–5L. 5% co-payment. Covers inpatient (ICU, AYUSH, 12 modern treatments, cataract, joint replacement). Maximum entry age: 65 years. Standardised benefit structure — same across all insurers. Exam angle: Arogya Sanjeevani is designed by IRDAI to provide a comparable baseline product across all insurers, reducing information asymmetry for buyers. As a mandated product, it cannot be used as a competitive differentiator — competition occurs only on price and claims service. Portability between insurers for this product requires transfer of full waiting period credit.
India Health Insurance — Market Insights & SA1 Comparisons

7 SAHIs licensed by IRDAI (as of April 2025) + ~25 general insurers writing health. Top 5 SAHIs by retail share: Star Health (~32%), Niva Bupa (~18%), Care Health (~14%), Aditya Birla Health (~12%), ManipalCigna (~9%). India health insurance penetration remains low (~0.35% of GDP vs. ~4% in USA). Rapid post-COVID growth: retail health CAGR 18–22% since 2020. Government schemes (PMJAY: ~500M lives, ₹5L/year for BPL families) dominate volume but not value — the private insured 'missing middle' is the key commercial segment.

Standard waiting period controls: 30-day general waiting period (prevents buying insurance when already ill), PED waiting period (now capped at 3 years under IRDAI 2024 regulations, down from 4), specific disease waiting periods (typically 1–2 years), sub-limits on high-cost predictable procedures (cataract, joint replacement, hernia). Portability (IRDAI-mandated): policyholders can switch insurers retaining accumulated waiting period credit — creates adverse selection risk for the acquiring insurer (healthy lives switch for lower premium; high-risk lives stay). Maternity: 9–12 month waiting period and sub-limits required — it is a near-certain event for target cohort, not a random risk.

NCB/Bonus: Standard (10–50% SI increase, reducible on claim) vs. Care Infinity Bonus / ManipalCigna Supreme Bonus (100% annual increase, non-reducible — SI can reach 6–9× base). Restoration: near-universal but differs on trigger (from 1st claim vs. 2nd) and scope (same illness vs. different illness only). Niva Bupa ReAssure Forever is the strongest (unlimited from claim 1, any illness). Wellness: ABHICL HealthReturns™ (up to 100% premium back) is the most advanced; all 5 major SAHIs now offer step-count rewards. Zone pricing: Care Classic uses explicit zone pricing; most others embed geographic cost differences implicitly via room-rent caps and network hospital rate negotiations.

(1) Medical inflation at 10–14% p.a. (2–3× CPI): the single most important external assumption in Indian health pricing. Plans with non-reducible SI bonuses accumulate exposure faster than premium grows — the gap must be funded at inception. (2) Data limitations: most SAHIs have <15 years of credible data. OPD data: 3–4 years only. LTC: does not exist in India. CI multi-pay: under 5 years. New entrants (Narayana, Galaxy): <2–3 years. Credibility formula Z = n/(n+k) gives Z ≈ 0 for new entrants — almost all pricing weight on reinsurer benchmarks. (3) ICR benchmarks (FY2024–25): Star ~82–85% (profitable), Niva Bupa ~91%, Care Health ~88%, ABHICL ~95–98% (investment phase). ICR above 100% = loss-making on claims alone. (4) Appointed Actuary responsibilities: certify premium fairness (not inadequate for solvency, not excessive for policyholders), file Actuarial Memorandum with IRDAI for each new product, certify any material change (>5–10% expected cost impact). (5) Group vs. individual: group = guaranteed issue, lower adverse selection, experience-rated for >200 lives; individual = self-selected, higher adverse selection, community/age-band rated.

This topic is the synthesis and comparison resource — use it to connect mechanics across all SAHI topics. The Appointed Actuary's role, ICR benchmarks, medical inflation, and the credibility data challenge are the highest-frequency SA1 exam themes from the India market section.
Market Structure & Regulatory Context
SAHI vs. General Insurer — Key Distinctions

7 SAHIs licensed by IRDAI (as of April 2025) — permitted to write only health, personal accident, and travel insurance. ~25 general insurers also write health as one of several lines. SAHIs: exclusive health focus → deeper product innovation, richer health claims data, stronger network hospital relationships, dedicated underwriting and claims expertise. General insurers: can cross-subsidise health losses from profitable motor/fire/marine lines; wider bancassurance distribution networks. Exam angle: SAHI pricing relies entirely on own health experience — no cross-subsidy mechanism. A sustained loss in health cannot be offset by another line. This creates stronger incentive for SAHIs to price health prudently and invest in risk selection and claims management.
IRDAI licenses SAHIs under the Insurance Act 1938 (as amended) — licence restricts business to health, PA, and travel only. Cannot write motor or property.

5 major SAHIs by retail market share (2024–25): Star Health (~32%), Niva Bupa (~18%), Care Health (~14%), Aditya Birla Health (~12%), ManipalCigna (~9%). Remaining ~15% split among newer/smaller SAHIs.
Mandatory IRDAI Products

Every health insurer must offer: (1) Arogya Sanjeevani — standard indemnity plan (SI ₹1–5L, 5% co-pay, inpatient + daycare + AYUSH + 12 modern treatments); (2) Saral Suraksha Bima — standard personal accident plan (AD, PTD, PPD, TTD). Both have standardised benefit structures identical across all insurers. Exam angle: mandatory products ensure a comparable baseline for consumers, reducing information asymmetry. They eliminate competitive differentiation at the base level — competition occurs only on price and service quality. Portability is also mandated — policyholders retain accumulated waiting period credit when switching insurers, which creates an adverse selection risk for the acquiring insurer.

Portability: acquiring insurer must credit the waiting period already served at the previous insurer. E.g. a policyholder 2 years into a 4-year PED waiting period who switches retains 2 years of credit — meaning the acquiring insurer faces the remaining 2-year PED exposure without having received the full pricing compensation for Years 1–2.
Market Growth & Data Context

India's health insurance penetration remains low (~0.35% of GDP vs ~4% in the USA, ~1.5% in OECD average). Rapid growth post-COVID: retail health has grown at 18–22% CAGR since 2020. Low historic penetration means thin long-run morbidity data — actuaries rely heavily on reinsurer global tables (Swiss Re, Munich Re, Gen Re) and IRDAI-published industry studies. As penetration rises, the insured pool becomes less adverse (early adopters of insurance are often sicker or more risk-aware than the general population). Exam angle: data limitations are a core SA1 topic for the Indian market. Know the specific gaps: OPD data is virtually absent; LTC data does not exist in India; CI multi-pay experience is under 5 years; senior morbidity at ages 75–85+ is anecdotal.

Indian healthcare inflation consistently runs at 10–14% p.a. — 2–3× general CPI. Drivers: (1) corporate hospital pricing power (Apollo, Fortis, Max negotiate rates insurers cannot easily reject); (2) rising diagnostic intensity (more tests per admission); (3) cost of new medical technology and targeted therapies; (4) low negotiating leverage of individual policyholders vs. network hospitals. Insurer responses: (a) SI inflation shield clauses (e.g. Care Plus CPI-linked SI); (b) provider network rate cap agreements; (c) co-insurance on high-cost treatments; (d) preferred provider agreements with volume discounts. Exam angle: medical inflation at 12% means a ₹5L SI purchased today is worth only ₹3.1L in real terms after 5 years. Products that guarantee SI growth (Care Infinity Bonus, ManipalCigna Supreme Bonus) are partly a response to this — but create their own growing liability.
Product Design Principles & Anti-Selection
Core Anti-Selection Controls

(1) General waiting period: 30 days from inception — prevents buying insurance when already ill and claiming immediately. (2) PED waiting period: standard 2–4 years for pre-existing conditions (IRDAI mandated maximum 3 years from 2024 regulations); insurer cannot exclude PED claims after 3 years of continuous cover. (3) Specific disease waiting period: 1–2 years for listed conditions (e.g. hernia, cataract, joint replacement) even if not declared as PED at entry. (4) Pre-policy medical: required typically for age 45+ or SI ≥₹15L — provides underwriting data. (5) Co-payment: policyholder bears a fixed % of each claim — used primarily for senior products. (6) Sub-limits: cap reimbursement per procedure (e.g. cataract ₹40k, knee replacement ₹1L). (7) Exclusions: cosmetic, dental, spectacles, war, self-inflicted, experimental treatments. Exam angle: the interaction between these controls is important — a product may remove one control (e.g. no PED wait) but compensate with another (e.g. higher premium loading, or a co-payment).

IRDAI 2024 guideline: PED waiting period capped at 3 years (down from 4 years). All policies issued post-guideline must cover PED claims after 3 years continuous cover — regardless of policy wording. Actuarial implication: reduces the deferral period, increasing expected claims in Years 3–4.

Sub-limit design: sub-limits cap insurer exposure for predictable high-cost procedures. They reduce adverse selection (people who know they need cataract surgery are less attracted to a plan with a ₹40k cataract cap). But they also reduce policyholder value — a tension in product design.
Portability — Adverse Selection Mechanism

IRDAI-mandated portability means healthy, low-claiming policyholders can switch to a competitor for lower premiums — leaving high-risk, high-claiming lives on the legacy insurer's book. The acquiring insurer must accept transferred PED waiting period credit and cannot apply fresh underwriting to the switcher. This means the acquiring insurer inherits an adverse selection risk without commensurate historical premium income. Exam angle: portability creates a systematic adverse selection mechanism. Pricing for new business must factor in the expected health profile of portability switchers (who tend to be healthier, since unhealthy policyholders prefer to stay with an insurer who already accepts their PED claims) — but also that some switchers are moving because their current insurer has tried to increase their renewal premium due to poor experience.
Near-Certain Events — Maternity & OPD

content.bold: Maternity — a near-certain event, not a risk
content.text: Maternity benefit requires special actuarial treatment. Unlike hospitalisation for illness (low probability for any given individual), pregnancy is planned and near-certain for the relevant cohort — it is not an insurable risk in the traditional sense. Without controls, adverse selection (buying a plan when already pregnant or planning pregnancy immediately) would make the benefit unviable. Standard controls: (1) Waiting period: 9–24 months from policy inception; (2) SI sub-limit: ₹50k–₹2L (far below a comprehensive health SI); (3) Claim count limit: typically 2 deliveries per policy lifetime; (4) Price loading: maternity plans command a significant premium over equivalent non-maternity plans. Exam angle: SA1 frequently asks candidates to distinguish 'insurable risk' from 'near-certain events'. Maternity is the clearest Indian market example — waiting period + sub-limit + count limit are the structural equivalent of making it a funded benefit with delay, not a risk transfer.

content.bold: OPD — high utilisation, high moral hazard
content.text: OPD claims are high-frequency, low-severity, and highly elastic to coverage. Unlike inpatient (where illness severity drives hospitalisation), OPD utilisation is strongly influenced by moral hazard — coverage drives demand (people consult a doctor more when it is free). Indian OPD data is extremely thin — most SAHIs have meaningful OPD data only since 2020–21. Pricing approaches: (1) Annual benefit cap (e.g. ₹10,000/year); (2) Per-consultation sub-limit (₹500 per visit); (3) Network-only access (limits choice to managed network); (4) Deductible per visit; (5) Pre-authorisation for diagnostics. Exam angle: OPD is an emerging and volatile product line. Exam questions may ask you to design controls or price an OPD benefit — key is to identify and quantify the moral hazard adjustment and explain why standard health incidence data (hospitalisation-based) cannot be directly applied.
Pricing Levers Across Insurers
NCB & Bonus Mechanics — Comparison

Standard NCB (most plans): 10–50% SI increase per claim-free year; reduced (partially or fully) on claim. Care Infinity Bonus: 100% SI increase per year; NOT reducible on claim — once earned, it stays. ManipalCigna Supreme Bonus: 100% guaranteed annual SI increase (up to 800% of base SI) — regardless of claim history. Niva Bupa ReAssure 2.0: unused SI carryforward up to 10× base SI. Actuarial implications: (a) non-reducible bonuses (Care, ManipalCigna) create a ratcheting SI liability — it only ever goes up. (b) At 100% per year for 5 years, SI is 6× inception level. Premium must fund this growing SI from Year 1. (c) Pricing must model the joint distribution of bonus accumulation alongside claim exhaustion events. (d) In high-inflation environments, non-reducible bonuses are a meaningful policyholder benefit — and a meaningful insurer liability. Exam angle: compare the actuarial reserve requirement for (i) reducible NCB vs. (ii) non-reducible guaranteed bonus. The latter requires a growing prospective liability reserve from Year 1.
Reducible NCB example: insurer can reduce SI back to base on first claim — limits the long-run liability growth.

Non-reducible bonus (Care Infinity Bonus): SI at Year 5 = 6× original. If original SI was ₹5L, it is now ₹30L — but the premium was priced for ₹5L at inception. The growing differential must be funded from premium loading at entry.
Restoration / Reinstatement — Comparison

Now a near-universal feature. Key differentiator is from which claim it activates and for what trigger. Star: on SI exhaustion; same or different illness (stronger than most). Care: unlimited auto-recharge for same or different illness. Niva Bupa ReAssure Forever: unlimited after even the 1st paid claim (strongest in market). ManipalCigna ProHealth Prime: from 2nd claim onward. Actuarial pricing: restoration increases expected annual claims significantly vs. a non-restoring plan. Must model multi-event claim distributions — cannot rely on single-event expected loss. Key inputs: hospitalisation frequency per insured life per year, correlation between multiple events for the same individual (a person who is hospitalised once is more likely to be hospitalised again). Exam angle: restoration/reinstatement features are extremely popular SA1 exam topics. Understand (a) how they differ from each other, (b) why they increase pricing complexity, and (c) the adverse selection dynamic — products with stronger restoration attract sicker lives.
Wellness Incentives & Zone Pricing

content.bold: Wellness incentives — behaviour vs. selection effect
content.text: All 5 major SAHIs now offer wellness rewards. ABHICL HealthReturns™: up to 100% premium back for meeting Active Dayz™ step targets. Niva Bupa: up to 30% renewal discount for step-count. ManipalCigna: step-count rewards. Star Young Star: wellness points. Key actuarial debate: do wellness incentives genuinely reduce morbidity (causal effect), or do they primarily attract already-healthy lives (selection effect)? Available evidence from global studies suggests selection effect dominates — high step-count earners are already active and healthy before taking the policy. True causal impact on claims is very hard to isolate without randomised control trials. Exam angle: this is a core SA1 exam topic. The correct answer structure is: (1) acknowledge that wellness may create genuine behaviour change; (2) but note selection effect likely dominates; (3) therefore pricing should treat wellness incentive costs as a marketing/distribution cost rather than a claims reduction, unless credible causal evidence exists from own experience data.

Used by Care Health (Care Classic) and implicitly embedded in room-rent caps across many plans. Metro (Zone 1) hospitalisation costs are 30–50% higher per DRG than non-metro (Zone 3) for the same procedure. Zone pricing reduces cross-subsidy between metro and non-metro policyholders and improves pricing accuracy. Risks: (1) arbitrage — policyholders register at a non-metro address to pay Zone 3 premium, then claim at metro hospitals; (2) operational complexity — ongoing zone boundary calibration required as hospital pricing evolves; (3) regulatory risk — IRDAI may restrict zone pricing differentials. Exam angle: zone pricing is an application of risk classification. The actuarial justification is that expected claim cost materially differs by geography. The counter-argument (from a fairness/regulatory perspective) is that it may restrict access to insurance for metro residents.
Claims Management Angle
Cashless vs. Reimbursement

~60–70% of Indian health claims are now processed cashless at network hospitals. Cashless: insurer pre-authorises the admission directly with the hospital — policyholder pays nothing upfront (or only co-pay). Benefits: reduces financial burden on policyholder; improves insurer's ability to manage claims at pre-auth stage. Risks: hospital billing tends to be higher for insured patients (moral hazard — hospital bills to insurance rather than price-sensitive individual); requires robust pre-authorisation fraud controls. Reimbursement: policyholder pays upfront, submits bills, insurer reimburses. Benefits: policyholder is cost-conscious (reduces moral hazard, lower average bill size). Risks: increases policyholder financial burden; documentation-heavy; may deter legitimate claims. Actuarial note: cashless claims tend to run ~10–20% higher in average cost than reimbursement claims for the same diagnosis — empirically observed across multiple SAHIs. Exam angle: the shift to cashless (driven by policyholder preference) has increased average claim costs. Pricing must reflect the cashless proportion of expected claims — a plan that moves from 50% to 70% cashless will see a material increase in average claim cost even with no change in underlying morbidity.
Fraud & Non-Payables

IRDAI's standardised list of non-payable items (gloves, cotton, syringes, registration fee, attendant charges, etc.) limits hospital billing inflation. Plans that cover non-payables (ManipalCigna ProHealth Prime, Star Comprehensive) command a premium loading to compensate. Key fraud typologies in Indian health insurance: (1) inflated bills — hospital charges higher than rate card to insurance patients; (2) fictitious hospitalisation — daycare or OPD coded as inpatient; (3) unnecessary daycare — minor procedures done under daycare to generate a claimable event; (4) PED non-disclosure — undeclared pre-existing conditions at entry, claimed later; (5) kickback schemes — TPAs or hospitals inflating bills and sharing proceeds. Exam angle: fraud management is both an operational and actuarial issue. Actuarially, a fraudulent claim is an unexpected cost that is not priced into the morbidity assumption. Systemic fraud inflates the actual-to-expected claims ratio and requires a fraud loading in pricing if it cannot be fully controlled through TPA/investigator processes.
ICR Benchmarks Across SAHIs

ICR = Net Claims Incurred / Net Earned Premium. IRDAI monitors ICR by insurer and segment. An ICR above 100% indicates the segment is loss-making on claims alone (before expenses). Approximate FY2024–25 ICRs: Star Health ~82–85% (profitable; strong network hospital rate management); Niva Bupa ~91% (thinner margin; heavy investment phase); ManipalCigna ~88%; Aditya Birla Health ~90–93% (wellness investment costs plus growing book); Care Health ~60–65% (conservative claims management; higher rejection/deduction rate). Exam angle: ICR must be interpreted carefully. Low ICR (e.g. Care ~62%) is not unambiguously good — it may indicate (a) efficient claims management; OR (b) excessive claim deductions and rejections that reduce policyholder value and increase complaints; OR (c) a very young book where PED waiting periods are still deferring claims. High ICR (e.g. Niva Bupa ~91%) may indicate competitive product design attracting more claims, not necessarily poor management. The AA must assess the ICR in context of the business strategy, not in isolation.
SA1 Exam Angles — Indian Market
Product Design Question Framework

content.bold: Framework for any new Indian health product question
content.text: For any SA1 question asking you to design, price, or critique an Indian health product, structure your answer around: (1) Benefit type — indemnity (reimburse actual cost) vs. fixed benefit (CI, hospital cash); (2) Coverage scope — inpatient, OPD, CI, PA, travel; (3) Sum insured level and sub-limits (SI design controls maximum loss and adverse selection); (4) Waiting period design — general, PED, specific disease; (5) NCB/bonus mechanics — reducible vs. non-reducible, rate; (6) Restoration — trigger, frequency, same/different illness; (7) Underwriting basis — age banding, SI bands, PED declaration, pre-policy medical; (8) Pricing data sources — own experience, reinsurer tables, IRDAI industry studies; (9) Distribution channel — individual agent, bancassurance, online direct, group; (10) IRDAI filing and Appointed Actuary certification requirements. Exam angle: questions on 'what would you consider when launching product X in India' will expect this framework. Don't just list benefits — discuss the actuarial rationale behind each design choice.
Data Limitations

Most SAHIs have fewer than 15 years of credible experience data. Specific data gaps: (1) OPD data: virtually absent — OPD insurance is very new; limited to 3–4 years of meaningful data; (2) LTC data: does not exist in India — no equivalent to UK/US nursing home experience; (3) CI multi-pay: under 5 years of Indian experience; (4) Senior morbidity (75–85+): thin data; very few Indian policyholders have held health cover into late old age; (5) Rural/micro-insurance: low data volume, geographically dispersed. Approaches: blend own (sparse) experience with reinsurer global tables using credibility weighting; apply conservative loadings for uncertainty; use scenario testing to explore the range of outcomes. Exam angle: data limitations is a guaranteed topic area in SA1 Indian questions. Know the specific gaps listed above, understand the credibility framework for blending thin own data with industry benchmarks, and be able to explain why conservative loading is justified under uncertainty.
Credibility formula: P* = Z × P_own + (1 − Z) × P_industry, where Z = credibility factor (0 to 1) based on data volume. Low Z (thin data) → heavily weight industry benchmarks.

IRDAI data sources: (a) IRDAI Annual Report — sector-level ICRs and premium data; (b) IRDAI health insurance handbooks; (c) Reinsurer experience studies (Gen Re, Munich Re, Swiss Re publish Indian market data periodically).
Group vs. Individual Market

content.bold: Group vs. individual market — actuarial differences
content.text: Group (employer-sponsored): guaranteed issue (no individual underwriting), employer-paid premium, annual rate review, experience-rated for large groups (>200 lives), lives data typically provided as census. Lower adverse selection (entry not self-selected for health reasons) but higher moral hazard (employees know they are covered and may overuse). Pricing methodology: group-specific rate based on industry SIC code, age/sex distribution, previous claims experience, sum insured. Individual: full underwriting at entry (PED declaration, pre-policy medical for older ages), policyholder-paid premium (highly price-sensitive), long-term renewal relationship, guaranteed renewability. Adverse selection at entry is higher than group but diminishes with policy duration. Exam angle: group and individual are priced using fundamentally different methodologies — do not mix them up. Group experience rating uses actual claims data; individual relies on morbidity tables and underwriting assumptions. The exam may ask you to compare or to advise a company switching from group to individual market.
Appointed Actuary's Role

Under IRDAI (Appointed Actuary) Regulations 2022: the AA must (1) advise management on product design, premium rates, and policy wording; (2) certify that premium rates are fair — not inadequate (ensures solvency), not excessive (ensures policyholder fairness); (3) ensure internal consistency between underwriting standards, pricing assumptions, and claims management policy; (4) sign off on the Product Summary and Actuarial Memorandum before IRDAI filing; (5) re-certify on any material change to benefit design or pricing basis. Exam angle: the AA's role in Indian health insurance is broader than in some other markets — they are expected to be involved in product design, not just pricing review. The 'fair pricing' standard means the AA must consider both insurer solvency adequacy AND policyholder value — a dual standard not always made explicit in other regulatory frameworks.
Actuarial Memorandum: filed with IRDAI for each new product. Contains: pricing basis, assumptions, data sources, sensitivity analysis, AA certification. Confidential — not disclosed to policyholders.

Material change: any change to benefit structure, waiting periods, exclusions, or premium formula that would change the expected cost by more than a de minimis threshold (typically 5–10% of premium) requires re-filing and AA re-certification.
Government Schemes — Interaction with Private Market

content.bold: PMJAY & government schemes — interaction with private market
content.text: PMJAY (Ayushman Bharat — Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana): covers BPL (below poverty line) families up to ₹5L/year for secondary and tertiary hospitalisation. ~500M people covered. Funded by Central and State governments. Implemented by empanelled government and private hospitals. AB-PMJAY effectively removes BPL families from the potential private health insurance market. State government schemes (Rajiv Aarogyasri in AP/Telangana, Bhamashah Yojana in Rajasthan, Mahatma Jyotiba Phule Jan Arogya Yojana in Maharashtra) add supplementary state-level cover. Exam angle: government health schemes create a tiered market. (1) BPL segment → PMJAY (not addressable by SAHIs commercially). (2) Low-income formal sector → ESIC (Employment State Insurance Corporation) for employees. (3) Middle-income segment → private SAHIs' core market. (4) Upper-income → high SI plans, global cover, HNI segments. The 'creaming off' of better-off, more profitable policyholders into the private market (while lower-income lives are covered by government) is the structure of the Indian health insurance market. Actuarial implication: the private market pool does not represent the general population — it is selected by income and health awareness.

ESIC (Employee State Insurance Corporation): mandatory social health insurance for formal sector employees earning <₹21,000/month. ~14M enrolled. Claims experience is separate from private insurer data — ESIC data is not typically available to SAHIs for benchmarking.
CGHS (Central Government Health Scheme): covers central government employees and pensioners. Another block of lives removed from the private market, with their own separate experience database.
Digit Health Insurance — Products & Features

Go Digit General Insurance (parent) received its SAHI licence as Digit Health Insurance. Digital-first insurer — onboarding, claims, and renewals fully app-based. Backed by Fairtree Capital and Prem Watsa (Fairfax). Younger, tech-savvy buyer base. Known for simplified product language and transparent policy documents.

Infinity Wallet Plan (flagship): the 'Wallet' mechanic — unused SI balance accumulates across years (up to 3× base SI) rather than resetting annually. Three tiers: Standard (core inpatient, 1% SI as annual OPD wallet), Plus (maternity, newborn, higher SI), Elite (no room rent cap, comprehensive OPD, global cover for specified conditions). Health Care Plus: super top-up sitting above employer group cover, designed for IT-sector employees (no pre-policy medical for deductibles ≤₹3L). Group Health: digital platform enables real-time member add/remove for SME and corporate clients.

(1) Wallet accumulation mechanic: unused SI carries forward up to 3× base — creates a variable, growing liability across the portfolio. Unlike annual SI reset, the insurer must model the distribution of accumulated wallet balances across all policyholders and the probability of drawing them down. (2) Digital-first distribution: lower acquisition cost but concentration risk in a younger, tech-savvy demographic — limited senior or chronic disease experience data. (3) Real-time group member management: additions create instantaneous new risk exposure (adverse selection risk if members are added mid-year when ill). (4) Wellness discounts up to 20% via step-count: smaller reward ceiling than ABHICL but still requires reserving for the contingent discount. (5) New entrant data challenge: limited own-experience data across all product lines — high reliance on reinsurer benchmarks.

Digit's Wallet accumulation is the key differentiator to understand — it fundamentally changes the liability structure from a standard annual SI reset product and is likely to appear in any product design or reserving question about Digit.
Infinity Wallet Plan (Flagship)
Core Concept

Digit's flagship comprehensive health plan (2022 launch). Key differentiator: the 'Wallet' mechanic — SI does not reset annually but accumulates unused balance across years (up to 3× base SI). Covers inpatient hospitalisation, daycare, pre/post-hospitalisation (60/180 days), AYUSH. No room rent cap on higher variants. Modular add-ons (maternity, OPD, CI). Entry: 91 days–65 years; lifelong renewal. Exam angle: wallet accumulation is an alternative to NCB — instead of increasing SI, it stockpiles unused allowance. Actuarial challenge: liability from accumulated wallet balances must be reserved appropriately — it is a contingent liability that materialises if the policyholder makes a large future claim.
Wallet balance can be used for claims of any type — inpatient, OPD (if add-on selected), CI. This creates a flexible but harder-to-price liability structure than a fixed annual SI.

Digit is known for simplified product language and digital-first distribution — onboarding, claims, and renewals are fully app-based. Exam angle: digital distribution reduces acquisition cost but concentrates the policyholder base in the younger, tech-literate segment — lower average morbidity but also lower premium tenure (younger lives may lapse more readily).
Variants

content.bold: Infinity Wallet — Standard vs. Plus vs. Elite
content.text: Three benefit tiers. Standard: core inpatient; no OPD; 1% of SI as OPD wallet per year. Plus: adds maternity, newborn cover, higher SI options. Elite: no room rent cap, comprehensive OPD, global cover for specified conditions. Exam angle: tiered structuring allows risk segmentation within a single product filing. Digit uses pricing bands tied to SI and room tier to manage expected cost per tier without requiring separate IRDAI filings for each variant.
Health Care Plus & Group Plans
Supplementary & Group Products

Top-up / super top-up plan designed to sit above employer group cover or government PMJAY. Activates above chosen deductible (₹1L–₹10L options). No pre-policy medical for deductibles ≤₹3L. SI up to ₹1 crore. Key use case: young IT-sector employees extend group cover with personal super top-up. Exam angle: super top-up placed above group cover means the pricing model must account for 'layered coverage' — the probability of exceeding the deductible is conditional on the primary group cover having already been exhausted.

Offered to SMEs and large corporate groups. Guaranteed issue. Experience-rated for groups >200 lives. Digit's digital platform allows group HR departments to add/remove employees in real time — lowers administrative burden vs. traditional insurers. Exam angle: real-time member management creates a dynamic policy pool. Actuarial pricing must use expected average group composition, not a static census, when setting rates for SME groups.
Critical Illness & Wellness Plans
Fixed Benefit & Wellness Products

Lump-sum fixed benefit on diagnosis of listed CIs (up to 20 conditions). Does not require hospitalisation — trigger is confirmed diagnosis. SI options: ₹1L–₹1 crore. Entry up to age 65, renewable to 75. Exam angle: lump-sum CI plans are pure fixed-benefit products — the insurer's liability is fixed at the SI on occurrence. Key pricing risk is CI incidence by age band and condition; Indian CI incidence data is thinner than UK/US comparables — reinsurer tables are the primary benchmark.

Embedded in higher-tier plans: step-count tracking via Digit app generates annual premium discount (up to 20%). Preventive health checks covered (annual). Digit's telematics-inspired approach: self-reported health behaviour lowers premium at renewal. Exam angle: wellness discounts create a form of dynamic risk classification post-entry. Policyholders who demonstrate low-risk behaviour receive lower renewal premiums — improves persistency but may concentrate high-risk lives who do not engage with wellness in the standard pool, requiring re-pricing of the wellness vs. non-wellness segments.
Galaxy Health & Allied Insurance — Products & Features
Background & Strategic Context

Galaxy Health & Allied Insurance Company received its IRDAI licence in March 2024, becoming the 7th and newest SAHI in India. Founded by V. Jagannathan — former CMD and founder of Star Health — alongside TVS Group (Venu Srinivasan, Chairman). Initial capital: ₹200 crore. Within its first year, Galaxy built 14,000+ agents and insured over 2.5 lakh lives — aggressive distribution ramp-up driven by founder's industry relationships.

Galaxy Promise (flagship): three-variant structure — Signature (top tier, no co-pay, no disease sub-limits, any room for SI ≥₹25L, Premium Promise in-built), Elite (mid-tier, optional enhancements), Premier (entry tier, sub-limits apply, lowest premium). Galaxy Marvel (Neo & Prime): highly modular/customisable plan with 'Advance Access' — policyholder can borrow next year's SI once per 5-year term. Premium Promise: premium locked at entry age until first claim or age 55 — whichever comes first. Galaxy Privilege: senior plan (entry 60–75). Smart OPD Rider available across products.

Three claimed industry firsts: (1) Organ Donor Premium Waiver — if insured donates an organ, Galaxy waives 2 years of premium for the policyholder and covered family; (2) Digital ICU Cover — covers ICU-level care delivered remotely (post-COVID innovation); (3) Sleep Disorder Treatment cover. Each is actuarially novel with no credible Indian claims history — priced from reinsurer studies and conservative loads.

(1) New entrant with <2 years experience: almost zero own-data credibility — Z ≈ 0 in the credibility formula; pricing relies almost entirely on reinsurer benchmarks. (2) Premium Promise (locked premium): insurer absorbs all future morbidity deterioration and medical inflation until first claim or age 55. This is a guaranteed long-duration price commitment — the AA must price for expected cost at lock-in termination, not inception. (3) Advance Access (borrow next year's SI): creates a 2-year SI pool compressed into year 1 for one event — must model the probability distribution of borrowing and its impact on subsequent year's available SI. (4) Organ Donor Waiver: no Indian precedent; premium waiver is a 2-year income loss for the insurer — probability of voluntary organ donation is very low but not zero. (5) Founder effect: V. Jagannathan's track record (built Star Health from zero to ₹12,000 crore GWP) influences distribution ramp-up credibility and reinsurer appetite.

Galaxy is the newest SAHI and the best exam example of a start-up insurer facing the new-entrant data challenge. Its Premium Promise and Advance Access features are actuarially novel — expect them in questions on long-duration pricing and product design innovation.
Galaxy Promise (Flagship)
Three-Variant Structure

Most comprehensive variant. No co-payment, no disease-specific sub-limits for listed treatments. Room: any room type for SI ≥₹25L; Deluxe room for SI ₹5L–₹20L. Unlimited automatic restoration of SI. Premium Promise (in-built): premium locked at entry age until first claim or age 55 — whichever comes earlier. PED waiting period reduced from 36 to 25 months on 3-year policy term. Covers maternity, delivery, assisted reproduction (IVF), in-utero fetal surgery. Also covers hospice care, chronic severe refractory asthma, sleep disorders — near-unique benefits in Indian SAHI market. Exam angle: the Premium Promise lock-in is a powerful anti-lapse mechanism but creates a pricing risk — the insurer locks in the premium while morbidity rises with policyholder age. This is viable only if the pricing at entry already loads for expected morbidity escalation over the lock-in period (a form of level-premium pricing, similar to the long-term actuarial challenge in life insurance).

Broad coverage with optional consumables, room rent enhancement, and premium promise add-ons. SI up to ₹1 crore. Automatic restoration of SI included. Maternity and newborn covered. Does not include the full scope of Signature's niche benefits (sleep disorders, hospice, asthma). Exam angle: the Signature/Elite/Premier tiering allows Galaxy to price-segment by risk profile and willingness to pay — the same underwriting framework, differentiated by benefit scope. This is efficient from a product management standpoint but creates adverse selection within the range: buyers self-select into tiers based on anticipated health needs.

Basic comprehensive cover. SI up to ₹50L. No unlimited restoration. Disease-specific sub-limits apply (cataract, modern treatments). Room rent limits apply and are predefined. No consumables cover. Lowest premium in the Promise range. Designed for price-sensitive buyers who want basic hospitalisation protection. Exam angle: the Premier variant's sub-limits and room caps are the insurer's risk management tools at the low-price end. Without them, the low premium would create a loss-making product for high-utilisation claims. This is a textbook illustration of how product design (sub-limits, room caps) acts as a pricing control mechanism.
Galaxy Marvel (Customisable Plan)
Flexible Architecture

Galaxy's second flagship — highly modular and customisable. Key innovation: 'Advance Access' — once per 5-year policy term, the policyholder can borrow next year's SI for the current year's claim. Unlimited automatic restoration in-built. Premium Promise available (in-built on Prime, optional on Neo). Enhanced Multiplier benefit: cumulative bonus of up to 3× (Neo) or 10× (Prime) of SI regardless of claims — non-reducible, accruing every year. Gala Fit-Pro Active Care wellness programme: step-count and fitness engagement generates up to 20% discount on renewal premium. Covers PEDs from day 31 (select conditions). Exam angle: the 10× cumulative bonus on Prime variant means a policyholder's effective SI can grow to 11× base over time — purely from claim-free bonus accrual. Actuarially this creates an enormous long-tail contingent liability. The AA must reserve for the expected distribution of bonus-accumulated SI across the cohort, not just the base SI. This is arguably the most aggressive NCB mechanic in the Indian SAHI market.

Advance Access (borrow next year's SI) is a partial pre-funding of future coverage — it is essentially a 2-year SI pool compressed into year 1 for one specific event. Pricing requires modelling the conditional probability that a policyholder exhausts their annual SI in year 1 and would benefit from the advance — this is concentrated in high-cost claims (cancer, cardiac surgery).

Partner Protect benefit: newly married spouse (up to age 35) receives continuity credit for PED, specific disease, and initial waiting periods — no fresh waiting period at add-on. Exam angle: this is an anti-lapse and family acquisition tool — it makes it significantly more attractive for policyholders to add a spouse under the existing policy than to buy a separate new policy. From an adverse selection standpoint, newly married spouses (typically young, healthy) are a favourable addition to the pool.
Senior & Supplementary Plans
Galaxy Privilege (Senior Citizens)

Dedicated senior citizen plan. Entry: 60–75 years individual and floater (spouse can be added from age 50). SI: ₹5L–₹25L. Shorter waiting periods vs. standard plans. Optional covers include PED buyback, consumables, room rent enhancement. Exam angle: senior-specific SAHI products face an acute adverse selection problem — the pool is entirely composed of high-morbidity individuals. Galaxy's entry as a new SAHI in the senior segment faces a particular challenge: without its own senior claims experience, pricing must rely entirely on reinsurer age-specific morbidity tables. Any systemic underestimation of Indian senior morbidity (relative to global tables) will show up quickly as ICR deterioration in Years 2–4 as PED waiting periods expire and accumulated chronic conditions drive claims.
Top-up & OPD

Galaxy Top-up: enhances coverage above a deductible (₹50,000–₹10L); acts as a super top-up on aggregate annual claims. SI: ₹5L–₹50L. Smart Outpatient Rider: OPD rider covering doctor consultations, diagnostics, pharmacy, specialist visits — available as a standalone rider to be added to any base Galaxy plan. Cashless OPD facility at network providers. Exam angle: OPD riders on a new-entrant SAHI book face heightened moral hazard — Galaxy's new policyholders are an unknown risk pool and OPD utilisation elasticity is the highest of any health insurance benefit (OPD is high-frequency, low-severity). Without own utilisation data, Galaxy must use conservative OPD loading. A 20–30% moral hazard uplift on expected OPD claims is a reasonable starting assumption per reinsurer benchmarks.
Distinctive Product Features — Exam Angles

Three claimed industry firsts: (1) Organ Donor Premium Waiver — if the insured donates an organ, Galaxy waives 2 years of premium for the policyholder and their covered family. This is a genuine innovation with no pricing precedent in Indian data. The AA must estimate the probability of voluntary organ donation in the insured population (very low — <0.005% per year) and the expected 2-year premium forgone. Actuarial cost is minimal but reputational/brand value is high. (2) Digital ICU cover — covers costs of remote intensive care monitoring (telemedicine-based ICU oversight). Represents emerging technology in Indian hospitals; pricing basis is extremely thin. (3) 68-item consumables cover (vs. IRDAI's standard excluded list) — Galaxy covers 68 consumable items including admission charges and insurance processing charges, which most insurers exclude. Exam angle: covering non-payables increases average claim cost materially (industry estimates: 8–15% uplift on average inpatient claim). Galaxy must load for this in pricing and reserve for higher average severity than competitors who use the standard excluded list.
General Insurers — Health Products in India

content.bold: Role in the Indian health market
content.text: ~25 general insurers (GIs) also write health insurance alongside motor, property, and other lines. GIs typically dominate the group/corporate health segment; SAHIs lead in retail individual products. Key GI health writers: HDFC Ergo, ICICI Lombard, New India Assurance (PSU), Bajaj Allianz. GIs cannot be exclusively health-focused — health is one product line among many, which affects data depth and product innovation relative to SAHIs.

HDFC Ergo: Optima Secure (SI doubles on Day 1 via 'Secure Benefit'; 'Plus Benefit' adds 25% SI per claim-free year, non-reducible), Optima Restore (SI reinstated once per year; Multiplier doubles SI after 2 claim-free years). ICICI Lombard: Health AdvantEdge (multi-year policy option 1/2/3 years — rare in Indian market; AdvantEdge Restore), Complete Health Insurance (legacy 4-variant suite). New India Assurance: New India Mediclaim (oldest large-scale health product in India; PSU, government employee base, CGHS supplement), Government Scheme Administrator for state and central schemes. Bajaj Allianz: Health Guard (NCB up to 100% of SI over 10 years), Global Personal Guard (SI up to USD 1 million, targets HNI/CXO), Criti Care (return-of-premium CI rider — 50% premium back after 10 claim-free years).

(1) GI vs. SAHI distinction: GIs cannot concentrate data and innovation exclusively on health — SAHIs have structural advantage in claims data depth and product design. (2) HDFC Ergo Secure Benefit (SI doubles Day 1): creates an immediate doubling of exposure at inception — the insurer must hold capital and reserves for 2× SI from policy start, even though premium was set at 1× SI level. (3) Multi-year policies (ICICI Lombard): premium is fixed at inception for 2–3 years — insurer absorbs medical inflation risk over the term. Multi-year lapse rate is also lower (policyholder is committed for 2–3 years). (4) PSU insurer (New India): cross-subsidised by government backing; large legacy in-force blocks; CGHS-supplementing policyholder base creates a different morbidity profile. (5) Return-of-premium CI (Bajaj Criti Care): dual liability — must reserve for both CI claim and RoP payout; natural partial hedge (high CI incidence lowers RoP probability, and vice versa).

The GI vs. SAHI structural distinction is a recurring SA1 theme — be precise on what SAHIs can and cannot write (health, PA, travel only) and why exclusive health focus gives SAHIs a data and innovation advantage over GIs.
HDFC Ergo
Flagship Plans

HDFC Ergo's premium flagship health plan. Key feature: 'Secure Benefit' — SI doubles on Day 1 at no extra cost (e.g., buy ₹5L, get ₹10L effective cover immediately). 'Plus Benefit': SI increases by 25% of base SI each claim-free year (non-reducible even after a claim). No room rent cap. Covers 586+ daycare procedures, AYUSH, domiciliary, organ donor. Entry: 91 days–65 years; lifelong renewal. Exam angle: the Day-1 doubling of SI is a powerful anti-lapse mechanic — the policyholder is immediately at risk of losing their ₹10L cover if they switch insurer (portability resets SI to paid base). Actuarially, the doubling creates an immediate contingent liability from Day 1 that must be reserved for. The pricing must assume the doubled SI is the exposure, not just the nominal bought SI.

HDFC Ergo's mid-market plan with the 'Restore' benefit: SI automatically reinstated once per policy year after exhaustion, at no extra charge. 'Multiplier Benefit': SI doubles after 2 consecutive claim-free years (and remains at 2× even after subsequent claims). Entry: 91 days–65 years. Exam angle: 'Restore' + 'Multiplier' combination creates compounding coverage accretion. The insurer's expected liability grows each year a policyholder does not claim — the maximum effective SI reaches 2× base. Pricing must include a term-structure projection of expected effective SI across the cohort.
Niche & Supplementary Plans

HDFC Ergo super top-up with deductible options ₹1L–₹15L. SI up to ₹50L above deductible. No medical for entry up to age 45 (deductible ≥₹3L). Designed to layer above employer group cover or Optima base plan. Exam angle: HDFC Ergo bundles the top-up with a recommendation to hold Optima Restore as the base — cross-selling strategy that creates a two-layer product portfolio per customer. From an actuarial standpoint, the base and top-up are correlated — a policy year where the base SI is exhausted (triggering Restore) is the same year the top-up activates. Pricing must avoid treating them as independent.

Lump-sum CI plan covering 15 critical illnesses (includes cancer, AMI, stroke, renal failure, major organ transplant, CABG). Payout is 100% of SI on first diagnosis (survival period: 30 days post-diagnosis). SI: ₹5L–₹1 crore. Entry: 18–65 years. Exam angle: the 30-day survival period is an underwriting control — it eliminates immediate post-diagnosis claims where prognosis was already poor at inception. Pricing: CI incidence × survival probability (30-day) × SI. Indian CI incidence data requires reinsurer benchmarking; HDFC Ergo uses Munich Re and Swiss Re tables with India-specific loading.
ICICI Lombard
Flagship Plans

ICICI Lombard's flagship post-2022 comprehensive plan. Multi-year policy option (1, 2, or 3 year terms) — rare in Indian health market. Room: no cap on private room for SI ≥₹5L. 'AdvantEdge Restore': SI reinstated once per year after full exhaustion. Covers OPD as an optional add-on (network-only). Global cover for specified conditions (cancer, cardiac surgery) as premium add-on. Entry: 91 days–65 years; lifelong renewal. Exam angle: multi-year policy pricing is an important actuarial challenge. Premium must be set for 2–3 year forward exposure — the insurer cannot revise pricing at year 2 or 3 if claims experience is adverse. This requires a longer pricing horizon with medical inflation and morbidity trend projections explicitly modelled.

Predecessor flagship plan (large in-force block). Four variants: Silver, Gold, Platinum, Diamond — differentiated by room entitlement and benefit scope. Disease-specific sub-limits apply on Silver/Gold; removed on Platinum/Diamond. Lifelong renewal guaranteed. Exam angle: maintaining a legacy in-force block alongside a newer flagship creates a 'closed book' vs. 'open book' dynamic. The legacy block (Silver/Gold) will age — morbidity worsens over time — and ICICI Lombard cannot adjust benefits but can reprice at renewal within IRDAI limits. AA must monitor the ICR trajectory of the legacy block separately from the new flagship.
Supplementary & Group

Market leader in large-group employer health insurance (>1000 lives). Experience-rated on individual group basis. TPA network: InHealth (in-house TPA — ICICI Lombard is one of the few GI companies with a fully owned TPA). In-house TPA reduces information asymmetry in claims management — the insurer controls both underwriting and claims adjudication data. Exam angle: vertical integration via in-house TPA provides a data advantage — claims experience flows directly to the pricing team without TPA intermediation delays. This is an important structural difference from SAHIs that rely on third-party TPAs.
New India Assurance
Public Sector Flagship

One of India's oldest and largest health insurance products by in-force count. Government-owned (PSU) insurer — New India Assurance is the largest non-life insurer in India by GWP. Standard indemnity plan: inpatient, 1–2% SI as room rent cap per day, disease-specific sub-limits. SI: ₹1L–₹15L (extended to ₹25L for Mediclaim Plus). Lifelong renewal. Exam angle: PSU health portfolios are characterised by (1) large, ageing in-force blocks with high morbidity; (2) limited ability to raise premiums sharply due to political sensitivity; (3) no wellness or digital engagement initiatives. ICRs on legacy PSU mediclaim books often exceed 100% — the business is typically cross-subsidised by other lines. AA must flag this when reserving.

Family floater version — shared SI for family (max 2 adults + 2 children). Popular with government employees who supplement CGHS cover. Entry: up to 65 years. Exam angle: PSU insurer floater books illustrate the intersection of government scheme (CGHS) and private cover — CGHS-covered employees who buy a floater supplement are typically the higher-income, higher-health-utilisation segment. This positive selection (from the insurer's perspective — wealthier, higher health-literate buyers) is counterbalanced by the fact that they are also more aware of their entitlements and more likely to claim fully.
Government Scheme Administration

New India Assurance is one of the primary implementing insurers for state government health schemes (e.g., administering components of PMJAY at state level, various state government employee health schemes). GWP from government schemes constitutes a significant portion of New India's health book. Exam angle: government scheme administration is not standard commercial insurance — the premium is set by government negotiation (not market pricing), the pool is defined (government employees or BPL families), and the ICR obligation may be defined in the scheme contract. The AA's role in a government scheme context differs materially from commercial product pricing — the focus shifts to reserving and scheme management rather than rate adequacy.
Bajaj Allianz
Core Health Products

Bajaj Allianz's primary comprehensive health plan. Variants: Silver (basic) and Gold (enhanced). Gold variant: no room rent cap, organ donor expenses, AYUSH, domiciliary. NCB: 10% SI increase per claim-free year (up to 100% additional SI — 10 years of claim-free experience doubles SI). Optional add-ons: maternity, critical illness cover, hospital cash. Entry: 18–65 years; lifelong renewal. Exam angle: NCB up to 100% of SI creates a material long-tail liability. After 10 claim-free years, SI has doubled — the insurer must reserve for this doubled exposure throughout the accumulation period, not just when the NCB is reached. The pricing must load for the expected NCB accrual across the expected claims-free duration of the portfolio.
