Premium product offering worldwide coverage for inpatient hospitalisation — targeted at high net worth individuals (HNI), frequent international travellers, and CXOs. No room restriction globally. Covers international medical evacuation, repatriation. SI up to USD 1 million. Premium: ₹30,000–₹1.5 lakh per annum depending on age and SI. Exam angle: global cover pricing requires claim cost benchmarking in international markets — Indian actuaries must use global hospital cost indices (US, UK, Singapore hospital tariff data) rather than Indian rates. Currency risk: claims are incurred in foreign currency but premium is paid in INR — the insurer must hedge or load for INR depreciation over the policy year.
Top-up & Critical Illness

Super top-up plan; aggregate annual deductible basis. Deductible: ₹2L–₹20L. SI above deductible: ₹10L–₹1 crore. No pre-policy medical for age <55 years. Annual premium ₹2,500–₹12,000 for typical buyer — highly price-competitive vs. comprehensive plans. Exam angle: super top-up pricing requires the insurer to model the tail of the loss distribution — specifically the probability of aggregate annual claims exceeding each deductible level by age. Bajaj Allianz uses own-experience data plus reinsurer studies for this, given the longer product history vs. newer SAHIs.

Lump-sum CI plan. Covers up to 10 critical illness categories (cancer, cardiac, neurological, organ failure). SI: ₹1L–₹50L. Entry: 18–65 years. Unique: optional CI 'return of premium' rider — if no claim in 10 years, 50% of cumulative premium refunded. Exam angle: return of premium (RoP) rider transforms the product into a savings-linked CI plan. Actuarially, the insurer must reserve for both the CI claim probability AND the RoP payout probability. If CI incidence is lower than priced, the RoP liability increases — there is a natural hedge, but only partial. Pricing must solve for the premium that is positive expected value for the insurer under both the CI-claim and no-claim scenarios.
Narayana Health Insurance (NHIL) — Products & Features
Background & Strategic Context

Narayana Health Insurance Limited (NHIL), branded as 'Narayana One Health', is the 6th SAHI licensed in India. IRDAI granted its Certificate of Registration in December 2023 — the first new SAHI licence after a 5-year hiatus. NHIL is a 100% subsidiary of Narayana Hrudayalaya Limited (NHL), one of India's largest hospital chains founded by Dr. Devi Shetty. This makes NHIL structurally unique among all SAHIs: it is India's first hospital-owned health insurer — vertically integrated with a major provider network.

Aditi (July 2024, first product): mass-market family floater of 4 for ₹10,000/year, SI up to ₹1 crore for surgery, network-restricted to Narayana hospitals. Aditi+ and Aditi Prime (2025): progressive network expansions from captive Narayana network → 2,400+ hospitals pan-India. Arya (January 2025): comprehensive retail plan SI ₹25L–₹1 crore, open network including Fortis, Gleneagles, Sparsh, with ZAP (Zero Additional Payment) protection at Narayana hospitals. Phased geographic rollout: began Karnataka (Mysuru, Bengaluru), expanded to Guwahati, Jaipur, Jamshedpur by early 2026.

content.bold: Vertical integration — the defining structural feature
content.text: Hospital-owned insurer removes the standard adversarial insurer-hospital dynamic. NHIL prices off its own DRG-level cost data from Narayana hospitals — a structural pricing advantage unavailable to any other SAHI. ZAP Protection (Arya plan) guarantees zero out-of-pocket deduction at Narayana hospitals — only possible because NHIL controls hospital billing. Gala Fit wellness integration delivers in-hospital OPD discounts — again only deliverable by a hospital-owned insurer.

(1) Vertical integration conflict of interest: NHIL may direct claims to Narayana hospitals even when alternatives are clinically equivalent but cheaper — parent earns revenue from claims. IRDAI and the AA must monitor that network restriction does not harm policyholder interests. (2) Pricing advantage: DRG-level own-cost data means NHIL does not need to estimate hospital tariffs from reinsurer benchmarks for network claims — significant underwriting information advantage. (3) New entrant data challenge: <3 years claims experience. Credibility Z ≈ 0; OPD (Arya) priced entirely from benchmarks; non-network claims (Aditi Prime) lack the vertical integration data advantage. (4) 'Missing middle' buyer segment (Aditi): uninsured population below formal SAHI market but above PMJAY eligibility — their health utilisation behaviour under insurance is essentially unknown. (5) Network expansion trajectory (Aditi → Aditi+ → Aditi Prime): each step increases addressable market but raises claims cost exposure — AA must re-price at each network expansion.

NHIL is the primary SA1 exam example of a hospital-owned insurer. Any question on vertical integration, conflict of interest in health insurance, or the 'missing middle' segment will reference NHIL's structure. Know the Aditi → Aditi Prime evolution and the ZAP Protection mechanic in detail.
Aditi Plan Series (Mass-Market Flagship)
Aditi — Core Design Philosophy

NHIL's first product. Positioned as the private-sector analogue to Ayushman Bharat PMJAY — targeting the 'missing middle' segment that falls outside government schemes but finds standard SAHI products unaffordable. Premium: ₹10,000/year for a family floater of 4 (2 adults + 2 children). SI: up to ₹1 crore for surgeries; ₹5 lakh for medical management. Coverage primarily restricted to Narayana Health hospital network. No long waiting periods — designed to cover emergencies and surgical events from near-inception. Pre-policy medical examination required at entry. Exam angle: ₹10,000/year for ₹1 crore SI is viable only through vertical integration. Coverage is network-restricted — Narayana hospitals, as the parent group, have pre-negotiated their own rates with the insurer, eliminating the information asymmetry and billing inflation typical of third-party hospital relationships. The insurer effectively prices off its own cost data (cost per DRG per Narayana hospital), not external tariff estimates. This is a structural pricing advantage unavailable to any other SAHI.

Daily deductible of ₹2,000/day for non-surgical medical management admissions. This controls moral hazard for the lower ₹5L medical bucket — patients bear meaningful out-of-pocket cost for routine inpatient stays, reducing unnecessary admissions.

Limited outside-network coverage — emergency-only. Exam angle: network restriction is both a cost control tool and an adverse selection control. Buyers who want true open-network access must choose a different SAHI. Aditi's buyer base is self-selected for willingness to use Narayana hospitals — reducing the moral hazard of shopping for premium providers at insurer expense.

Aditi+ extended coverage to all 21+ Narayana Health hospitals plus a curated partner network. Aditi Prime (launched 2025): cashless access at 2,400+ hospitals pan-India — a major network expansion that transitions NHIL from a captive-network insurer toward an open-network model. Aditi Prime covers up to ₹1 crore for both surgical and non-surgical treatment within the same limit (removing the ₹5L medical management sub-limit of the original Aditi). Exam angle: the evolution from Aditi → Aditi+ → Aditi Prime illustrates the growth trajectory of a new SAHI — beginning with tight network control to manage cost and claims experience, then progressively opening the network as the book matures and data accumulates. Each step expands the addressable market but increases claim cost exposure. The AA must re-price at each network expansion stage.
Arya Plan (Comprehensive Retail)
Arya — Broader Retail Offering

NHIL's second product — a comprehensive retail plan targeting the broader individual and family market beyond the mass-market Aditi segment. SI: ₹25L–₹1 crore. Two plan variants: different network coverage scopes. Network access: Narayana Health hospitals nationwide plus a curated Verified Provider Network (includes Fortis, Gleneagles BGS, Sparsh, Narayana Nethralaya, CloudNine, Sakra World in Bengaluru). OPD covered (consultations, diagnostics, pharmacy). GST exemption benefit fully passed to policyholders — a regulatory arbitrage advantage NHIL highlighted at launch (health insurance premiums are GST-exempt, but NHIL structured Arya to make this explicit vs. competitors). ZAP (Zero Additional Payment) Protection: for eligible claims at Narayana Health hospitals, zero out-of-pocket deduction — all eligible claim costs fully covered. Exam angle: ZAP Protection is the hospital-insurer integration advantage made explicit as a product benefit. The insurer can offer this only because it controls the hospital's billing — it knows exactly what will be charged and can guarantee no surprise deductions. No standalone SAHI can replicate this without a similar hospital ownership structure.

Gala Fit wellness integration: OPD discount of 10% at Narayana network hospitals (Value Added Service). Exam angle: in-hospital wellness discounts are only deliverable by a hospital-owned insurer — competitor SAHIs must use third-party wellness apps (Niva Bupa's step-count app, ABHICL's HealthReturns) with indirect reward mechanisms.
Strategic & Actuarial Exam Angles
Vertical Integration — Actuarial Implications

NHIL's vertical integration with Narayana Hrudayalaya creates unique actuarial dynamics not present in any other SAHI. (1) Pricing data advantage: the insurer has direct access to Narayana hospitals' cost-per-procedure data — DRG-level cost intelligence that standalone SAHIs must estimate from reinsurer benchmarks or limited own experience. (2) Conflict of interest: the AA must certify pricing is 'fair' under IRDAI regulations. If the insurer systematically directs claims to Narayana hospitals (which benefits the parent group), the claims cost structure may differ from open-market comparisons. IRDAI oversight must ensure this does not harm policyholder value. (3) Reserving: the SI structure of Aditi (₹1 crore surgical / ₹5L medical with daily deductible) requires separate loss distributions for surgical and medical management admissions — a two-bucket reserving model. Exam angle: any SA1 question on a hospital-owned insurer will test awareness of the conflict of interest, the pricing advantage from cost data, and the regulatory framework the AA operates within.

content.bold: New entrant data challenge — NHIL specific
content.text: NHIL has fewer than 3 years of claims experience. Specific challenges: (1) no own-experience CI or OPD data — Arya's OPD benefit must be priced entirely from reinsurer and industry benchmarks; (2) surgical cost data is available from the Narayana hospital group for network claims, but non-network claims (Aditi Prime) lack this advantage; (3) the 'missing middle' buyer segment targeted by Aditi has very limited prior insurance experience — their morbidity and claims behaviour is less well-documented than the insured middle-class segment that all other SAHIs have accumulated data on. Exam angle: NHIL's actuarial challenge is a combination of the standard new-entrant data problem AND the novel challenge of pricing for a buyer segment (uninsured missing middle) whose health utilisation behaviour under insurance is essentially unknown. The credibility weighting formula Z = n/(n+k) gives a Z near zero for NHIL — almost all pricing weight falls on external benchmarks, with conservative uncertainty loadings required.
